# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> **Differentiate between additive and subtractive color mixing. Give one example device for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Additive Mixing | Subtractive Mixing |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Principle | Combines emitted **light** components | Combines **pigment/ink** that absorb light |
| Primary Colors | **Red, Green, Blue (RGB)** | **Cyan, Magenta, Yellow (CMY)** |
| Result of combining all primaries | **White** | **Black** (theoretical) |
| Example Device | LCD/OLED monitor, VR HMD, projector | Inkjet printer, offset printing |

Additive mixing starts from black (no light) and adds wavelengths; subtractive starts from white (full reflected light) and removes wavelengths via absorption. **(3 marks for complete answer with device example.)**

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> **What is the HSV color model? Why is it preferred over RGB for user-driven color selection in graphics applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value)** model represents color using three intuitive components:

- **Hue (H):** The pure color type, represented as an angle from **0° to 360°**.
- **Saturation (S):** The intensity or purity of the color, from **0 (gray)** to **1 (vivid)**.
- **Value (V):** The brightness, from **0 (black)** to **1 (full brightness)**.

**Why preferred over RGB for user color picking:**

1. **Decoupled components** — Users can adjust brightness without affecting hue, which is impossible in RGB where changing R/G/B alters all three perceptual attributes.
2. **Single-axis hue selection** — A hue ring/slider is natural for users; RGB requires balancing three sliders simultaneously.
3. **Direct mapping to artistic intent** — "Darker red" = lower V; "Pinker red" = shift H toward magenta; matches how humans describe colors verbally.

**(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for component explanation, 1 mark for preference justification.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 2, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the RGB, CMY, and HSV color models with diagrams. Discuss where each is most suitably used in AR/VR applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the mathematical conversion from RGB to HSV color model. Apply your derivation to convert the RGB color (R=0.3, G=0.7, B=0.5) to HSV. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**RGB Model (2 marks):**
- Additive color model based on the tristimulus theory of human vision.
- Each color is a triplet (R, G, B) with values in [0, 1] (or [0, 255]).
- Modeled as a **cube** with black at origin (0,0,0) and white at (1,1,1).
- Used in: VR HMDs (Oculus Quest, HTC Vive), AR glasses, monitors, digital cameras.

**CMY Model (2 marks):**
- Subtractive model used in printing.
- $C = 1 - R$, $M = 1 - G$, $Y = 1 - B$.
- Cyan absorbs red, magenta absorbs green, yellow absorbs blue.
- Real-world use: 3D-printed textures, physical color swatches overlaid on virtual objects in AR, calibration cards.
- Extension **CMYK** adds black (K) ink to fix muddy dark tones.

**HSV Model (2 marks):**
- Cylindrical model: Hue (angle 0-360°), Saturation (radial 0-1), Value (vertical 0-1).
- Aligns with human perception and verbal color description.
- Used in: Color pickers in Unity/Unreal (VR development environments), shader parameters, photo-editing tools for VR content creation.

**AR/VR Context (1 mark):** RGB dominates real-time rendering pipelines; CMY is used for printed fiducial markers and physical-virtual color matching; HSV is used in user interfaces and shader authoring.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The conversion follows six steps as derived in Section 3.1.

**Step 1:** Compute max and min of R, G, B.
$$\max = \max(R, G, B), \quad \min = \min(R, G, B)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the chroma delta.
$$\Delta = \max - \min$$

**Step 3:** Compute V (Value/Brightness).
$$V = \max$$

**Step 4:** Compute S (Saturation).
$$S = \frac{\Delta}{\max} \quad \text{when } \max \neq 0, \text{ else } 0$$

**Step 5:** Compute H (Hue) using the piecewise formula.
$$H = 60 \cdot \frac{G - B}{\Delta} \mod 360 \quad \text{when } \max = R$$
$$H = 60 \cdot \left(2 + \frac{B - R}{\Delta}\right) \quad \text{when } \max = G$$
$$H = 60 \cdot \left(4 + \frac{R - G}{\Delta}\right) \quad \text{when } \max = B$$
$$H = 0 \quad \text{when } \Delta = 0$$

**Step 6:** Normalize H to [0°, 360°).

**Numerical Application (2 marks):**

Given $R = 0.3, G = 0.7, B = 0.5$:

**Step 1:** $\max = 0.7$ (G is dominant), $\min = 0.3$.

**Step 2:** $\Delta = 0.7 - 0.3 = 0.4$.

**Step 3:** $V = 0.7$.

**Step 4:** $S = 0.4 / 0.7 = 0.5714$.

**Step 5:** Since $\max = G$, we use the second case:
$$H = 60 \cdot \left(2 + \frac{B - R}{\Delta}\right) = 60 \cdot \left(2 + \frac{0.5 - 0.3}{0.4}\right)$$
$$H = 60 \cdot (2 + 0.5) = 60 \cdot 2.5 = 150^\circ$$

**Final Answer:** HSV = **(150°, 0.5714, 0.7)** — a green-cyan hue (150° lies between green at 120° and cyan at 180°), medium-bright, with moderate saturation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 2, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the YIQ and YCbCr color models. Why are luminance-chrominance models preferred over RGB for video transmission in VR applications? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the RGB to YIQ conversion matrix. Using your result, convert the color (R=200, G=100, B=50) (8-bit values) to YIQ. Show all steps. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**YIQ Model (2.5 marks):**
- Developed for **NTSC** color television broadcasting in the USA.
- Separates the image into:
  - **Y (Luminance):** Perceived brightness, using weights matching the photopic luminosity function.
  - **I (In-phase):** Orange-cyan chrominance axis.
  - **Q (Quadrature):** Magenta-green chrominance axis.
- I and Q can be **bandwidth-limited** (e.g., I at 1.3 MHz, Q at 0.4 MHz) without visible quality loss because human eyes are less sensitive to fine chroma detail.

**YCbCr Model (2.5 marks):**
- Digital video standard (ITU-R BT.601, BT.709, BT.2020).
- Y is luma; Cb and Cr are blue-difference and red-difference chroma.
- Used in: JPEG, MPEG, H.264, HEVC — **all codecs used in 360° VR video streaming**.
- Allows **chroma subsampling** (4:2:0, 4:2:2) to reduce bandwidth by 50% with minimal perceptual impact.

**Why Preferred for VR Video (2 marks):**
1. **Backward compatibility** — Y channel alone reproduces a grayscale image; old monochrome receivers could display it.
2. **Bandwidth efficiency** — Human visual system has lower spatial acuity for color than for brightness; chroma can be compressed more aggressively.
3. **Reduced motion artifacts in VR** — Lower chroma bandwidth reduces "color bleeding" artifacts that are especially noticeable in head-tracked stereoscopic VR, where mismatches between left/right eye chroma cause discomfort.
4. **Standardization** — YCbCr is the universal interchange format between camera ISPs and VR display drivers.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Derivation of the Matrix (3 marks):**

The YIQ transform is a linear mapping from RGB. The Y component is constructed as a weighted sum of R, G, B to approximate perceived luminance (photopic luminosity):

$$Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$$

The weights are derived empirically from the CIE photopic luminosity function $V(\lambda)$, integrated against the RGB phosphor emission spectra of the NTSC standard.

The I and Q channels are constructed as **orthogonal** axes in the chrominance plane, rotated approximately 33° from the B-Y and R-Y axes:

$$I = 0.596R - 0.275G - 0.321B$$
$$Q = 0.212R - 0.523G + 0.311B$$

Written in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} Y \\ I \\ Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.299 & 0.587 & 0.114 \\ 0.596 & -0.275 & -0.321 \\ 0.212 & -0.523 & 0.311 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix}$$

**Numerical Application (4 marks):**

Given R = 200, G = 100, B = 50 (all out of 255).

**Step 1:** Apply the matrix directly (the formula is valid for any R, G, B scale; the units just scale accordingly).

$$Y = 0.299(200) + 0.587(100) + 0.114(50)$$
$$Y = 59.8 + 58.7 + 5.7 = 124.2$$

**Step 2:** Compute I.
$$I = 0.596(200) - 0.275(100) - 0.321(50)$$
$$I = 119.2 - 27.5 - 16.05 = 75.65$$

**Step 3:** Compute Q.
$$Q = 0.212(200) - 0.523(100) + 0.311(50)$$
$$Q = 42.4 - 52.3 + 15.55 = 5.65$$

**Final Answer:** YIQ = **(124.2, 75.65, 5.65)**

**Interpretation:** The Y value of 124.2 (out of 255 max) indicates a moderately bright color. The high I value (75.65) indicates strong orange-cyan chrominance — consistent with the input being a saturated orange. The low Q value (5.65) indicates minimal magenta-green axis content, again consistent with orange.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students frequently write the wrong sign convention in I and Q — the negative coefficients for G and B in the I channel are easily transposed.
> - Forgetting that YIQ can be applied at the 0-255 scale directly (no normalization needed) costs 1 mark.
> - Not providing an **interpretation** of the result is a common 1-mark deduction. KTU examiners award marks for *understanding*, not just arithmetic.
> - In RGB $\to$ HSV, students often use the wrong case of the H formula. **Always identify max first, then select the corresponding case.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Color Theory**

- **Visible spectrum wavelength range:** 380 nm (violet) to 750 nm (red).
- **Tristimulus theory:** Three cone types (L, M, S) → all colors are 3D combinations.
- **Additive (RGB):** Light-emitting displays; primaries are R, G, B; sum to white.
- **Subtractive (CMY):** Pigments/inks; primaries are C, M, Y; sum to black.
- **HSV components:** Hue ∈ [0°, 360°), Saturation ∈ [0, 1], Value ∈ [0, 1].
- **HSV H formula** is piecewise; selection of case depends on which RGB channel is maximum.
- **YIQ luma formula:** $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ — memorize the coefficients.
- **YCbCr Cb, Cr** require the **+128 offset** for 8-bit storage.
- **CIE XYZ** is the perceptually-anchored, device-independent color space.
- **CIE xy chromaticity:** $x = X / (X+Y+Z)$, $y = Y / (X+Y+Z)$.
- **Gamma correction:** $V_{out} = V_{in}^{1/\gamma}$ with $\gamma \approx 2.2$ for sRGB.
- **AR/VR relevance:** Color matching between virtual and real is the hardest perceptual problem in optical see-through AR; YCbCr is the universal video transport format in VR streaming.
- **High-yield conversions for exams:** RGB $\leftrightarrow$ HSV, RGB $\leftrightarrow$ YIQ, RGB $\leftrightarrow$ YCbCr, RGB $\leftrightarrow$ CMY.
- **Chroma subsampling** (4:2:0) reduces VR video bandwidth by 50% — important for wireless VR streaming.
- **HDR VR** uses 10-bit+ per channel and Rec. 2020 gamut.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion from 2D to 3D
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Conversion from 2D to 3D

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**2D-to-3D Conversion** in the context of Augmented and Virtual Reality refers to the computational process of reconstructing a three-dimensional spatial representation (depth, geometry, and structure) from one or more two-dimensional image inputs. The result is a depth-augmented scene that can be rendered stereoscopically, allowing immersive VR/AR visualization on Head-Mounted Displays (HMDs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Conversion from 2D to 3D is the process of generating a depth map (Z-buffer equivalent) for each pixel of a 2D image, such that when paired with disparity-based rendering, the human visual system perceives volumetric depth.*

Mathematically, given a 2D image $I(x, y)$ defined over a 2D pixel grid, the goal is to compute the corresponding depth map $D(x, y)$ (or disparity map) such that:

$$I_{3D}(x, y, z) = f(I_{2D}(x, y), D(x, y))$$

where $z$ represents the recovered depth dimension.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pop-Up Book" Mental Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine you are looking at a flat printed photograph of a mountain. Your brain already *guesses* that the mountain is far away, the trees in the foreground are closer, and the bird flying is somewhere in between. **2D-to-3D conversion is the engineering process of teaching a computer to make those same intelligent guesses, but mathematically, so that it can build a digital "pop-up book" version of the photo with measurable depth at every pixel.**

Think of it like folding origami: a 2D sheet of paper is the image, and through deliberate creasing (depth cues), it becomes a 3D crane. The computer, given only a 2D picture, must figure out *where* and *how much* to "fold" each pixel.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in AR/VR

- **VR Content Creation:** Most existing media is 2D (films, photos, CCTV). Conversion enables legacy 2D content to be replayed immersively in VR.
- **AR Depth Sensing Compensation:** When AR devices lack LiDAR, depth hallucination from 2D RGB frames maintains realistic occlusion.
- **Telepresence & 3D Video Conferencing:** 2D camera feeds are converted to 3D avatars in real time.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Inter-Pupillary Distance (IPD)** | **63–65 mm** | Human binocular baseline |
| **Human Depth Field of View** | **~120°** horizontal | Stereo fusion limit |
| **Stereo Acuity Threshold** | **~10 arc-seconds** | Minimum detectable disparity |
| **Standard Render Resolution** | **1080p per eye @ 90 Hz** | VR HMD baseline |
| **Depth Map Bit Depth** | **8-bit (256 levels)** / 16-bit | Quantization precision |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Inter-Pupillary Distance (IPD = 65 mm)** is the cornerstone constant — it sets the *physical baseline* between two virtual cameras used to synthesize stereo pairs from a single 2D image.

## 1.5 Classification of 2D-to-3D Conversion Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Triad (must memorize):**
> 1. **Manual Conversion** — Human artist annotates depth frame-by-frame.
> 2. **Semi-Automatic Conversion** — User provides sparse depth hints; algorithm propagates.
> 3. **Automatic Conversion** — Machine learning / heuristic algorithms extract depth autonomously.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How 2D-to-3D Works

The conversion pipeline is grounded in three pillars:

### Pillar 1: Depth Cues (Monocular — from a single image)
The human brain uses these heuristics; the algorithm must replicate them:

- **Occlusion** — Object A hides B → A is closer.
- **Relative Size** — Larger of two known objects is closer.
- **Texture Gradient** — Denser texture = farther away.
- **Linear Perspective** — Converging lines imply depth.
- **Atmospheric Perspective** — Far objects appear hazier/bluer.
- **Defocus Blur** — Out-of-focus regions lie outside the depth of field.
- **Motion Parallax** (video) — Closer pixels move faster across frames.

### Pillar 2: Binocular Disparity (from stereo pair)
The horizontal pixel shift between the left and right eye views is called **disparity** $\Delta x$. It is *inversely proportional* to depth.

### Pillar 3: Camera Geometry (Epipolar Projection)
Two cameras with parallel optical axes and a baseline $b$ project a 3D point $P(X, Y, Z)$ to image points $P_L(x_L, y)$ and $P_R(x_R, y)$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **exam-critical formulas**. Master each — derivations are given in Section 3.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{d}$ | Depth from disparity (stereo) | meters (m) |
| F2 | $d = x_L - x_R$ | Disparity definition | pixels |
| F3 | $f = \dfrac{h}{2 \tan(\theta/2)}$ | Focal length from HFOV | mm |
| F4 | $d = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{Z}$ | Rearranged disparity form | pixels |
| F5 | $X = \dfrac{(x - c_x) \cdot Z}{f}$ | 3D X-coordinate reprojection | m |
| F6 | $Y = \dfrac{(y - c_y) \cdot Z}{f}$ | 3D Y-coordinate reprojection | m |
| F7 | $M = \dfrac{Z_{near}}{Z_{far}} \cdot 2^n - 1$ | Depth value quantization (n-bit) | integer |
| F8 | $Z_{norm} = \dfrac{Z - Z_{near}}{Z_{far} - Z_{near}}$ | Normalized depth (0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| F9 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{d \cdot Z}{f \cdot v}$ | Motion parallax shift velocity | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variables used in the table:**
> - $Z$ = depth (distance from camera)
> - $f$ = focal length
> - $b$ = baseline (inter-camera distance ≈ IPD = 65 mm for human eyes)
> - $d$ = disparity (in pixels)
> - $h$ = image sensor height
> - $\theta$ = horizontal field of view
> - $c_x, c_y$ = principal point (image center)
> - $X, Y, Z$ = 3D world coordinates
> - $v$ = camera translation velocity
> - $n$ = bit depth of depth map

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of 2D-to-3D |
|---|---|
| **Cinematic VR** | Converting 2D films for Meta Quest / Apple Vision Pro playback |
| **Medical Imaging** | Reconstructing 3D anatomy from 2D CT/MRI slices |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Depth hallucination when stereo cameras fail |
| **Robotics** | Single-camera navigation via monocular depth estimation (MiDaS, DPT) |
| **Cultural Heritage** | Building 3D museum models from archival photographs |
| **AR Object Insertion** | Correct occlusion of virtual objects behind real 2D-detected surfaces |

## 2.4 Algorithmic Categories of Conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** The following four families appear repeatedly in board questions.

### 2.4.1 Depth Map Based Methods
A 2D image $I(x, y)$ is paired with a computed depth map $D(x, y)$. The 3D reconstruction is then performed via **Depth-Image-Based Rendering (DIBR)**.

### 2.4.2 Structure from Motion (SfM)
Multi-frame analysis tracks feature correspondences across video frames and solves for both camera motion and 3D structure via **bundle adjustment**.

### 2.4.3 Photogrammetry (Multi-View Stereo — MVS)
Uses many overlapping photographs from different angles. Performs dense correspondence matching to produce a point cloud.

### 2.4.4 Learning-Based (Deep CNN / Transformer)
Modern methods (e.g., **MiDaS**, **DPT-Large**, **Monodepth2**) train neural networks on RGB → depth datasets like NYU Depth v2 or KITTI.

## 2.5 The Mathematical Anatomy of Stereo Disparity

Consider two pinhole cameras with:
- Identical focal length $f$
- Parallel optical axes
- Baseline separation $b$ along the X-axis
- Common image plane at $Z = f$

A 3D point $P$ at world coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ projects to:

$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X + b/2}{Z}, \quad x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - b/2}{Z}$$

The **disparity** is then:

$$d = x_L - x_R = \frac{f \cdot b}{Z}$$

This is the **cornerstone equation** of 2D-to-3D conversion. It establishes that **depth is inversely proportional to disparity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** When $Z \to \infty$, disparity $d \to 0$ (objects at infinity have zero parallax — this is why the Moon appears "flat" even with two eyes). When $Z$ is small, disparity is large, producing strong 3D effect.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1: Depth from Disparity (Stereo Triangulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **#1 most-asked derivation in KTU exams** for AR/VR Module 2.

### Given Setup
- Two identical pinhole cameras $C_L$ (left) and $C_R$ (right)
- Optical axes are parallel to the Z-axis
- Baseline $b$ separates their centers along the X-axis
- A 3D point $P(X, Y, Z)$ lies in front of both cameras
- Image planes are at $Z = f$ (focal length $f$)
- Principal point is at the image center for simplicity

### Step 1: Projection from Left Camera
Using the pinhole camera model, the image coordinates of $P$ in the left camera are:

$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X + b/2}{Z}$$

$$y_L = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

### Step 2: Projection from Right Camera
Similarly, in the right camera:

$$x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - b/2}{Z}$$

$$y_R = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$$

### Step 3: Compute Disparity
Disparity is defined as the horizontal pixel difference:

$$d = x_L - x_R$$

Substituting:

$$d = f \cdot \frac{X + b/2}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - b/2}{Z}$$

$$d = \frac{f}{Z} \cdot \left[ (X + b/2) - (X - b/2) \right]$$

$$d = \frac{f}{Z} \cdot b$$

### Step 4: Solve for Depth Z
Rearranging for Z:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Depth is **inversely proportional to disparity**. The product $f \cdot b$ is called the **stereo constant** and is determined by hardware geometry.

### Step 5: Recover 3D World Coordinates
Using $Z$, we back-project to obtain $X$ and $Y$:

$$X = \frac{(x_L - c_x) \cdot Z}{f} - \frac{b}{2}$$

$$Y = \frac{(y - c_y) \cdot Z}{f}$$

where $c_x, c_y$ are the coordinates of the principal point (typically the image center).

### Step 6: Numerical Verification (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A stereo camera has $f = 50$ mm, $b = 65$ mm (human IPD), and a detected disparity of $d = 5$ pixels. The pixel size is $p = 0.01$ mm/pixel.

**Solution:**

Step A — Convert disparity from pixels to mm:

$$d_{mm} = d \cdot p = 5 \cdot 0.01 = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

Step B — Apply depth formula:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d_{mm}} = \frac{50 \cdot 65}{0.05} = \frac{3250}{0.05}$$

$$Z = 65000 \text{ mm} = 65 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 5-pixel disparity corresponds to a 65-meter distant object — reasonable for far-field depth perception.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2: Depth Map Quantization

Depth values are stored as integers in memory. The conversion from real depth $Z$ to integer depth value $M$ uses a non-linear mapping (perspective projection):

$$M = \text{round}\left( \frac{1}{Z} \cdot \left( \frac{Z_{far} \cdot Z_{near}}{Z_{far} - Z_{near}} \right) \cdot 2^n + \text{offset} \right)$$

For simplicity, a normalized linear form is:

$$Z_{norm} = \frac{Z - Z_{near}}{Z_{far} - Z_{near}}$$

$$M = \text{round}(Z_{norm} \cdot (2^n - 1))$$

### Step-by-Step Numerical Example
Given: $Z = 5$ m, $Z_{near} = 0.5$ m, $Z_{far} = 100$ m, $n = 8$ bits.

Step 1 — Normalize:

$$Z_{norm} = \frac{5 - 0.5}{100 - 0.5} = \frac{4.5}{99.5} \approx 0.0452$$

Step 2 — Quantize:

$$M = \text{round}(0.0452 \cdot 255) = \text{round}(11.53) = 12$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** Linear quantization wastes bits at far distances. **Logarithmic quantization** (Z-buffer style) is preferred:

$$M = \text{round}\left( \frac{2^n - 1}{\ln(Z_{far}/Z_{near})} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{Z}{Z_{near}}\right) \right)$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

| Method | Input | Output | Accuracy | Computation | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual Annotation** | Single 2D image | Depth map | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Human hours | 2 |
| **SfM (Structure from Motion)** | Video sequence | Sparse 3D points | ⭐⭐⭐ | Medium | 3 |
| **MVS (Multi-View Stereo)** | Many 2D images | Dense point cloud | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | High | 3 |
| **Deep Learning (MiDaS, DPT)** | Single 2D image | Dense depth map | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | GPU-dependent | 4 |
| **DIBR (Depth Image Based Rendering)** | 2D image + depth | Stereo pair | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | Low | 4 |
| **Anaglyph Generation** | Stereo pair | Color-coded 2D | ⭐⭐ | Trivial | 2 |
| **Photogrammetry (Agisoft, Reality Capture)** | 50+ photos | Textured 3D mesh | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | High | 3 |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation #3: Anaglyph 3D Channel Extraction

The **anaglyph method** encodes stereo information in a single 2D image by overlaying left and right views in complementary color channels.

### Mathematical Form
Given left image $I_L$ and right image $I_R$, the anaglyph $A$ is:

$$A_R(x, y) = 0.299 \cdot I_L(x, y) + 0.587 \cdot I_L(x, y) + 0.114 \cdot I_L(x, y)$$

$$A_G(x, y) = 0.299 \cdot I_L(x, y) + 0.587 \cdot I_L(x, y) + 0.114 \cdot I_L(x, y)$$

$$A_B(x, y) = 0.299 \cdot I_R(x, y) + 0.587 \cdot I_R(x, y) + 0.114 \cdot I_R(x, y)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Simplified Color-Anaglyph encoding (most common):
> - **Red channel** ← Left eye image
> - **Green + Blue channels** ← Right eye image
> - The viewer wears red-cyan glasses to separate the channels.

### Why this works
The **red filter** in the left lens blocks red wavelengths, so the left eye sees only the green+blue content (right eye view). The **cyan filter** in the right lens blocks green+blue, leaving only red (left eye view). The brain fuses the two views into a 3D perception.

## 3.5 Derivation #4: Motion Parallax Depth Cue

When the camera translates with velocity $v$ in the X-direction, a static point at depth $Z$ shifts in image coordinates by:

$$\Delta x = \frac{f \cdot v \cdot \Delta t}{Z}$$

This is mathematically identical to disparity, but here $b = v \cdot \Delta t$ is the *temporal* baseline (distance traveled between frames). Converting to disparity:

$$d = \frac{f \cdot v \cdot \Delta t}{Z}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** For monocular video, **motion parallax alone can recover depth** — this is the principle behind Structure from Motion (SfM) and the entire monocular SLAM family (ORB-SLAM, DROID-SLAM).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level 2D-to-3D Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input 2D Image or Video] --> B[Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction]
    C --> D{Method Selection}
    D -->|Single Image| E[Monocular Depth Estimation]
    D -->|Stereo Pair| F[Disparity Computation]
    D -->|Multi-View| G[Structure from Motion]
    E --> H[Depth Map Generation]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Depth Refinement and Filtering]
    I --> J[3D Point Cloud or Mesh]
    J --> K[DIBR Stereo Synthesis]
    K --> L[VR or AR HMD Output]
    L --> M[Final 3D Scene Perception]
```

## 4.2 Stereo Camera Geometry Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph World3D[3D World Space]
        P[Point P at XYZ]
    end
    subgraph CamL[Left Camera]
        CLopt[Optical Center CL]
        CLimg[Image Plane at Z equals f]
    end
    subgraph CamR[Right Camera]
        CRopt[Optical Center CR]
        CRimg[Image Plane at Z equals f]
    end
    subgraph Recovered3D[Recovered Depth]
        Zout[Z equals f times b over d]
    end
    P -->|ray 1| CLopt
    P -->|ray 2| CRopt
    CLopt --> CLimg
    CRopt --> CRimg
    CLimg --> Disparity[Disparity Computation]
    CRimg --> Disparity
    Disparity --> Zout
    Zout --> Coord3D[X Y Z Coordinate Recovery]
```

## 4.3 2D-to-3D Method Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[2D to 3D Conversion Methods]
    Root --> Man[Manual]
    Root --> Semi[Semi Automatic]
    Root --> Auto[Automatic]

    Man --> Man1[Frame by Frame Depth Artist Annotation]
    Man --> Man2[Stereoscopic Painting in 3D Software]

    Semi --> Semi1[Sparse User Depth Scribbles]
    Semi --> Semi2[Edge Guided Propagation]
    Semi --> Semi3[Rotoscoping Workflow]

    Auto --> Auto1[Heuristic Based]
    Auto --> Auto2[Learning Based]
    Auto --> Auto3[Multi View Geometry Based]

    Auto1 --> H1[Defocus Blur Estimation]
    Auto1 --> H2[Texture Gradient Analysis]
    Auto1 --> H3[Color Haze Mapping]

    Auto2 --> L1[CNN Architectures like Monodepth2]
    Auto2 --> L2[Transformers like DPT and MiDaS]
    Auto2 --> L3[GAN Based Synthesis]

    Auto3 --> M1[Structure from Motion SfM]
    Auto3 --> M2[Multi View Stereo MVS]
    Auto3 --> M3[SLAM Based Reconstruction]
```

## 4.4 Depth-Image-Based Rendering (DIBR) Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RGB[Input RGB Image I of x y] --> Merge[Merge with Depth Map D of x y]
    Depth[Depth Map D of x y] --> Merge
    Merge --> Reproject[3D Reprojection using Pinhole Model]
    Reproject --> Shift[Pixel Shift by Disparity]
    Shift --> LeftView[Left Eye View]
    Shift --> RightView[Right Eye View]
    LeftView --> Holes[Disocclusion Hole Filling]
    RightView --> Holes
    Holes --> Out[Final Stereoscopic Pair for HMD]
```

## 4.5 Anaglyph Encoding-Decoding Round-Trip

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Left View Image] --> Encode[Anaglyph Encoder]
    R[Right View Image] --> Encode
    Encode --> Ana[2D Anaglyph Image]
    Ana --> Display[Display on 2D Monitor]
    Display --> Glasses[Red Cyan Glasses]
    Glasses --> LE[Left Eye Sees Right View]
    Glasses --> RE[Right Eye Sees Left View]
    LE --> Brain[Brain Fuses to 3D]
    RE --> Brain
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "disparity" in the context of 2D-to-3D conversion. How is it related to depth?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Disparity** $d$ is defined as the horizontal pixel displacement of a 3D point between the left and right eye (or left and right camera) image views:

$$d = x_L - x_R$$

It is **inversely proportional to depth** $Z$ according to the relationship:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}$$

where $f$ is the focal length and $b$ is the baseline (inter-camera distance). Closer objects produce larger disparities; distant objects produce smaller disparities. At infinite depth, disparity approaches zero, which is why very far objects appear flat in stereo viewing. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for relationship explanation)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any three monocular depth cues used in 2D-to-3D conversion algorithms.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Three monocular depth cues are:

1. **Occlusion** — When one object partially hides another, the hiding object is perceived as closer. (1 mark)
2. **Texture Gradient** — Regions where texture density increases are perceived as closer; sparse texture regions appear farther. (1 mark)
3. **Linear Perspective** — Parallel lines that converge in the image (e.g., railway tracks) imply increasing depth toward the vanishing point. (1 mark)

*(Alternative valid cues: relative size, atmospheric perspective, defocus blur, motion parallax — any 3 are acceptable.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | CO2]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **pinhole camera model** for stereo vision and derive the **depth-from-disparity equation** $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{d}$. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Depth-Image-Based Rendering (DIBR)** technique for converting a 2D image into a stereoscopic 3D pair. Discuss the **disocclusion problem** and two common hole-filling methods. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Diagram Description (2 marks):**

```
        Image Plane (Left)            Image Plane (Right)
            |                              |
            |  x_L                         |  x_R
   ---------●----------         -----------●---------
            |\                         |/
            | \                       / |
            |  \  f                 /   |
            |   \                 /     |
   ----------+----●----     ----●-------+----------
                C_L  ←—b—→  C_R
                (Baseline = b)
                          \
                           \  
                            \  
                             ● P(X, Y, Z)   <-- 3D World Point
```

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Both cameras are **identical pinhole cameras** with focal length $f$.
2. Optical axes are **parallel** and perpendicular to the baseline.
3. The image planes are **coplanar** and at distance $f$ from the optical centers.
4. The principal point is at the **image center** (no lens distortion).
5. Both cameras have the **same intrinsic parameters**.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

From similar triangles in the left camera:

$$\frac{x_L}{f} = \frac{X + b/2}{Z} \implies x_L = f \cdot \frac{X + b/2}{Z}$$

From the right camera:

$$\frac{x_R}{f} = \frac{X - b/2}{Z} \implies x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - b/2}{Z}$$

Disparity:

$$d = x_L - x_R = \frac{f}{Z} \cdot \left[ (X + b/2) - (X - b/2) \right] = \frac{f \cdot b}{Z}$$

Solving for depth:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}}$$

**(Total: 2 + 1 + 4 = 7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**DIBR Pipeline (3 marks):**

Depth-Image-Based Rendering synthesizes novel viewpoints from a single 2D image $I(x, y)$ and its corresponding depth map $D(x, y)$. The steps are:

1. **Reproject** every pixel $(x, y, Z)$ to 3D space using the camera intrinsics:

$$X = \frac{(x - c_x) \cdot Z}{f}, \quad Y = \frac{(y - c_y) \cdot Z}{f}$$

2. **Re-project** the 3D point onto the virtual left/right image plane with a horizontal shift equal to $\pm d/2$:

$$x_{L/R} = \frac{f \cdot X}{Z} \pm \frac{b}{2}$$

3. **Copy** the color value $I(x, y)$ to the new location in the stereoscopic view.

**Disocclusion Problem (2 marks):**

When a pixel is shifted to make way for newly revealed background, a "hole" (black region) appears in the destination image. This happens because the original 2D image contains no information about the previously occluded area. For example, the area behind a foreground object becomes visible after the object is shifted sideways, but no color data exists for that background region.

**Two Common Hole-Filling Methods (2 marks):**

1. **Inpainting (Exemplar-Based)** — Propagates pixel colors from neighboring valid regions into the hole using patch-matching algorithms (e.g., Criminisi et al.'s algorithm).
2. **Layered Depth Image (LDI)** — Stores multiple depth values per pixel (background layers), so that disoccluded regions can be filled from the stored background layer.
3. *(Alternative: Bilateral filtering, edge-aware smoothing)*

**(Total: 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 2 | CO2]**
**(a)** Explain the **Anaglyph 3D** technique for 2D-to-3D conversion. With suitable equations, describe how left and right eye images are combined into a single 2D anaglyph and later separated by colored glasses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Structure from Motion (SfM)** as an automatic 2D-to-3D conversion technique. List its pipeline stages and discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Anaglyph Concept (2 marks):**
The anaglyph method encodes a stereo pair into a single 2D image using **complementary color channels**. The viewer wears **red-cyan glasses**; the brain fuses the two filtered views to perceive depth.

**Encoding Equation (3 marks):**
For the standard Red-Cyan anaglyph:

$$A_R(x, y) = I_L(x, y)$$

$$A_G(x, y) = 0.7 \cdot I_R(x, y)$$

$$A_B(x, y) = 0.7 \cdot I_R(x, y)$$

That is, the **red channel is sourced from the left image** and the **green and blue channels are sourced from the right image**.

**Decoding via Glasses (2 marks):**
- The **red lens** blocks the red component, so the left eye sees only the green+blue content (the right-eye view).
- The **cyan lens** blocks the green and blue components, so the right eye sees only the red content (the left-eye view).
- The brain fuses these two views as if from two slightly offset eyes, producing depth perception.

**Limitations to Mention:** Color distortion (true colors are lost), eye strain, ghosting artifacts.

**(Total: 2 + 3 + 2 = 7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**SfM Definition (1 mark):**
Structure from Motion reconstructs 3D scene geometry and camera poses from a **sequence of 2D images** taken from different viewpoints by analyzing **feature correspondences and motion**.

**Pipeline Stages (4 marks):**

1. **Feature Detection** — Detect keypoints (e.g., SIFT, SURF, ORB) in each frame.
2. **Feature Matching** — Match corresponding keypoints across frames using descriptor similarity.
3. **Epipolar Geometry Estimation** — Compute the **Essential Matrix** $E$ or **Fundamental Matrix** $F$ to establish geometric relationships between views.
4. **Triangulation** — Recover 3D points from 2D correspondences using the camera projection matrices.
5. **Bundle Adjustment** — Jointly refine all 3D points and camera poses by minimizing **reprojection error**:

$$\min_{P_i, X_j} \sum_{i,j} \left\| \pi(P_i, X_j) - x_{ij} \right\|^2$$

where $P_i$ are camera poses, $X_j$ are 3D points, $\pi$ is the projection function, and $x_{ij}$ are observed 2D keypoints.

6. **Dense Reconstruction** (optional) — Use Multi-View Stereo to densify the sparse point cloud.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Works from casual video footage (no special equipment).
- Produces accurate metric 3D reconstructions.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Computationally expensive (especially bundle adjustment).
- Fails in textureless regions or with rapid motion.
- Requires sufficient baseline between views.

**(Total: 1 + 4 + 1 + 1 = 7 marks)**

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in 2D-to-3D Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the baseline units** — Students write $Z = f \cdot b / d$ but forget that $f$ and $b$ must be in **same units** (mm or m), and $d$ must be converted from pixels to physical units using the pixel pitch. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing disparity with depth direction** — Disparity is a *scalar magnitude*; depth is the *perpendicular distance* from the camera plane. They are **inversely related**, not equal. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Skipping the principal point** $c_x, c_y$ in reprojection equations. The general form is $X = (x - c_x) Z / f$, not $X = xZ/f$. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
>
> 4. **DIBR diagrams missing the disocclusion arrows** — Examiners *always* check for this in DIBR answers. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Conflating SfM with MVS** — SfM produces *sparse* point clouds from *video*; MVS produces *dense* point clouds from *many still images*. Mixing them up = **−2 marks.**

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — 2D to 3D Conversion**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- ✅ **Disparity** $d$ = horizontal pixel difference between left & right views: $d = x_L - x_R$
- ✅ **Depth from disparity**: $Z = f \cdot b / d$ (inverse relationship)
- ✅ **IPD baseline**: $b \approx 65$ mm (human Inter-Pupillary Distance)
- ✅ **Reprojection formulas**: $X = (x - c_x) Z / f$, $Y = (y - c_y) Z / f$

### 🎯 Three Pillars of 2D-to-3D
- ✅ **Monocular cues**: Occlusion, texture gradient, perspective, defocus, motion parallax
- ✅ **Binocular cues**: Disparity, convergence angle
- ✅ **Camera geometry**: Pinhole model, epipolar geometry

### 🛠️ Four Method Families (Exam Must-Know)
- ✅ **Manual** — Artist draws depth per frame (highest accuracy, highest cost)
- ✅ **Semi-Automatic** — User gives sparse depth hints (balanced)
- ✅ **Automatic Heuristic** — Defocus, haze, texture analysis
- ✅ **Automatic Learning-Based** — MiDaS, DPT, Monodepth2 (state-of-the-art)

### 📐 Key Numerical Values to Memorize
- ✅ IPD = **65 mm**
- ✅ Stereo acuity = **10 arc-seconds**
- ✅ Standard FOV = **90°–120°** horizontal
- ✅ Depth map precision = **8-bit or 16-bit**

### 🔄 Key Techniques
- ✅ **DIBR** — Depth-Image-Based Rendering for stereo synthesis; faces **disocclusion hole problem**
- ✅ **Anaglyph** — Red-cyan color encoding of stereo pair in single 2D image
- ✅ **SfM** — Structure from Motion; recovers 3D from video via feature tracking and bundle adjustment
- ✅ **MVS** — Multi-View Stereo; densifies SfM output using many still images

### ⚠️ Common Exam Traps
- ✅ Disparity ∝ 1/Z (inverse, not direct)
- ✅ Focal length and baseline must be in **same units**
- ✅ Disparity in **pixels** must be multiplied by pixel pitch to get mm
- ✅ SfM = sparse; MVS = dense
- ✅ DIBR = left and right views need disocclusion hole filling

### 📚 Acronyms to Recall
- ✅ **DIBR** = Depth-Image-Based Rendering
- ✅ **SfM** = Structure from Motion
- ✅ **MVS** = Multi-View Stereo
- ✅ **SLAM** = Simultaneous Localization and Mapping
- ✅ **IPD** = Inter-Pupillary Distance
- ✅ **FOV** = Field of View
- ✅ **LDI** = Layered Depth Image
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3D Space Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Space Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **3D Space Curve:** A **3D Space Curve** is a one-dimensional geometric locus embedded in three-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^3$, parameterized by a single scalar variable (typically $t \in [a, b]$), such that every point on the curve corresponds to a unique vector-valued position function $\vec{r}(t) = (x(t),\ y(t),\ z(t))$, where $x(t)$, $y(t)$, and $z(t)$ are continuous (preferably $C^2$ or $C^3$) real-valued functions of the parameter $t$.

In the context of the KTU 2024 PECPT634 syllabus (Augmented and Virtual Reality), a **3D space curve** is treated as the mathematical backbone for describing **paths, trajectories, and smooth geometric primitives** that govern camera motion, virtual object animation, avatar locomotion, and procedural scene generation in immersive environments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a drone flying through your living room.** The drone's flight path is invisible, but every single point it visits during its journey — from takeoff to landing — traces out a 3D space curve. The drone is the *moving point*, the room is the *3D space*, and the invisible path is the *space curve*. Now imagine telling the drone exactly where to go — high yield, sharp turn, gentle glide — using mathematical equations. Those equations **are** the space curve.

Another everyday analogy: a roller-coaster track. The track is a 3D space curve. At every point on the track, the coaster has a direction of motion (tangent), a sense of "leaning inward" (curvature), and a sense of "twisting" (torsion). In AR/VR, we mathematically describe these three attributes to make virtual motion look believable and smooth.

## 1.3 Why 3D Space Curves Matter in AR/VR

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** *Geometric primitives of 3D content — points, vectors, and parametric curves — form the foundation for object representation, animation paths, and interaction geometry in immersive content pipelines.*

In AR/VR pipelines, space curves are used for:

| AR/VR Application | Role of 3D Space Curve |
|------------------|------------------------|
| **Camera Trajectories** | Defining cinematic flythroughs, cutscene paths, and stabilized user view smoothing |
| **Avatar Locomotion** | Generating footstep paths, walking cycles, and procedural locomotion blends |
| **Object Animation** | Orbital motion, projectile arcs, particle emitter paths |
| **Procedural Geometry** | Tube/extrusion around a curve, ribbon meshes, sword trails, laser beams |
| **Interaction Geometry** | Ray-cast Bezier handles, gesture recognition trajectories (e.g., drawing in VR) |
| **Spline-Based UI** | Smooth menu curves, viewport edge transitions, curved HUD elements |

## 1.4 Core Components of a Space Curve

Every space curve is described by three mutually orthogonal vector fields, collectively known as the **Frenet–Serret Frame** (also called the **TNB Frame** or **Moving Trihedron**):

- **Tangent Vector** $\vec{T}(t)$ — the instantaneous direction of motion.
- **Principal Normal Vector** $\vec{N}(t)$ — the direction of curvature (centripetal direction).
- **Binormal Vector** $\vec{B}(t)$ — the direction perpendicular to both, defining the curve's plane orientation.

These three vectors form an orthonormal right-handed coordinate frame that **moves along the curve** and is critical for orienting virtual cameras, lighting, and extruded geometry in AR/VR scenes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parametric Representation of a Space Curve

A space curve is fundamentally a **vector-valued function of one variable**:

$$\vec{r}(t) = x(t)\,\hat{i} + y(t)\,\hat{j} + z(t)\,\hat{k}, \qquad t \in [a,b]$$

The smoothness of the curve depends on the differentiability of $x(t)$, $y(t)$, $z(t)$. For AR/VR rendering pipelines, at minimum **$C^2$ continuity** (continuous up to the second derivative) is required to avoid visible jerk in animated motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Regular Curve Condition:** A curve is called **regular** at $t = t_0$ if $\vec{r}\,'(t_0) \neq \vec{0}$. A regular curve is everywhere locally "smooth enough" to admit a well-defined tangent direction. In AR/VR, regular curves are essential because rendering engines compute screen-space tangents at every vertex.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Computation of the Frenet–Serret Frame

The Frenet–Serret frame is built up from elementary vector operations:

### Step 1 — Unit Tangent Vector $\vec{T}(t)$

The derivative $\vec{r}\,'(t)$ gives the direction of motion. To make it a unit vector, normalize it:

$$\vec{T}(t) = \frac{\vec{r}\,'(t)}{\vert\,\vec{r}\,'(t)\,\vert}$$

> **Why this works:** Normalization removes the effect of parameterization speed, isolating *direction* from *magnitude*. This is crucial in AR/VR because animations should respond to **direction**, not to raw velocity magnitude (which depends on how the curve was parameterized).

### Step 2 — Principal Normal Vector $\vec{N}(t)$

Differentiate $\vec{T}(t)$ with respect to arc length $s$ (not $t$). This gives a vector that points toward the curve's center of curvature. Normalize to obtain:

$$\vec{N}(t) = \frac{\dfrac{d\vec{T}}{ds}}{\left\vert\,\dfrac{d\vec{T}}{ds}\,\right\vert}$$

> **Why this works:** $\dfrac{d\vec{T}}{ds}$ measures how fast the tangent is *rotating*, i.e., how sharply the curve is bending. Its direction points to the inside of the bend. The arc-length parameterization removes the speed bias, leaving pure geometric bending information.

### Step 3 — Binormal Vector $\vec{B}(t)$

The binormal is defined as the **cross product** of $\vec{T}$ and $\vec{N}$:

$$\vec{B}(t) = \vec{T}(t) \times \vec{N}(t)$$

> **Why this works:** Since $\vec{T}$ and $\vec{N}$ are unit and orthogonal, their cross product is automatically unit, orthogonal to both, and obeys the right-hand rule. $\vec{B}$ defines the **osculating plane** — the plane of "instantaneous bending" — which is critical for orienting billboards, particle ribbons, and sword trails in VR.

### Step 4 — Curvature $\kappa(s)$

Curvature quantifies *how sharply* the curve bends:

$$\kappa(s) = \left\vert \frac{d\vec{T}}{ds} \right\vert = \frac{\vert\,\vec{r}\,'(t) \times \vec{r}\,''(t)\,\vert}{\vert\,\vec{r}\,'(t)\,\vert^{3}}$$

> **Why this matters:** In VR, high-curvature regions (tight bends) require **denser tessellation** to render smoothly. Low-curvature regions (straight or gentle curves) can use fewer polygons. Adaptive tessellation driven by curvature is a standard VR optimization technique.

### Step 5 — Torsion $\tau(s)$

Torsion measures *how fast* the osculating plane twists:

$$\tau(s) = -\frac{d\vec{B}}{ds} \cdot \vec{N}$$

A more directly computable form:

$$\tau(t) = \frac{(\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'') \cdot \vec{r}\,'''}{\vert\,\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,''\,\vert^{2}}$$

> **Why this matters:** Torsion is **zero** for planar curves (curves lying entirely in one plane). For 3D space curves in VR — like corkscrew weapon trails or helical motion paths — torsion is non-zero and defines the "screw-like" character.

## 2.3 The Frenet–Serret Formulas — The Differential Engine

The frame vectors evolve along the curve according to the classical Frenet–Serret system:

$$\frac{d\vec{T}}{ds} = \kappa\,\vec{N}$$

$$\frac{d\vec{N}}{ds} = -\kappa\,\vec{T} + \tau\,\vec{B}$$

$$\frac{d\vec{B}}{ds} = -\tau\,\vec{N}$$

> **Why this works:** The system expresses that as you walk along the curve, the tangent rotates toward the normal (at rate $\kappa$), the normal rotates in the osculating plane (with components $-\kappa$ toward tangent and $+\tau$ toward binormal), and the binormal rotates in the normal–binormal plane (at rate $-\tau$). It is the geometric "rotation law" of 3D motion.

## 2.4 Bezier Curves — The AR/VR Designer's Workhorse

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bezier Curves** are the **most widely used curve representation** in real-time AR/VR graphics for defining smooth paths with a small number of intuitive control points.

A **cubic Bezier curve** is defined by four control points $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3$:

$$\vec{B}(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t\, P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2\, P_2 + t^3 P_3, \quad t \in [0,1]$$

Properties:
- **Affine Invariance** — transforms apply to control points, then curve transforms equivalently.
- **Convex Hull Property** — the curve lies entirely inside the convex hull of its control points (enables cheap bounding-volume collision culling).
- **Endpoint Interpolation** — the curve passes through $P_0$ (at $t=0$) and $P_3$ (at $t=1$), but only *influenced* by $P_1$ and $P_2$.
- **Tangents at endpoints** — $\vec{B}\,'(0) = 3(P_1 - P_0)$, $\vec{B}\,'(1) = 3(P_3 - P_2)$.

> **Why this matters:** VR authoring tools (Unity Editor, Unreal Editor, Blender) expose Bezier handles as draggable UI widgets precisely because the four-control-point design maps naturally to mouse interaction. Unity's `AnimationCurve` and Unreal's `UCurveFloat` are both Bezier- or Hermite-based.

## 2.5 B-Splines — Localized Control for Long, Complex Paths

A **B-spline of order $k$** is a piecewise polynomial curve defined by a knot vector $\mathcal{U} = \{u_0, u_1, \dots, u_{n+k+1}\}$ and control points $P_0, P_1, \dots, P_n$:

$$\vec{C}(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,k}(u)\, P_i$$

where $N_{i,k}(u)$ are the **B-spline basis functions**, computed by the Cox–de Boor recursion:

$$N_{i,0}(u) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } u_i \leq u < u_{i+1} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

$$N_{i,k}(u) = \frac{u - u_i}{u_{i+k} - u_i} N_{i,k-1}(u) + \frac{u_{i+k+1} - u}{u_{i+k+1} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,k-1}(u)$$

> **Why this matters:** Unlike a global Bezier curve (where moving any control point affects the *entire* curve), a B-spline has **local support** — moving $P_i$ only affects the curve in the parameter range $[u_i, u_{i+k+1}]$. This makes B-splines ideal for **long VR camera paths** or **multi-keyframe animation tracks** where designers iterate locally.

## 2.6 Catmull–Rom Spline — The VR Animation Industry Default

> [!NOTE]
> **The Catmull–Rom Spline is the de-facto standard for VR/AR camera flythroughs and animated object paths** because it passes through every control point (interpolates) and has $C^1$ continuity with simple, closed-form evaluation.

For four control points $P_{i-1}, P_i, P_{i+1}, P_{i+2}$, the curve segment over $t \in [0,1]$ is:

$$\vec{C}(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & t & t^2 & t^3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & -5 & 4 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 & -3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} P_{i-1} \\ P_i \\ P_{i+1} \\ P_{i+2} \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Why this matters:** Unreal Engine's `USplineComponent`, Unity's Cinemachine spline paths, and many VR authoring plugins use Catmull–Rom because designers can "drop keyframes" and the curve automatically threads through them — no extra tangent handles needed.

## 2.7 Hermite Curve — Tangent-Driven Specification

A **cubic Hermite curve** is defined by two endpoints and two tangent vectors:

$$\vec{H}(t) = \begin{bmatrix} t^3 & t^2 & t & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 & 1 & 1 \\ -3 & 3 & -2 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} P_0 \\ P_1 \\ \vec{T}_0 \\ \vec{T}_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is the **mathematical bridge** between Bezier and Catmull–Rom: a Bezier curve is a Hermite curve whose tangents are derived from the inner control points; Catmull–Rom tangents are derived from neighboring keyframes.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. KTU ESE questions in this module frequently test these formulas directly or via short derivations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Engineering Use in AR/VR |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Parametric curve | $\vec{r}(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t))$ | Master definition for any animated path |
| 2 | Tangent vector | $\vec{T} = \dfrac{\vec{r}\,'}{\vert \vec{r}\,' \vert}$ | Camera forward direction, velocity vector |
| 3 | Curvature | $\kappa = \dfrac{\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert}{\vert \vec{r}\,' \vert^3}$ | Adaptive tessellation, motion sickness control |
| 4 | Torsion | $\tau = \dfrac{(\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'') \cdot \vec{r}\,\,'''}{\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert^2}$ | Detects "out-of-plane" twist (helical motion) |
| 5 | Binormal | $\vec{B} = \vec{T} \times \vec{N}$ | Ribbon orientation, particle trail normal |
| 6 | Frenet tangent rate | $\dfrac{d\vec{T}}{ds} = \kappa\,\vec{N}$ | Rotational acceleration of moving frame |
| 7 | Frenet normal rate | $\dfrac{d\vec{N}}{ds} = -\kappa\,\vec{T} + \tau\,\vec{B}$ | Combined bend-and-twist dynamics |
| 8 | Frenet binormal rate | $\dfrac{d\vec{B}}{ds} = -\tau\,\vec{N}$ | Osculating plane rotation |
| 9 | Arc length | $s = \displaystyle\int_a^b \vert \vec{r}\,'(t) \vert \, dt$ | Constant-speed camera flythroughs (anti-cybersickness) |
| 10 | Cubic Bezier | $\vec{B}(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 P_2 + t^3 P_3$ | UI curves, animation tweens, gesture strokes |
| 11 | B-spline | $\vec{C}(u) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} N_{i,k}(u) P_i$ | Long cinematic paths, avatar skeletal arcs |
| 12 | Cox–de Boor recursion | $N_{i,k}(u) = \dfrac{u - u_i}{u_{i+k} - u_i} N_{i,k-1}(u) + \dfrac{u_{i+k+1} - u}{u_{i+k+1} - u_{i+1}} N_{i+1,k-1}(u)$ | Basis function generation engine |
| 13 | Catmull–Rom segment | $\vec{C}(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[1\ t\ t^2\ t^3]\,M_{CR}\,[P_{i-1}\ P_i\ P_{i+1}\ P_{i+2}]^T$ | Spline flythroughs, Cinemachine tracks |
| 14 | Hermite curve | $\vec{H}(t) = [t^3\ t^2\ t\ 1]\,M_H\,[P_0\ P_1\ \vec{T}_0\ \vec{T}_1]^T$ | Tangent-driven camera control |
| 15 | Frenet trihedron check | $\vec{T}\cdot\vec{N} = 0$, $\vec{T}\cdot\vec{B} = 0$, $\vec{N}\cdot\vec{B} = 0$, $\vert\vec{T}\vert=\vert\vec{N}\vert=\vert\vec{B}\vert=1$ | Validate orthonormal frame in shader code |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility Summary

- **Cinematography in VR:** Reparameterizing a curve by arc length gives *constant-speed* camera motion, which drastically reduces VR motion sickness (cybersickness).
- **Adaptive LOD (Level of Detail):** Curvature $\kappa$ is the gold-standard signal for adding/removing polygon density along extruded curves (e.g., VR sword trails).
- **Collision Geometry:** The convex-hull property of Bezier curves enables rapid broad-phase collision checks in VR physics.
- **Procedural Content:** Noise-driven Bezier perturbations generate endless, varied terrain paths, asteroid belts, or NPC patrol routes.
- **Gesture Recognition:** Trajectories drawn by VR controllers are fit to cubic Bezier or Catmull–Rom curves for stroke classification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Analytical Derivations — the kind of step-by-step reasoning KTU board examiners look for in 14-mark problems.** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No skips, no shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation A — Tangent Vector from First Principles

**Given:** A space curve $\vec{r}(t) = (\cos t,\ \sin t,\ t)$.

**Goal:** Compute the unit tangent vector $\vec{T}(t)$.

**Step 1.** Differentiate $\vec{r}(t)$ component-wise with respect to $t$.

$$\vec{r}\,'(t) = \frac{d}{dt}(\cos t,\ \sin t,\ t)$$

$$\vec{r}\,'(t) = (-\sin t,\ \cos t,\ 1)$$

**Step 2.** Compute the magnitude of $\vec{r}\,'(t)$ using the Euclidean norm.

$$\vert \vec{r}\,'(t) \vert = \sqrt{(-\sin t)^2 + (\cos t)^2 + (1)^2}$$

$$\vert \vec{r}\,'(t) \vert = \sqrt{\sin^2 t + \cos^2 t + 1}$$

Using the Pythagorean identity $\sin^2 t + \cos^2 t = 1$:

$$\vert \vec{r}\,'(t) \vert = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 3.** Form the unit tangent by dividing $\vec{r}\,'(t)$ by its magnitude.

$$\vec{T}(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,(-\sin t,\ \cos t,\ 1)$$

> **Geometric interpretation:** The given curve is a **circular helix** (think: a spring). Its tangent has constant horizontal component (circular) and constant vertical drift (linear rise), giving the helix its characteristic "corkscrew" motion. The $\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ factor ensures $\vert \vec{T} \vert = 1$ — verified by $\tfrac{1}{2}(\sin^2 t + \cos^2 t + 1) = \tfrac{1}{2}(2) = 1$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation B — Curvature of the Circular Helix

**Continuing from 3.1,** compute the curvature $\kappa$ of $\vec{r}(t) = (\cos t,\ \sin t,\ t)$.

**Step 1.** Compute the second derivative $\vec{r}\,''(t)$.

$$\vec{r}\,''(t) = \frac{d}{dt}(-\sin t,\ \cos t,\ 1)$$

$$\vec{r}\,''(t) = (-\cos t,\ -\sin t,\ 0)$$

**Step 2.** Compute the cross product $\vec{r}\,'(t) \times \vec{r}\,''(t)$.

$$\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -\sin t & \cos t & 1 \\ -\cos t & -\sin t & 0 \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding along the first row:

$$\hat{i}\,(\cos t \cdot 0 - 1 \cdot (-\sin t)) - \hat{j}\,((-\sin t)\cdot 0 - 1\cdot(-\cos t)) + \hat{k}\,((-\sin t)(-\sin t) - \cos t\,(-\cos t))$$

$$\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' = \hat{i}\,(\sin t) - \hat{j}\,(\cos t) + \hat{k}\,(\sin^2 t + \cos^2 t)$$

$$\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' = (\sin t,\ -\cos t,\ 1)$$

**Step 3.** Compute the magnitude of the cross product.

$$\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert = \sqrt{\sin^2 t + \cos^2 t + 1} = \sqrt{2}$$

**Step 4.** Compute $\vert \vec{r}\,' \vert^3$. From 3.1, $\vert \vec{r}\,' \vert = \sqrt{2}$, so:

$$\vert \vec{r}\,' \vert^3 = (\sqrt{2})^3 = 2\sqrt{2}$$

**Step 5.** Apply the curvature formula.

$$\kappa = \frac{\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert}{\vert \vec{r}\,' \vert^3} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

> **Geometric interpretation:** The circular helix has **constant curvature** $\kappa = \tfrac{1}{2}$. This makes physical sense: a helix is just a circle "lifted" vertically, and its bending geometry repeats every turn. In VR, this means a constant-density mesh extruded along the helix will look uniformly smooth — no adaptive tessellation needed.

## 3.3 Derivation C — Torsion of the Circular Helix

**Continuing from 3.1 and 3.2,** compute the torsion $\tau$.

**Step 1.** Compute the third derivative $\vec{r}\,\,'''(t)$.

$$\vec{r}\,\,'''(t) = \frac{d}{dt}(-\cos t,\ -\sin t,\ 0)$$

$$\vec{r}\,\,'''(t) = (\sin t,\ -\cos t,\ 0)$$

**Step 2.** Compute the numerator $(\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'') \cdot \vec{r}\,\,'''$.

From Step 2 of Derivation B: $\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' = (\sin t,\ -\cos t,\ 1)$.

$$(\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'') \cdot \vec{r}\,\,''' = (\sin t)(\sin t) + (-\cos t)(-\cos t) + (1)(0)$$

$$= \sin^2 t + \cos^2 t + 0 = 1$$

**Step 3.** Compute the denominator $\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert^2$. From Step 3 of Derivation B:

$$\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert^2 = (\sqrt{2})^2 = 2$$

**Step 4.** Apply the torsion formula.

$$\tau = \frac{(\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'') \cdot \vec{r}\,\,'''}{\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

> **Geometric interpretation:** The helix has **constant torsion** $\tau = \tfrac{1}{2}$ — it twists at a uniform rate per unit parameter. The ratio $\tau/\kappa = 1$ indicates a "perfect" helix where each full turn advances by exactly one unit of $t$. For VR weapon trails or helical particle effects, this constant torsion allows pre-computed Frenet frames to be cached — a major rendering optimization.

## 3.4 Derivation D — Principal Normal and Binormal for the Helix

**Goal:** Complete the Frenet frame for $\vec{r}(t) = (\cos t,\ \sin t,\ t)$.

**Step 1.** From Derivation A: $\vec{T}(t) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-\sin t,\ \cos t,\ 1)$.

**Step 2.** Differentiate $\vec{T}(t)$ with respect to $t$.

$$\frac{d\vec{T}}{dt} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\,(-\cos t,\ -\sin t,\ 0)$$

**Step 3.** Convert to derivative with respect to arc length $s$. Since $\dfrac{ds}{dt} = \vert \vec{r}\,' \vert = \sqrt{2}$:

$$\frac{d\vec{T}}{ds} = \frac{d\vec{T}/dt}{ds/dt} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(-\cos t,\ -\sin t,\ 0)}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{2}(-\cos t,\ -\sin t,\ 0)$$

**Step 4.** Compute its magnitude: $\left\vert \tfrac{1}{2}(-\cos t,\ -\sin t,\ 0) \right\vert = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\cos^2 t + \sin^2 t} = \tfrac{1}{2}$. This equals $\kappa$, confirming Derivation B. ✓

**Step 5.** Normalize to get $\vec{N}(t)$.

$$\vec{N}(t) = \frac{d\vec{T}/ds}{\vert d\vec{T}/ds \vert} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}(-\cos t,\ -\sin t,\ 0)}{1/2} = (-\cos t,\ -\sin t,\ 0)$$

**Step 6.** Compute $\vec{B}(t) = \vec{T}(t) \times \vec{N}(t)$.

$$\vec{B}(t) = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -\tfrac{\sin t}{\sqrt{2}} & \tfrac{\cos t}{\sqrt{2}} & \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \\ -\cos t & -\sin t & 0 \end{vmatrix}$$

Expanding:

$$\hat{i}\left(\tfrac{\cos t}{\sqrt{2}}\cdot 0 - \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\cdot(-\sin t)\right) - \hat{j}\left(-\tfrac{\sin t}{\sqrt{2}}\cdot 0 - \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\cdot(-\cos t)\right) + \hat{k}\left(\tfrac{\sin^2 t}{\sqrt{2}} + \tfrac{\cos^2 t}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

$$\vec{B}(t) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\sin t,\ -\cos t,\ 1)$$

**Step 7.** Verify orthonormality.

- $\vec{T}\cdot\vec{N} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\sin t\cos t - \cos t\sin t + 0) = 0$ ✓
- $\vec{T}\cdot\vec{B} = \tfrac{1}{2}(-\sin^2 t - \cos^2 t + 1) = 0$ ✓
- $\vec{N}\cdot\vec{B} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\sin t\cos t + \cos t\sin t + 0) \cdot 1 = 0$... 

  Wait — recompute: $\vec{N}\cdot\vec{B} = (-\cos t)(\tfrac{\sin t}{\sqrt{2}}) + (-\sin t)(\tfrac{-\cos t}{\sqrt{2}}) + (0)(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}) = \tfrac{-\cos t\sin t}{\sqrt{2}} + \tfrac{\sin t\cos t}{\sqrt{2}} + 0 = 0$ ✓

> **Engineering takeaway:** The Frenet frame of a helix is itself a slowly rotating coordinate system. VR rendering shaders can store $(\vec{T}, \vec{N}, \vec{B})$ in a **3×3 matrix per curve vertex** and apply it as a world-to-local transform for billboards, ribbons, and sword trails.

## 3.5 Derivation E — Bezier Curve Tangent at Endpoint

**Given:** Cubic Bezier $\vec{B}(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 P_2 + t^3 P_3$.

**Goal:** Show that $\vec{B}\,'(0) = 3(P_1 - P_0)$.

**Step 1.** Apply the product rule to each term.

$$\vec{B}\,'(t) = -3(1-t)^2 P_0 + 3\bigl[(1-t)^2 - 2(1-t)t\bigr] P_1 + 3\bigl[2(1-t)t - t^2\bigr] P_2 + 3t^2 P_3$$

**Step 2.** Simplify the bracketed coefficients.

- First bracket: $-(1-t)^2$
- Second bracket: $(1-t)^2 - 2t(1-t) = (1-t)[(1-t) - 2t] = (1-t)(1-3t)$
- Third bracket: $2t(1-t) - t^2 = t[2(1-t) - t] = t(2 - 3t)$

So:

$$\vec{B}\,'(t) = -3(1-t)^2 P_0 + 3(1-t)(1-3t) P_1 + 3t(2-3t) P_2 + 3t^2 P_3$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $t = 0$.

- $-(1-0)^2 = -1$
- $(1-0)(1-0) = 1$
- $0 \cdot (\text{anything}) = 0$
- $0^2 = 0$

$$\vec{B}\,'(0) = -3 P_0 + 3 P_1 = 3(P_1 - P_0) \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** This result is *exactly* why VR authoring tools can show a "tangent handle" emerging from $P_0$ and pointing toward $P_1$: the tangent direction is the line $P_0 \to P_1$. Designers drag $P_1$ to retime/smooth the takeoff of any Bezier-driven animation.

## 3.6 Derivation F — Arc-Length Reparameterization (Why It Matters for VR Cybersickness)

**Given:** Any regular curve $\vec{r}(t)$, $t \in [0, 1]$.

**Goal:** Compute arc length and reparameterize so that motion is at constant speed.

**Step 1.** Arc length from $t=a$ to $t=b$:

$$s(a, b) = \int_a^b \vert \vec{r}\,'(u) \vert \, du$$

**Step 2.** Total arc length over $[0,1]$:

$$S = s(0,1) = \int_0^1 \vert \vec{r}\,'(u) \vert \, du$$

**Step 3.** Define the new parameter $\tau \in [0, S]$:

$$\tau = \int_0^{t} \vert \vec{r}\,'(u) \vert \, du$$

**Step 4.** Invert numerically (Newton's method or table lookup) to obtain $t(\tau)$. Substitute back into $\vec{r}(t)$:

$$\vec{R}(\tau) = \vec{r}\bigl(t(\tau)\bigr), \qquad \tau \in [0, S]$$

**Step 5.** Verify constant speed: $\left\vert \dfrac{d\vec{R}}{d\tau} \right\vert = 1$.

$$\frac{d\vec{R}}{d\tau} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt}\cdot\frac{dt}{d\tau} = \vec{r}\,'(t)\cdot\frac{1}{\vert \vec{r}\,'(t)\vert} = \vec{T}(t)$$

$$\left\vert \frac{d\vec{R}}{d\tau} \right\vert = \vert \vec{T}(t) \vert = 1 \quad \blacksquare$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** When a VR camera traverses $\vec{R}(\tau)$ with $\tau$ advancing at, say, 1 unit per second, the camera moves **exactly one meter per second through 3D space** — regardless of how the original parameter $t$ was spaced. This is the **#1 cybersickness mitigation** for VR cinematics: the human vestibular system tolerates constant linear velocity far better than accelerating/decelerating motion.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All questions below are modeled strictly on the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for PECPT634.** Part A is 3 marks each. Part B is 14 marks with **internal choice** (Question A OR Question B).

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**Define a 3D space curve. Explain the Frenet–Serret frame and list its three unit vectors with their geometric meaning.**

**Model Answer:**

A **3D space curve** is a one-dimensional locus embedded in $\mathbb{R}^3$ described by a vector-valued function $\vec{r}(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t))$, where $x, y, z$ are real-valued functions of a single scalar parameter $t \in [a,b]$.

The **Frenet–Serret frame** (TNB frame) is the orthonormal coordinate system that travels along a regular space curve, defined at every point by:

1. **Tangent vector $\vec{T}$** — the instantaneous direction of motion along the curve.
2. **Principal normal $\vec{N}$** — the direction of curvature, pointing toward the inside of the bend.
3. **Binormal $\vec{B}$** — the cross product $\vec{T} \times \vec{N}$, defining the orientation of the osculating plane.

All three vectors are mutually orthogonal unit vectors and obey the right-hand rule. **[3 marks]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between an interpolating curve and an approximating curve. Give one example of each used in AR/VR pipelines.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Interpolating Curve | Approximating Curve |
|--------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Passes through control points?** | Yes, every control point lies on the curve | No, control points only "pull" the curve |
| **Locality** | Typically global (each control point affects whole curve) | Local support possible (e.g., B-spline) |
| **Example in AR/VR** | Catmull–Rom spline (VR camera flythroughs) | Cubic Bezier (UI gesture strokes) |

In AR/VR, interpolating curves (Catmull–Rom, Hermite) are preferred when designers want to "drop keyframes" and have the curve thread through them. Approximating curves (Bezier, B-spline) are preferred when fine-grained tangent control is needed via handle points. **[3 marks]**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Attempt **Question A OR Question B**. Each carries 14 marks and has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **unit tangent vector**, **principal normal vector**, and **binormal vector** of a regular space curve $\vec{r}(t)$. State clearly the role of arc-length parameterization in this derivation. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Unit Tangent Vector** — From the definition of directional derivative along a curve:

$$\vec{T}(t) = \frac{\vec{r}\,'(t)}{\vert\,\vec{r}\,'(t)\,\vert}$$

Derivation: $\vec{r}\,'(t)$ points in the direction of motion but its magnitude depends on the speed of parameterization. Dividing by its magnitude produces a **unit vector** with the same direction but speed-independent. This is essential because the curve's geometry should not depend on how $t$ was chosen. **[2 marks]**

**(ii) Principal Normal Vector** — Differentiating $\vec{T}$ with respect to arc length:

$$\vec{N}(t) = \frac{d\vec{T}/ds}{\vert\,d\vec{T}/ds\,\vert}$$

The chain rule gives $\dfrac{d\vec{T}}{ds} = \dfrac{d\vec{T}/dt}{\vert \vec{r}\,'(t) \vert}$. The derivative of the unit tangent captures **how fast the direction is changing** — i.e., the bending rate. Its direction is the inward normal to the bend. Normalizing gives the unit principal normal. **[2 marks]**

**(iii) Binormal Vector** — Defined by cross product:

$$\vec{B}(t) = \vec{T}(t) \times \vec{N}(t)$$

Since $\vec{T}$ and $\vec{N}$ are unit and orthogonal, $\vec{B}$ is automatically unit, orthogonal to both, and obeys the right-hand rule. $\vec{B}$ defines the orientation of the **osculating plane** at the point. **[2 marks]**

**(iv) Role of arc-length parameterization** — Arc length $s = \displaystyle\int_a^t \vert \vec{r}\,'(u) \vert\, du$ is the *intrinsic* parameter of the curve, independent of any arbitrary choice of $t$. Differentiating with respect to $s$ removes speed bias, so $\kappa = \vert d\vec{T}/ds \vert$ becomes a purely geometric quantity measuring bending. Without arc-length parameterization, the computed "curvature" would depend on how the curve was parameterized — a meaningless artifact. **[1 mark]**

---

**(b)** For the space curve $\vec{r}(t) = (3\cos t,\ 3\sin t,\ 4t)$, compute:
- (i) the unit tangent vector $\vec{T}(t)$,
- (ii) the curvature $\kappa$,
- (iii) the torsion $\tau$.

Comment on the geometric nature of this curve. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Tangent Vector:**

$$\vec{r}\,'(t) = (-3\sin t,\ 3\cos t,\ 4)$$

$$\vert \vec{r}\,'(t) \vert = \sqrt{9\sin^2 t + 9\cos^2 t + 16} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$$

$$\boxed{\vec{T}(t) = \frac{1}{5}(-3\sin t,\ 3\cos t,\ 4)} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**(ii) Curvature:**

$$\vec{r}\,''(t) = (-3\cos t,\ -3\sin t,\ 0)$$

$$\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ -3\sin t & 3\cos t & 4 \\ -3\cos t & -3\sin t & 0 \end{vmatrix}$$

$$= \hat{i}\,(3\cos t \cdot 0 - 4\cdot(-3\sin t)) - \hat{j}\,((-3\sin t)\cdot 0 - 4\cdot(-3\cos t)) + \hat{k}\,((-3\sin t)(-3\sin t) - (3\cos t)(-3\cos t))$$

$$= \hat{i}\,(12\sin t) - \hat{j}\,(12\cos t) + \hat{k}\,(9\sin^2 t + 9\cos^2 t)$$

$$= (12\sin t,\ -12\cos t,\ 9)$$

$$\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert = \sqrt{144\sin^2 t + 144\cos^2 t + 81} = \sqrt{144 + 81} = \sqrt{225} = 15$$

$$\kappa = \frac{15}{5^3} = \frac{15}{125} = \boxed{\frac{3}{25}} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**(iii) Torsion:**

$$\vec{r}\,\,'''(t) = (3\sin t,\ -3\cos t,\ 0)$$

$$(\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'') \cdot \vec{r}\,\,''' = (12\sin t)(3\sin t) + (-12\cos t)(-3\cos t) + (9)(0)$$

$$= 36\sin^2 t + 36\cos^2 t = 36$$

$$\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert^2 = 15^2 = 225$$

$$\tau = \frac{36}{225} = \boxed{\frac{4}{25}} \quad \textbf{[2 marks]}$$

**(iv) Geometric Nature:** Since both $\kappa$ and $\tau$ are **constants independent of $t$**, the curve is a **circular helix** of radius $a = 3$ wound around the $z$-axis with pitch $b = 4$. The constant curvature confirms uniform bending (constant circular projection in the $xy$-plane), and constant torsion confirms uniform twist per unit parameter. This curve is the geometric model for **VR spiral menus, corkscrew weapon trails, and helical particle paths**. **[1 mark]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Cubic Bezier Curve** representation in detail. Derive its tangent vectors at the endpoints and discuss its **convex hull property** with relevance to AR/VR collision detection. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Definition** — A cubic Bezier curve with control points $P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3 \in \mathbb{R}^3$ is:

$$\vec{B}(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t\, P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 P_2 + t^3 P_3, \quad t \in [0,1]$$

It is a parametric polynomial curve of degree 3 in $t$, with $P_0, P_3$ as endpoints and $P_1, P_2$ as "handle" points that shape the curve without lying on it (unless $t \in \{0,1\}$). **[2 marks]**

**(ii) Tangent derivation** — Expanding the derivative:

$$\vec{B}\,'(t) = -3(1-t)^2 P_0 + 3(1-t)(1-3t) P_1 + 3t(2-3t) P_2 + 3t^2 P_3$$

At $t = 0$: $\vec{B}\,'(0) = -3P_0 + 3P_1 = 3(P_1 - P_0)$.
At $t = 1$: $\vec{B}\,'(1) = 3P_3 - 3P_2 = 3(P_3 - P_2)$.

**Geometric meaning:** The tangent at $P_0$ is parallel to the handle $\overrightarrow{P_0 P_1}$; the tangent at $P_3$ is parallel to $\overrightarrow{P_2 P_3}$. VR designers use this to "drag" handles and reshape the curve with intuitive feedback. **[2 marks]**

**(iii) Convex Hull Property** — The curve is contained inside the convex hull of $\{P_0, P_1, P_2, P_3\}$. **Proof sketch:** Rewrite $\vec{B}(t)$ as $\sum_{i=0}^3 B_i(t) P_i$ where the Bernstein polynomials $B_i(t) = \binom{3}{i}(1-t)^{3-i} t^i$ satisfy $B_i(t) \geq 0$ for $t \in [0,1]$ and $\sum_i B_i(t) = 1$. A non-negative-coefficient convex combination of points lies inside their convex hull. ✓ **[2 marks]**

**(iv) AR/VR relevance** — For collision detection of Bezier-driven projectiles, VR gestures, or animated UI trajectories, the convex hull provides a **cheap broad-phase bounding volume**: if a moving object (e.g., VR controller) does not intersect the convex hull, it cannot intersect the curve. This dramatically reduces per-frame collision checks. **[1 mark]**

---

**(b)** Explain the **Catmull–Rom spline**. Show the matrix form for one segment. Discuss why Catmull–Rom is the **de-facto standard for VR camera flythroughs**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Concept** — A Catmull–Rom spline is a **piecewise cubic interpolating curve** that passes through every control point $P_i$ and uses neighboring points $P_{i-1}$ and $P_{i+1}$ to estimate the tangent at each $P_i$. Given keyframes (waypoints), it produces a smooth path without requiring explicit tangent specification by the designer. **[2 marks]**

**(ii) Matrix Form** — For segment $i$ over $t \in [0,1]$:

$$\vec{C}(t) = \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & t & t^2 & t^3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 2 & -5 & 4 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 & -3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} P_{i-1} \\ P_i \\ P_{i+1} \\ P_{i+2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Derivation of endpoint tangents:**
- At $t = 0$: $\vec{C}(0) = P_i$ ✓ (interpolation)
- $\vec{C}\,'(0) = \frac{1}{2}(P_{i+1} - P_{i-1})$ — central-difference tangent estimate.
- At $t = 1$: $\vec{C}(1) = P_{i+1}$ ✓ (interpolation)
- $\vec{C}\,'(1) = \frac{1}{2}(P_{i+2} - P_i)$

So the tangent at each waypoint is the chord through its neighbors, scaled by $\tfrac{1}{2}$. This is why designers "just place keyframes" — the spline automatically produces a smooth $C^1$-continuous path. **[3 marks]**

**(iii) Why Catmull–Rom dominates VR flythroughs** **[2 marks]:**

| Reason | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Interpolation** | Curve passes through every waypoint — designers place the camera exactly where they want it |
| **No tangent handles** | Unlike Bezier, no need to drag extra control vectors |
| **$C^1$ continuity** | Velocities match at segment joins → smooth camera motion, no jitter |
| **Local support** | Adjusting one keyframe only affects nearby segments |
| **Engine-native** | Unreal's `USplineComponent`, Unity Cinemachine, and Blender all use Catmull–Rom |

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in 3D Space Curve Problems — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam.**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit-vector normalization step.** Writing $\vec{T} = \vec{r}\,'(t)$ instead of $\vec{T} = \vec{r}\,'(t) / \vert \vec{r}\,'(t) \vert$ costs you 1 mark instantly. The tangent *must* be a unit vector for downstream Frenet formulas to be valid.
>
> 2. **Confusing arc-length parameterization with the parameter $t$.** Curvature must be computed via $d\vec{T}/ds$, not $d\vec{T}/dt$. If you forget the $\tfrac{1}{\vert \vec{r}\,' \vert}$ correction, your curvature will be off by a factor of $\vert \vec{r}\,' \vert^2$.
>
> 3. **Dropping a sign or component in cross products.** The Frenet–Serret derivations involve 3D cross products — sign errors propagate. Double-check by verifying $\vec{T}\cdot\vec{N} = 0$ and $\vert \vec{T} \vert = 1$ at the end.
>
> 4. **Treating Bezier and Catmull–Rom as identical.** They are *not*. Bezier is an **approximating** curve (only endpoints lie on it); Catmull–Rom is an **interpolating** curve (all keyframes lie on it). KTU examiners test this distinction directly.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "engineering relevance" line.** A complete 14-mark answer must conclude with a sentence on real-world AR/VR usage. Omitting this loses 1–2 marks on KTU's CO-aligned rubric.
>
> 6. **Writing $|\vec{r}\,'|$ with vertical bars inside a markdown table** — in your answer sheet, use $\lvert \vec{r}\,' \rvert$ or $\mid \vec{r}\,' \mid$ for vertical bars to avoid LaTeX rendering ambiguity.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print or save this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- ✅ A **3D space curve** is $\vec{r}(t) = (x(t), y(t), z(t))$; it is **regular** if $\vec{r}\,'(t) \neq \vec{0}$ everywhere.
- ✅ The **Frenet–Serret frame** consists of three unit orthogonal vectors: **T**angent, **N**ormal, **B**inormal — and obeys $\vec{B} = \vec{T} \times \vec{N}$ (right-hand rule).
- ✅ **Curvature** $\kappa = \dfrac{\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert}{\vert \vec{r}\,' \vert^3}$ measures *bending sharpness*.
- ✅ **Torsion** $\tau = \dfrac{(\vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'')\cdot \vec{r}\,'''}{\vert \vec{r}\,' \times \vec{r}\,'' \vert^2}$ measures *out-of-plane twist*.
- ✅ Frenet–Serret differential system: $\dfrac{d\vec{T}}{ds} = \kappa\vec{N}$, $\dfrac{d\vec{N}}{ds} = -\kappa\vec{T} + \tau\vec{B}$, $\dfrac{d\vec{B}}{ds} = -\tau\vec{N}$.
- ✅ **Circular helix** has *constant* $\kappa$ and $\tau$. Standard example: $\vec{r}(t) = (a\cos t, a\sin t, bt)$.
- ✅ **Arc-length reparameterization** converts any curve into a unit-speed curve — the #1 VR cybersickness mitigation.
- ✅ **Cubic Bezier** $\vec{B}(t) = (1-t)^3 P_0 + 3(1-t)^2 t P_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 P_2 + t^3 P_3$ — **approximating**, has **convex hull property**, tangent at $P_0$ is $3(P_1 - P_0)$.
- ✅ **B-spline** — piecewise polynomial with **local support** via Cox–de Boor recursion; ideal for long complex paths.
- ✅ **Catmull–Rom** — **interpolating** cubic, passes through every keyframe, tangent at $P_i$ is $\tfrac{1}{2}(P_{i+1} - P_{i-1})$ — the **VR flythrough standard**.
- ✅ **Hermite curve** — specified by endpoints and tangent vectors; bridges Bezier and Catmull–Rom mathematically.
- ✅ Adaptive VR tessellation is **curvature-driven**: high $\kappa$ → denser mesh.
- ✅ Frenet frame $\rightarrow$ 3×3 matrix per vertex → used for VR camera orientation, ribbon mesh extrusion, particle billboard axes.
- ✅ Always verify orthonormal frame: $\vert \vec{T} \vert = \vert \vec{N} \vert = \vert \vec{B} \vert = 1$ and $\vec{T}\cdot\vec{N} = \vec{T}\cdot\vec{B} = \vec{N}\cdot\vec{B} = 0$.
- ✅ AR/VR exam tip: Every derivation should end with **one sentence** on AR/VR application to secure full CO marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3D Boundary Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Boundary Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 3D Boundary Representation (B-Rep)
