- If $p_k < 0$ → $t_{\text{enter}} = \max(t_{\text{enter}}, q_k/p_k)$.
- If $p_k > 0$ → $t_{\text{exit}} = \min(t_{\text{exit}}, q_k/p_k)$.
- If $t_{\text{enter}} > t_{\text{exit}}$ → reject.

Otherwise, the clipped segment lies at $t \in [\max(0, t_{\text{enter}}), \min(1, t_{\text{exit}})]$.

---

#### (b) Compare 2D and 3D clipping algorithms with respect to computational complexity, planes handled, and VR/AR pipeline usage. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | 2D Clipping | 3D Clipping |
|---|---|---|
| Bounding Region | Rectangle (4 planes) | Cuboid / Frustum (6 planes) |
| Outcode Bits | 4 bits | 6 bits |
| Trivial Tests | OR, AND on 4-bit codes | OR, AND on 6-bit codes |
| Liang–Barsky Coefficients | 4 pairs $(p_k, q_k)$ | 6 pairs $(p_k, q_k)$ |
| Max Subdivisions (Cohen–Sutherland) | Up to 4 | Up to 6 |
| Best-case Time | $O(1)$ (trivial accept/reject) | $O(1)$ |
| Worst-case Time | $O(n)$ for $n$ lines | $O(n)$ but with constant 6 instead of 4 |
| VR Pipeline Use | Screen-space culling | Frustum culling + HMD field-of-view limiting |
| AR Pipeline Use | 2D plane masking | Floor/tabletop plane detection in 3D |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** 3D clipping extends 2D clipping by adding two additional planes (Near and Far) and a third dimension in the outcode. The asymptotic complexity is identical, but the constant factor increases proportionally. In VR/AR, 3D clipping is essential for **frustum culling** before stereoscopic projection.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Canonical View Volume:** $-1 \le x, y, z \le +1$ (KTU 2024 standard).
- **Six Clipping Planes:** Left, Right, Bottom, Top, Near, Far.
- **Cohen–Sutherland Outcode:** 6-bit code; OR test = trivial accept; AND test = trivial reject.
- **Intersection Parameter:** $t = (b - c_1) / \Delta c$ where $b$ is the boundary value.
- **Boundary inclusivity:** Points exactly on a plane have outcode bit = 0 (inside).
- **Liang–Barsky advantage:** Single sweep using $t_{\text{enter}}, t_{\text{exit}}$ — faster than Cohen–Sutherland.
- **Parallel-to-plane edge case:** If $p_k = 0$ and $q_k < 0$ in Liang–Barsky → reject the entire line.
- **Cyrus–Beck generalization:** Works for arbitrary convex polyhedra (not just canonical cuboid) — relevant for portal culling in VR.
- **VR pipeline order:** Model → View → **3D Clipping** → Projection → Viewport → Rasterization.
- **Performance:** Clipping must execute at 90–120 Hz to keep VR latency under 20 ms.
- **Common trap:** Always check the boundary inclusivity convention ($-1 \le \text{inside} \le +1$ vs. $0 \le z \le 1$).
- **Bit order to memorize:** TOP, BOTTOM, RIGHT, LEFT, NEAR, FAR → bits 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 0.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Illumination Models
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 💡 Illumination Models

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Illumination Models

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📖 Formal Academic Definition

An **Illumination Model** (also called a **Lighting Model** or **Reflection Model**) is a mathematical framework used in computer graphics and virtual reality to compute the color and intensity of light reflected from a 3D surface toward the viewer. It approximates the interaction between light sources, surface material properties, and the observer's viewpoint to produce realistic shading of virtual objects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Illumination Models are foundational to the rendering pipeline of any VR/AR system. The two classical models are **Phong Illumination Model** (local illumination) and **Whitted Ray Tracing** (global illumination).

---

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a room with a single bright torch (flashlight):

- **Ambient Light** → The faint glow that fills the room even where the torch doesn't directly shine (like reflected light from walls).
- **Diffuse Reflection** → A matte painting on the wall that looks equally bright from any angle you look at it (like chalk on a blackboard).
- **Specular Reflection** → A shiny mirror where you see a bright "hotspot" only when looking from the right angle.
- **Combined (Phong) Model** → A realistic plastic ball that has a soft overall glow, a uniformly bright body, AND a shiny highlight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An illumination model is essentially the **mathematical recipe** a GPU uses to decide, for every single pixel: *"How bright should this point be, and what color should it have?"*

---

### 🔑 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **N** | Surface Normal Vector (perpendicular to surface) |
| **L** | Light Direction Vector (from surface toward light source) |
| **V** | View Direction Vector (from surface toward eye/camera) |
| **R** | Reflection Vector (mirror reflection of L about N) |
| **H** | Halfway Vector (between V and L) — used in Blinn-Phong |
| **I_L** | Intensity of Light Source |
| **k_a, k_d, k_s** | Ambient, Diffuse, Specular Reflection Coefficients (**0 ≤ k ≤ 1**) |
| **n (shininess)** | Specular exponent — controls the size of the highlight |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 Components of Illumination

An illumination model is decomposed into three physically meaningful light contributions:

1. **Ambient Light (`I_ambient`)** — Background illumination. Light bounces multiple times before hitting the surface from all directions.
   - It has **no direction** and **no shadow**.
   - Acts as a "floor" of brightness so dark regions aren't pitch black.

2. **Diffuse Reflection (`I_diffuse`)** — Light scattered equally in all directions (Lambertian surface).
   - Depends only on the angle between **N** and **L**.
   - A surface looks brightest when light hits it head-on (`N·L = 1`) and darkest at grazing angles.

3. **Specular Reflection (`I_specular`)** — Mirror-like highlight.
   - Depends on the angle between **R** and **V** (Phong) or **N** and **H** (Blinn-Phong).
   - The shininess exponent **n** controls highlight tightness: low n = broad/dull, high n = sharp/mirror-like.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why combine these?** Real materials exhibit all three behaviors simultaneously. The Phong model linearly sums them because light contributions are approximately additive in physically based rendering (PBR).

---

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Component | Formula | Range / Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | **Ambient Term** | $I_a = k_a \cdot I_L$ | $k_a \in [0,1]$, dimensionless |
| 2 | **Diffuse Term** (Lambert's Cosine Law) | $I_d = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})$ | $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L} \geq 0$ |
| 3 | **Specular Term** (Phong) | $I_s = k_s \cdot I_L \cdot (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V})^n$ | $n \in [1, 200]$, integer |
| 4 | **Specular Term** (Blinn-Phong) | $I_s = k_s \cdot I_L \cdot (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{H})^n$ | Often faster to compute |
| 5 | **Reflection Vector** | $\mathbf{R} = 2(\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - \mathbf{L}$ | Unit vector |
| 6 | **Halfway Vector** | $\mathbf{H} = \frac{\mathbf{L} + \mathbf{V}}{\vert \mathbf{L} + \mathbf{V} \vert}$ | Unit vector |
| 7 | **Combined Phong Model** (single light) | $I = k_a I_a + I_L \left[ k_d(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}) + k_s(\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n \right]$ | Final pixel intensity |
| 8 | **Multiple Lights** | $I = k_a I_a + \sum_{i=1}^{m} I_{L_i}\left[k_d(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}_i) + k_s(\mathbf{R}_i\cdot\mathbf{V})^{n}\right]$ | Sum over $m$ lights |
| 9 | **Lambert's Cosine Law** | $I_{received} = I_{incident} \cdot \cos\theta = I_{incident}(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})$ | $\theta$ = angle between N & L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** The dot product **N · L** must be **clamped to [0, 1]** (or max(0, N·L)) because if the light is behind the surface, the diffuse term would otherwise produce negative intensity — physically meaningless.

---

### 🏗️ Engineering Utility

- **VR/AR Headsets:** Real-time illumination is critical for visual comfort and depth perception.
- **Medical Visualization:** Surgeons use illuminated 3D organ models for pre-operative planning.
- **Automotive & Aerospace CAD:** Simulating how light interacts with car paint or aircraft surfaces.
- **Gaming Industry:** Every modern GPU shader (HLSL/GLSL) implements a variant of Phong/Blinn-Phong.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 📐 Derivation 1: Lambert's Cosine Law (Diffuse Reflection)

**Assumption:** A perfect diffuse (Lambertian) surface scatters incoming light equally in all directions above the surface.

**Step 1 — Define the incoming light flux on a surface patch of area $A$.**

Let the surface normal be **N** and the light direction be **L** (unit vectors). The angle between them is $\theta$.

**Step 2 — The effective (projected) area receiving light.**

Light "sees" only the projected area, which is $A \cdot \cos\theta$.

**Step 3 — Intensity is power per unit area.**

$$
I_{received} = \frac{P}{A \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by the incoming intensity $I_L$.**

Since $\cos\theta = \mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L}$ (for unit vectors):

$$
I_d = I_L \cdot \cos\theta = I_L \cdot (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by material coefficient $k_d$.**

$$
\boxed{I_d = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})}
$$

> **Logic Note:** When $\theta = 0°$ (light perpendicular to surface), $\cos\theta = 1$, maximum brightness. When $\theta = 90°$, light grazes the surface, brightness → 0.

---

### 📐 Derivation 2: Reflection Vector **R**

**Geometric setup:** Given incoming light direction **L** (pointing toward the light, from the surface), find **R** (the perfect mirror reflection of -L about N).

**Step 1 — Decompose -L into normal and tangential components.**

The component along **N** is $(\mathbf{N} \cdot (-\mathbf{L}))\mathbf{N} = -(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N}$.

The tangential component is $-\mathbf{L} - (-(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N}) = -\mathbf{L} + (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N}$.

**Step 2 — Reflect by negating the normal component.**

$$
\mathbf{R} = -\big[-(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N}\big] + \big[-\mathbf{L} + (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N}\big]
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
\mathbf{R} = (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - \mathbf{L} + (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{R} = 2(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - \mathbf{L}}
$$

---

### 📐 Derivation 3: Complete Phong Illumination Model

**Step 1 — Start with the three additive light contributions.**

$$
I_{total} = I_{ambient} + I_{diffuse} + I_{specular}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the individual term formulas.**

$$
I_{total} = k_a I_a + k_d I_L (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}) + k_s I_L (\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n
$$

**Step 3 — Group the light-dependent terms under $I_L$.**

$$
I_{total} = k_a I_a + I_L \left[ k_d (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}) + k_s (\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to $m$ light sources (linear superposition).**

$$
\boxed{I_{total} = k_a I_a + \sum_{i=1}^{m} I_{L_i}\left[k_d (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}_i) + k_s (\mathbf{R}_i\cdot\mathbf{V})^n\right]}
$$

> **Logic Note:** Linearity is an approximation. In reality, light interaction is more complex (energy conservation, BRDFs), but for real-time VR, Phong is computationally efficient and visually convincing.

---

### 📊 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** Given:
- $\mathbf{N} = (0, 0, 1)$, $\mathbf{L} = (0.5, 0, 0.866)$, $\mathbf{V} = (0, 0, 1)$
- $k_a = 0.2, k_d = 0.6, k_s = 0.4, n = 20$
- $I_a = 0.3, I_L = 1.0$

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L}$.**

$$
\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L} = (0)(0.5) + (0)(0) + (1)(0.866) = 0.866
$$

**Step 2 — Compute reflection vector R.**

$$
\mathbf{R} = 2(0.866)(0,0,1) - (0.5, 0, 0.866) = (1.732, 0, 2) - (0.5, 0, 0.866)
$$

$$
\mathbf{R} = (1.232, 0, 1.134)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V}$.**

$$
\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V} = (1.232)(0) + (0)(0) + (1.134)(1) = 1.134
$$

> Since dot products of unit vectors are bounded by 1, we treat $1.134 \rightarrow 1.0$ (clamped) — this happens because the vectors weren't normalized. After normalization, $\mathbf{R} \approx (0.736, 0, 0.677)$, giving $\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V} = 0.677$.

**Step 4 — Compute $(\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n$.**

$$
(0.677)^{20} \approx 0.00156
$$

**Step 5 — Sum all three terms.**

$$
I = (0.2)(0.3) + (1.0)\big[(0.6)(0.866) + (0.4)(0.00156)\big]
$$

$$
I = 0.06 + 0.5196 + 0.000624 \approx 0.580
$$

$$
\boxed{I \approx 0.580}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Block Diagram: Phong Illumination Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Geometry: Vertex/Pixel Data] --> B[Compute Surface Normal N]
    B --> C[Compute Light Vector L]
    C --> D{Compute N . L}
    D -->|Yes| E[Diffuse Term: kd * IL * N.L]
    D -->|No, N.L less than 0| F[Set Diffuse = 0]
    E --> G[Compute Reflection R]
    G --> H[Compute View Vector V]
    H --> I{Compute R . V to power n}
    I -->|Yes| J[Specular Term: ks * IL * R.V to power n]
    I -->|No| K[Set Specular = 0]
    F --> L[Ambient Term: ka * Ia]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Sum All Components]
    M --> N[Final Pixel Color]
```

---

### �️ Diagram 2: Vector Relationships on a Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Light Vector L] -->|incoming| S((Surface Point))
    S -->|N| N[Surface Normal N]
    V[View Vector V] -->|eye direction| S
    S -->|mirrored direction| R[Reflection Vector R]
    THETA[Angle theta between N and L] -.-> S
    ALPHA[Angle alpha between R and V] -.-> S
```

---

### 🗂️ Diagram 3: Shading Strategy Comparison (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shading Methods] --> B[Flat Shading]
    A --> C[Gouraud Shading]
    A --> D[Phong Shading]
    B --> B1[One normal per polygon face]
    B1 --> B2[Constant color across face]
    C --> C1[Interpolate color across face]
    C1 --> C2[Normal computed per vertex]
    D --> D1[Interpolate normals across face]
    D1 --> D2[Illumination computed per pixel]
    D2 --> D3[Highest quality, most expensive]
```

---

### 📊 Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a VR Rendering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SceneGraph["3D Scene Graph (Virtual World)"]
        OBJ1[Polygonal Objects]
        MAT[Material Properties: ka, kd, ks, n]
        LT[Light Sources: positions, colors, intensity]
    end

    subgraph GeometryStage["Geometry Processing Stage"]
        TRANS[Model-View-Projection Transform]
        CLIP[Clipping and Culling]
        RAST[Rasterization]
    end

    subgraph ShadingStage["Per-Pixel Shading Stage"]
        NORM[Normal Interpolation]
        LIGHT[Light Vector Computation]
        PHONG[Phong Illumination Model]
        COLOR[Final Pixel Color Output]
    end

    OBJ1 --> TRANS
    MAT --> PHONG
    LT --> LIGHT
    TRANS --> CLIP --> RAST --> NORM --> LIGHT --> PHONG --> COLOR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The scene graph provides all the inputs (geometry, materials, lights) to the shader. The Phong model sits at the end of the pipeline, just before the pixel is written to the framebuffer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the Phong Illumination Model. List its three components.**

**Model Answer:**

The Phong Illumination Model, proposed by Bui Tuong Phong in 1975, is a phenomenological model that approximates the shading of a 3D surface by computing the light intensity reflected from a point on the surface to the viewer.

It has **three components**:

1. **Ambient Light** — constant background illumination: $I_a = k_a I_a$.
2. **Diffuse Reflection** — Lambertian scattering: $I_d = k_d I_L (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})$.
3. **Specular Reflection** — view-dependent highlight: $I_s = k_s I_L (\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n$.

These are added linearly to produce the final pixel intensity.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Distinguish between Gouraud Shading and Phong Shading.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Gouraud Shading | Phong Shading |
|--------|-----------------|---------------|
| Computation Location | At **vertices** only | At **every pixel** |
| Interpolated Quantity | **Color** (intensity) | **Normal vector** |
| Illumination Calculation | Once per vertex | Once per pixel |
| Quality | Smoother but can miss highlights | Sharper, captures specular highlights accurately |
| Speed | Faster | Slower (more expensive) |
| Visual Artifact | Mach band effect possible | No Mach bands |

---

### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Phong Illumination Model** mathematically. Explain the significance of each term with appropriate diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given $\mathbf{N} = (0, 1, 0)$, $\mathbf{L} = (0.6, 0.8, 0)$, $\mathbf{V} = (0, 1, 0)$, $k_a = 0.1, k_d = 0.7, k_s = 0.5, n = 50$, $I_a = 0.2, I_L = 1.0$. Calculate the total intensity at the point. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### 📘 Model Solution (a)

The Phong Illumination Model is derived by **linearly combining** three physical light contributions:

$$
I = I_{ambient} + I_{diffuse} + I_{specular}
$$

**Step 1 — Ambient Term.** Approximates constant background illumination from multiple indirect bounces:

$$
I_{ambient} = k_a \cdot I_a
$$

**Step 2 — Diffuse Term.** Lambert's cosine law states that a perfect matte surface scatters light equally; brightness depends on the cosine of angle $\theta$ between **N** and **L**:

$$
I_{diffuse} = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})
$$

**Step 3 — Specular Term.** Mirror-like highlight depends on cosine of angle $\alpha$ between reflection vector **R** and view vector **V**, raised to power $n$ to control highlight sharpness:

$$
I_{specular} = k_s \cdot I_L \cdot (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V})^n
$$

**Step 4 — Combined equation.**

$$
\boxed{I = k_a I_a + I_L\left[k_d(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}) + k_s(\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n\right]}
$$

**Significance:**
- $k_a, k_d, k_s \in [0,1]$ — material reflectance properties.
- $n$ controls highlight sharpness (e.g., $n=10$ for plastic, $n=100+$ for metal).
- Multiple lights are summed via superposition.
- The model is local — it ignores inter-object reflections.

---

##### 📘 Model Solution (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L}$.**

$$
\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L} = (0)(0.6) + (1)(0.8) + (0)(0) = 0.8
$$

**Step 2 — Compute reflection vector R using $\mathbf{R} = 2(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - \mathbf{L}$.**

$$
\mathbf{R} = 2(0.8)(0,1,0) - (0.6, 0.8, 0) = (0, 1.6, 0) - (0.6, 0.8, 0)
$$

$$
\mathbf{R} = (-0.6, 0.8, 0)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V}$.**

$$
\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V} = (-0.6)(0) + (0.8)(1) + (0)(0) = 0.8
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n = (0.8)^{50}$.**

$$
(0.8)^{50} \approx 1.43 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0
$$

**Step 5 — Sum all three terms.**

$$
I = (0.1)(0.2) + (1.0)\left[(0.7)(0.8) + (0.5)(0)\right]
$$

$$
I = 0.02 + 0.56 + 0 = 0.58
$$

$$
\boxed{I \approx 0.58}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **Ambient, Diffuse, and Specular Reflection** with diagrams. How do they combine in the Phong model? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Blinn-Phong Illumination Model**. How is it different from the original Phong Model? Why is it preferred for real-time graphics? **(7 Marks)**

---

##### 📘 Model Solution (a)

**Ambient Reflection:** Light arriving from all directions due to multiple inter-reflections in the environment. It adds a uniform "floor" brightness to all surfaces, preventing pitch-black shadows.

- **Diagram:** Arrows pointing toward surface from **all directions** (hemisphere).
- **Formula:** $I_a = k_a I_a$.

**Diffuse Reflection:** Light scattered equally in all outgoing directions above the surface (Lambertian). Brightness depends only on **N · L**.

- **Diagram:** Arrows radiating outward from the surface point in a **hemisphere**.
- **Formula:** $I_d = k_d I_L (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L})$.

**Specular Reflection:** Light reflected in a preferred direction (mirror-like). Brightness depends on **R · V**.

- **Diagram:** A single dominant ray along **R**, brightest when **V ≈ R**.
- **Formula:** $I_s = k_s I_L (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V})^n$.

**Combination in Phong Model:**

$$
\boxed{I = k_a I_a + k_d I_L (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}) + k_s I_L (\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n}
$$

The three are **linearly summed** because light contributions are approximately independent in real-time rendering.

---

##### 📘 Model Solution (b)

**Blinn-Phong Model** (James Blinn, 1977) modifies the specular component by using the **halfway vector H** instead of the reflection vector R.

**Halfway Vector:**

$$
\mathbf{H} = \frac{\mathbf{L} + \mathbf{V}}{\vert \mathbf{L} + \mathbf{V} \vert}
$$

**Blinn-Phong Specular Term:**

$$
I_s = k_s \cdot I_L \cdot (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{H})^n
$$

**Combined Blinn-Phong Equation:**

$$
I = k_a I_a + I_L \left[ k_d (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}) + k_s (\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{H})^n \right]
$$

**Differences from Phong:**

| Aspect | Phong | Blinn-Phong |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| Specular uses | $\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V}$ | $\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{H}$ |
| Computes reflection vector | Yes (extra cost) | No (faster) |
| Accuracy | Physically intuitive | Equally plausible visually |

**Why Preferred for Real-Time Graphics:**
1. **Computational Efficiency:** No need to compute **R** (saves a vector reflection per pixel).
2. **GPU Friendly:** $\mathbf{H}$ is straightforward to compute in shaders.
3. **Used as Default** in OpenGL fixed-function pipeline and many shader tutorials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Forgetting to clamp $(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L}) \geq 0$ — this produces negative intensities when light is behind the surface (impossible physically).
> 2. **Second common mistake:** Not normalizing vectors before taking dot products — leads to nonsensical results.
> 3. **Third common mistake:** Confusing $\mathbf{L}$ direction — in some textbooks **L** points *from light to surface* (then we use $-\mathbf{L}$ in formulas). KTU convention is **L** = surface → light.
> 4. **Examiner Tip:** Always state the units/range of $k_a, k_d, k_s$ as dimensionless reflectance coefficients in $[0, 1]$. Marks are deducted for vague statements.
> 5. **Skip-penalty:** In derivations, if a student writes only the final equation without showing decomposition steps, expect **partial credit only** (~4 out of 7).

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Phong Model** = Ambient + Diffuse + Specular, all linearly combined.
- 🎯 **Lambert's Law:** Diffuse brightness $\propto \cos\theta$ (clamped to $[0,1]$).
- 🎯 **Specular exponent $n$:** Higher = sharper, more mirror-like highlight.
- 🎯 **Reflection vector:** $\mathbf{R} = 2(\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L})\mathbf{N} - \mathbf{L}$.
- 🎯 **Blinn-Phong** uses **halfway vector $\mathbf{H}$** — faster, GPU-friendly.
- 🎯 **Gouraud shading** = per-vertex color interpolation; **Phong shading** = per-pixel normal interpolation (best quality).
- 🎯 **Local vs Global illumination:** Phong is *local* (ignores inter-object reflections); Radiosity/Ray Tracing are *global*.
- 🎯 **Always clamp** $\mathbf{N}\cdot\mathbf{L} \geq 0$ and $(\mathbf{R}\cdot\mathbf{V})^n \geq 0$ before summing.
- 🎯 **Multiple lights** are handled by linear superposition (sum over $m$ light sources).
- 🎯 **KTU favorite question patterns:** (i) Derive Phong formula, (ii) Compare Gouraud vs Phong shading, (iii) Numerical calculation with given vectors.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"If a derivation is asked, show every step. If a numerical is asked, clamp the dot products. If a comparison is asked, use a table."* ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflection Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reflection Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Reflection Model?

A **Reflection Model** (also called an *illumination model* or *shading model*) is a mathematical formulation that describes the way light interacts with a surface to determine its final visible color, intensity, and appearance. In the context of **Augmented Reality (AR)** and **Virtual Reality (VR)**, reflection models are the cornerstone of **physically-based rendering (PBR)** because they dictate how virtual objects reflect, absorb, and scatter light from real-world illumination sources, ensuring seamless blending of virtual content with the real environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A reflection model is a set of equations that approximates the **Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF)** of a surface, computing the outgoing radiance $L_o$ at a surface point as a function of the incoming light direction $\vec{\omega}_i$, outgoing view direction $\vec{\omega}_o$, surface normal $\vec{n}$, and material properties (albedo, roughness, metalness, index of refraction).

Mathematically, a reflection model computes the intensity $I$ of light leaving a surface point as:

$$I = I_{\text{ambient}} + I_{\text{diffuse}} + I_{\text{specular}}$$

This decomposition is the foundation of nearly every classical shading model.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rain on a Marble Floor" Intuition

Imagine walking into a room with two distinct floors:

1. **A rough, unfinished concrete floor** — When sunlight hits it, the light scatters equally in all directions. No matter where you stand, the floor looks *uniformly bright*. This is **diffuse reflection** (Lambertian).
2. **A polished marble floor with a thin layer of water** — At certain angles, you see a bright, sharp *highlight* of the window. Move your head, and the highlight *glides* across the surface. This is **specular reflection**.
3. **The faint gray glow** in the darkest corners, where no direct light reaches, is **ambient light** — the soft bounce-light filling the shadows.

A reflection model simply *quantifies* these three physical phenomena so a GPU can compute them millions of times per frame in a VR scene.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Reflection Models Matter in AR/VR:**
> - **Visual Realism**: Without accurate reflections, virtual objects look "stickered" onto the real world.
> - **Geometric Consistency**: Specular highlights inform users about surface curvature, material identity (metal vs. plastic), and lighting direction.
> - **Latency Budget**: A VR headset runs at **90 Hz** (or higher) — reflection models must be evaluated for every pixel within a strict **11.1 ms** frame budget.

---

## 1.3 The Three Primary Physical Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Physical Description | Typical Materials | Directional Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diffuse Reflection** | Light penetrates the surface, scatters internally, and re-emerges uniformly | Matte paint, chalk, unfinished wood | View-independent (Lambertian) |
| **Specular Reflection** | Light reflects off the outer surface layer with angle of incidence = angle of reflection | Polished metal, mirror, wet plastic | View-dependent; produces highlights |
| **Ambient / Global Illumination** | Indirect light from all other surfaces in the environment | All surfaces (soft fill light) | Approximated as a constant |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PECPT634 Module 2 places special emphasis on the **Phong, Blinn-Phong, and Cook-Torrance** reflection models because they are the most commonly implemented in real-time VR engines (Unity HDRP, Unreal Engine 5, OpenXR).

---

## 1.4 The Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF)

At the heart of every modern reflection model lies the **BRDF**, $f_r(\vec{\omega}_i, \vec{\omega}_o)$:

$$f_r(\vec{\omega}_i, \vec{\omega}_o) = \frac{dL_o(\vec{\omega}_o)}{dE_i(\vec{\omega}_i)} \quad \left[\frac{1}{\text{sr}}\right]$$

where:
- $dL_o$ is the differential outgoing radiance (W·sr⁻¹·m⁻²)
- $dE_i$ is the differential incoming irradiance (W·m⁻²)
- $\vec{\omega}_i$ = incoming light direction, $\vec{\omega}_o$ = outgoing view direction
- **sr** = steradian (the SI unit of solid angle)

A valid BRDF must obey two physical laws:
1. **Helmholtz Reciprocity**: $f_r(\vec{\omega}_i, \vec{\omega}_o) = f_r(\vec{\omega}_o, \vec{\omega}_i)$
2. **Energy Conservation**: The total reflected energy $\leq$ total incoming energy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Lambertian (Diffuse) Reflection Model

**Johann Heinrich Lambert** (1760) proposed that a perfectly matte surface reflects light *equally in all directions* of the upper hemisphere. The intensity depends only on the angle between the light direction and the surface normal.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **A surface element** $dA$ receives light from a source of intensity $I_L$ at direction $\vec{L}$.
2. **The effective area** that intercepts the light is $dA \cdot \cos\theta = dA \cdot (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the normal $\vec{n}$ and light vector $\vec{L}$.
3. **The reflected energy** is spread uniformly across the hemisphere (Lambert's cosine law).
4. **Therefore**, the observed intensity from any view direction is proportional to $\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}$.

### Key Equations

$$I_{\text{diffuse}} = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})$$

where:
- $k_d \in [0, 1]$ = **diffuse reflectance coefficient** (material's albedo color)
- $I_L$ = **light source intensity** (radiance)
- $(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) = \cos\theta$ = dot product of normal and light vectors
- The model is clamped: $\max(0, \vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})$ to avoid negative values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Lambertian is "View-Independent"**: Because $I_{\text{diffuse}}$ contains *no* view vector $\vec{V}$, a Lambertian sphere looks uniformly bright across its entire visible hemisphere — exactly like a ping-pong ball under a desk lamp.

---

## 2.2 The Phong Reflection Model

**Bui Tuong Phong** (1973, University of Utah) extended Lambert's model with a **view-dependent specular term** to simulate shiny highlights. The full Phong model is the sum of three components:

$$I_{\text{Phong}} = I_{\text{ambient}} + I_{\text{diffuse}} + I_{\text{specular}}$$

### Component Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 Ambient Component
$$I_{\text{ambient}} = k_a \cdot I_a$$
- $k_a$ = ambient reflection coefficient
- $I_a$ = ambient light intensity
- Approximates the soft, global fill light bouncing off all surfaces.

#### 2.2.2 Diffuse Component (Lambertian)
$$I_{\text{diffuse}} = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot \max(0, \vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})$$

#### 2.2.3 Specular Component
$$I_{\text{specular}} = k_s \cdot I_L \cdot \max(0, \cos\alpha)^n$$
where $\alpha$ is the angle between the **perfect reflection vector $\vec{R}$** and the **view vector $\vec{V}$**:

$$\vec{R} = 2(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})\vec{n} - \vec{L}$$

$$\cos\alpha = \vec{R} \cdot \vec{V}$$

- $k_s$ = specular reflection coefficient
- $n$ = **shininess exponent** (typical values: 1–256)
- Higher $n$ → tighter, sharper highlight (mirror-like)
- Lower $n$ → broad, soft highlight (plastic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility**: The Phong model powers the default shaders in legacy OpenGL fixed-function pipelines and is still taught as the *pedagogical introduction* to shading. In VR, Phong shading is used for stylized/non-photorealistic rendering (cel-shading) in games like *The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild*.

---

## 2.3 The Blinn-Phong Reflection Model (The Modern Standard)

**James F. Blinn** (1977) modified Phong's specular term to use a **half-vector $\vec{H}$** instead of the reflection vector $\vec{R}$. This was not just cosmetic — it made the model **physically more plausible** and computationally faster.

### The Half Vector
$$\vec{H} = \frac{\vec{L} + \vec{V}}{\|\vec{L} + \vec{V}\|}$$

### Blinn-Phong Specular Term
$$I_{\text{specular}} = k_s \cdot I_L \cdot \max(0, \vec{n} \cdot \vec{H})^n$$

### Why Blinn-Phong is Better

1. **No reflection vector computation** — saves 1 dot product + vector subtraction per pixel.
2. **More physically realistic highlights** — the half-vector aligns with the microfacet normal (central concept in microfacet theory).
3. **Stable at grazing angles** — Phong's $\vec{R} \cdot \vec{V}$ can become undefined when $\vec{V} = \vec{R}$ at silhouette edges.

---

## 2.4 The Cook-Torrance Reflection Model (Microfacet Theory)

**Robert Cook and Kenneth Torrance** (1982) introduced a model grounded in **physical optics** rather than empirical fitting. The surface is modeled as a collection of microscopic mirrors (microfacets), each with its own normal $\vec{m}$.

### Cook-Torrance Specular BRDF

$$f_r^{\text{spec}} = \frac{D(\vec{h}) \cdot G(\vec{n}, \vec{L}, \vec{V}) \cdot F(\vec{V}, \vec{h})}{4 \cdot (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \cdot (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{V})}$$

The three crucial functions:

| Function | Name | Physical Meaning | Typical Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D(\vec{h})$ | **Normal Distribution Function (NDF)** | Fraction of microfacets aligned with half-vector | GGX / Trowbridge-Reitz |
| $G(\vec{n}, \vec{L}, \vec{V})$ | **Geometric Attenuation / Shadowing-Masking** | Probability that light reaches and leaves a microfacet without being blocked | Smith-Schlick |
| $F(\vec{V}, \vec{h})$ | **Fresnel Reflectance** | Fraction of light reflected vs. refracted at the surface | Schlick approximation |

### The Full Cook-Torrance Rendering Equation

$$L_o(\vec{\omega}_o) = \int_{\Omega} \left[ k_d \frac{c}{\pi} + \frac{D \cdot G \cdot F}{4 (\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{V})} \right] L_i(\vec{\omega}_i) (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{\omega}_i) d\vec{\omega}_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **VR/AR Relevance**: The Cook-Torrance model (especially GGX + Smith + Schlick) is the *de facto* standard in **Unity's High Definition Render Pipeline (HDRP)** and **Unreal Engine 5's Lumen**, which power commercial VR titles like *Half-Life: Alyx*.

---

## 2.5 The Fresnel Effect

The **Fresnel equations** (Augustin-Jean Fresnel, 1821) describe how the reflectance of a surface changes with the **angle of incidence**. Counterintuitively, *all* materials become more mirror-like at grazing angles — even chalk!

### Schlick's Approximation (1994)

$$F(\theta) = F_0 + (1 - F_0)(1 - \cos\theta)^5$$

where:
- $\theta$ = angle between view direction and surface normal
- $F_0$ = reflectance at normal incidence (0°)
  - **Non-metals (dielectrics)**: $F_0 \approx 0.04$ (4%)
  - **Metals (conductors)**: $F_0$ = colored (e.g., gold: $(1.0, 0.71, 0.29)$)

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET** — Memorize this table before every KTU exam. It contains every reflection-model equation you will be asked to derive or apply.

| # | Model | Equation | Key Parameters | Physical Basis |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lambertian Diffuse** | $I_d = k_d I_L (\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})$ | $k_d, I_L$ | Energy conservation |
| 2 | **Ambient Term** | $I_a = k_a I_a$ | $k_a$ | Global fill light |
| 3 | **Phong Specular** | $I_s = k_s I_L (\vec{R}\cdot\vec{V})^n$ | $k_s, n, \vec{R}$ | Empirical (1973) |
| 4 | **Reflection Vector** | $\vec{R} = 2(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})\vec{n} - \vec{L}$ | $\vec{n}, \vec{L}$ | Snell's law |
| 5 | **Half Vector** | $\vec{H} = \frac{\vec{L}+\vec{V}}{\vert\vec{L}+\vec{V}\vert}$ | $\vec{L}, \vec{V}$ | Blinn's optimization |
| 6 | **Blinn-Phong Specular** | $I_s = k_s I_L (\vec{n}\cdot\vec{H})^n$ | $k_s, n$ | Empirical (1977) |
| 7 | **Cook-Torrance BRDF** | $f_r = \frac{DGF}{4(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{V})}$ | $D, G, F$ | Microfacet theory |
| 8 | **GGX NDF** | $D(\vec{h}) = \frac{\alpha^2}{\pi(\cos^2\theta_h(\alpha^2-1)+1)^2}$ | $\alpha = \text{roughness}^2$ | Trowbridge-Reitz |
| 9 | **Schlick Fresnel** | $F(\theta) = F_0 + (1-F_0)(1-\cos\theta)^5$ | $F_0$ | Approx. of full Fresnel |
| 10 | **BRDF Definition** | $f_r = \frac{dL_o}{dE_i}$ | $L_o, E_i$ | Units: sr⁻¹ |
| 11 | **Rendering Equation** | $L_o = \int_{\Omega} f_r L_i (\vec{n}\cdot\vec{\omega}_i) d\omega_i$ | $f_r, L_i$ | Kajiya (1986) |
| 12 | **Lambert's Cosine Law** | $E = I \cos\theta$ | $E, I, \theta$ | Irradiance falloff |

**Standard Material Reference Values:**

| Material | $F_0$ (Linear RGB) | Typical Roughness $\alpha$ | Model Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water | 0.02 | 0.00–0.05 | Dielectric |
| Glass | 0.04 | 0.00–0.10 | Dielectric |
| Plastic | 0.04–0.05 | 0.30–0.70 | Dielectric |
| Wood | 0.04 | 0.50–0.90 | Dielectric |
| Iron | (0.56, 0.57, 0.58) | 0.20–0.60 | Conductor |
| Gold | (1.00, 0.71, 0.29) | 0.00–0.40 | Conductor |
| Copper | (0.95, 0.64, 0.54) | 0.20–0.50 | Conductor |

---

## 2.7 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Significance

### The "Why" — Why Three Models?
- **Lambertian** handles 80% of real-world surfaces (cloth, paper, skin) and is *cheap* (1 dot product).
- **Phong/Blinn-Phong** adds view-dependent highlights needed for plastics, ceramics, polished wood.
- **Cook-Torrance** is required when the AR/VR application must be **indistinguishable from reality** (e.g., automotive configurators, architectural visualization, medical training).

### The "How" — How Light Interacts at the Nanoscale
1. Photon strikes the **macrosurface** (the polygon).
2. At the **microscale**, the photon hits a single **microfacet** with normal $\vec{m}$.
3. If $\vec{m}$ aligns with the half-vector $\vec{H}$, the photon reflects toward the viewer (specular).
4. If $\vec{m}$ is misaligned, the photon is either:
   - **Absorbed** (becomes heat → diffuse color),
   - **Refracted** (passes through for translucent materials),
   - **Scattered** (Lambertian diffuse).
5. The BRDF $f_r$ *integrates* all these microscopic possibilities into a single macroscopic color value per pixel.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams**: Think of a reflection model as a **3-stage pipeline** — *Ambient* (fill), *Diffuse* (matte), *Specular* (shiny). Every classical model = Ambient + Diffuse + Specular with different math.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lambert's Cosine Law (Irradiance Falloff)

**Goal**: Prove that the irradiance $E$ on a tilted surface equals $E = I \cos\theta$, where $I$ is the source intensity and $\theta$ is the angle of incidence.

### Given
- A point light source of luminous intensity $I$ (W/sr).
- A small surface patch of area $dA$ oriented at angle $\theta$ to the incident ray.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Solid angle subtended by the patch**

The solid angle $\d\omega$ subtended by a surface element $dA$ (whose normal is $\vec{n}$) at a distance $r$ is defined as:

$$\d\omega = \frac{\dA \cos\theta}{r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Luminous flux received**

The total flux $d\Phi$ (in watts) intercepted by the patch is the product of the source intensity and the solid angle:

$$\d\Phi = I \cdot \d\omega = \frac{I \cdot \dA \cos\theta}{r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Definition of irradiance**

Irradiance $E$ is flux per unit area:

$$E = \frac{\d\Phi}{\dA} = \frac{I \cos\theta}{r^2}$$

**Step 4 — Ignoring distance attenuation** (directional light assumption)

For a directional light (e.g., the sun, infinitely far away), $r \to \infty$, so the $\frac{1}{r^2}$ term becomes a constant $I_L$. The final Lambertian irradiance is:

$$E = I_L \cos\theta = I_L (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})$$

**Step 5 — Lambertian reflectance**

The reflected intensity from a matte surface is a fraction $k_d$ of the incident irradiance, scattered uniformly. Hence:

$$\boxed{I_{\text{diffuse}} = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Phong Reflection Vector

**Goal**: Derive the formula $\vec{R} = 2(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})\vec{n} - \vec{L}$ for the perfect mirror reflection direction.

### Given
- Incident light direction $\vec{L}$ (pointing *from* the surface *toward* the light).
- Surface normal $\vec{n}$ (unit vector).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Decompose the incident vector**

Split $\vec{L}$ into components parallel ($\vec{L}_\parallel$) and perpendicular ($\vec{L}_\perp$) to $\vec{n}$:

$$\vec{L} = \vec{L}_\parallel + \vec{L}_\perp$$

where:
- $\vec{L}_\parallel = (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n}$ (component along $\vec{n}$)
- $\vec{L}_\perp = \vec{L} - (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n}$ (component tangent to surface)

**Step 2 — Apply the law of reflection**

The reflection vector $\vec{R}$ flips the parallel component and keeps the perpendicular component:

$$\vec{R} = -\vec{L}_\parallel + \vec{L}_\perp$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the decomposition**

$$\vec{R} = -(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n} + \vec{L} - (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n}$$

$$\vec{R} = \vec{L} - 2(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n}$$

**Step 4 — Conventional sign**

In computer graphics, $\vec{L}$ typically points *toward* the light (outward from surface), so the reflection vector pointing back outward is:

$$\boxed{\vec{R} = 2(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n} - \vec{L}}$$

**Verification**: If $\vec{L} = (0, 1, 0)$ and $\vec{n} = (0, 1, 0)$ (light straight down on a flat floor), then $\vec{R} = 2(1)(0,1,0) - (0,1,0) = (0,1,0)$ — the light reflects straight back up. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Blinn-Phong Half Vector

**Goal**: Show that the half-vector $\vec{H}$ is the normalized sum of $\vec{L}$ and $\vec{V}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Definition of the half vector**

$$\vec{H} = \frac{\vec{L} + \vec{V}}{\vert \vec{L} + \vec{V} \vert}$$

**Step 2 — Why does this approximate the reflection vector?**

The reflection vector satisfies $\vec{R} = 2(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})\vec{n} - \vec{L}$. When the surface is smooth and the view is close to mirror direction, $\vec{n} \approx \vec{H}$, so:

$$\vec{n} \cdot \vec{H} \approx \vec{R} \cdot \vec{V}$$

This is the **key insight** of Blinn's 1977 paper: the half-vector dot product gives the same highlight as the reflection-vector dot product, but is *cheaper* to compute.

**Step 3 — Cost comparison**

| Operation | Phong | Blinn-Phong |
|---|---|---|
| Compute $\vec{R}$ | 1 dot + 1 vector sub | None |
| Specular evaluation | $\vec{R} \cdot \vec{V}$ | $\vec{n} \cdot \vec{H}$ |
| Total per pixel | 2 dot products | 1 dot product |

This **50% reduction** in per-pixel cost was revolutionary in 1977 and still matters for mobile VR (Oculus Quest).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Schlick Fresnel Approximation

**Goal**: Derive the Schlick approximation from the full Fresnel equations.

### Step 1 — Full Fresnel reflectance for dielectrics

For unpolarized light incident from vacuum (n₁ = 1) onto a dielectric (n₂ = n), the full Fresnel equations give:

$$F(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\left[F_\parallel^2 + F_\perp^2 \right]$$

where:
$$F_\perp = \frac{\cos\theta_i - n \cos\theta_t}{\cos\theta_i + n \cos\theta_t}, \quad F_\parallel = \frac{n\cos\theta_i - \cos\theta_t}{n\cos\theta_i + \cos\theta_t}$$

with Snell's law: $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$.

### Step 2 — Series expansion at grazing angles

Christophe Schlick (1994) observed that $F(\theta)$ has a characteristic shape: it equals $F_0$ at $\theta = 0$ and rises to 1 at $\theta = \pi/2$. A polynomial of degree 5 fits this curve with **less than 1% error** across the entire range $[0, \pi/2]$.

### Step 3 — The Schlick formula

$$\boxed{F(\theta) = F_0 + (1 - F_0)(1 - \cos\theta)^5}$$

**Verification at endpoints**:
- At $\theta = 0$: $F(0) = F_0 + 0 = F_0$ ✓
- At $\theta = \pi/2$: $F(\pi/2) = F_0 + (1-F_0)(1)^5 = 1$ ✓ (total reflection at grazing)

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Cook-Torrance Microfacet BRDF

**Goal**: Derive the Cook-Torrance specular BRDF formula from first principles.

### Step 1 — Energy balance per microfacet

Consider a single microfacet with normal $\vec{m}$. For light to be reflected from $\vec{L}$ to $\vec{V}$:
- The microfacet normal must equal the half-vector: $\vec{m} = \vec{H} = \frac{\vec{L}+\vec{V}}{\vert \vec{L}+\vec{V}\vert}$.

### Step 2 — Projected area of microfacets

A microfacet with true area $dA_m$ projects to an area $dA_m (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{m})$ on the macrosurface. The total projected area of microfacets aligned with $\vec{H}$ is governed by the **Normal Distribution Function** $D(\vec{h})$:

$$D(\vec{h}) = \frac{1}{\vert \vec{n} \cdot \vec{h}\vert} \cdot \frac{dN}{dA}$$

where $dN$ is the number of microfacets with normals in the cone $d\omega_h$ around $\vec{h}$.

### Step 3 — Geometric shadowing and masking

A microfacet may be **shadowed** (blocked from the light) or **masked** (blocked from the view). The function $G(\vec{n}, \vec{L}, \vec{V})$ gives the fraction of microfacets that are *visible* to both. The factor:

$$\frac{G(\vec{n}, \vec{L}, \vec{V})}{(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{V})}$$

corrects for this visibility loss in the projected area.

### Step 4 — Fresnel reflection at the microfacet

Each microfacet reflects light according to the Fresnel equations. The function $F(\vec{V}, \vec{h})$ captures this.

### Step 5 — Combining all factors

The reflected radiance from the microfacet, projected back to the macrosurface, gives the Cook-Torrance BRDF:

$$\boxed{f_r^{\text{spec}}(\vec{L}, \vec{V}) = \frac{D(\vec{h}) \cdot G(\vec{n}, \vec{L}, \vec{V}) \cdot F(\vec{V}, \vec{h})}{4 \cdot (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \cdot (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{V})}}$$

The full reflected radiance from a directional light source is:

$$L_o = \int_{\Omega} f_r(\vec{L}, \vec{V}) \cdot L_i(\vec{L}) \cdot (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \, d\vec{L}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Blinn-Phong on a Tilted Surface

**Problem**: A point on a surface has normal $\vec{n} = (0, 0.6, 0.8)$. The light direction is $\vec{L} = (0, 0.8, 0.6)$ and the view direction is $\vec{V} = (0, 0, 1)$. The light intensity $I_L = 1.0$, $k_d = 0.7$, $k_s = 0.3$, shininess $n = 50$. Compute the Blinn-Phong intensity. (Take ambient $I_a = 0.1$, $k_a = 0.2$.)

### Solution

**Step 1 — Verify unit vectors**
- $\vert\vec{n}\vert = \sqrt{0 + 0.36 + 0.64} = 1.0$ ✓
- $\vert\vec{L}\vert = \sqrt{0 + 0.64 + 0.36} = 1.0$ ✓
- $\vert\vec{V}\vert = 1.0$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute ambient term**
$$I_a = k_a I_a = 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.02$$

**Step 3 — Compute diffuse term**
$$\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L} = (0)(0) + (0.6)(0.8) + (0.8)(0.6) = 0.48 + 0.48 = 0.96$$
$$I_d = k_d I_L (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) = 0.7 \times 1.0 \times 0.96 = 0.672$$

**Step 4 — Compute half vector**
$$\vec{L} + \vec{V} = (0, 0.8, 1.6)$$
$$\vert\vec{L} + \vec{V}\vert = \sqrt{0 + 0.64 + 2.56} = \sqrt{3.2} \approx 1.789$$
$$\vec{H} = (0, 0.447, 0.894)$$

**Step 5 — Compute specular term**
$$\vec{n} \cdot \vec{H} = (0)(0) + (0.6)(0.447) + (0.8)(0.894) = 0.268 + 0.715 = 0.983$$
$$I_s = k_s I_L (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{H})^n = 0.3 \times 1.0 \times (0.983)^{50}$$

Since $0.983^{50} \approx e^{50 \times \ln(0.983)} \approx e^{50 \times (-0.01715)} \approx e^{-0.858} \approx 0.424$

$$I_s = 0.3 \times 0.424 = 0.127$$

**Step 6 — Total intensity**
$$\boxed{I_{\text{total}} = I_a + I_d + I_s = 0.02 + 0.672 + 0.127 = 0.819}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note**: In KTU exams, always (1) show that vectors are unit length, (2) state the clamping $\max(0, \vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})$, and (3) round to 3 decimal places.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection Guide

| Use Case | Recommended Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Stylized VR game** (low-poly) | Lambertian + Phong | Fast, simple, artistic look |
| **Photoreal VR** (architectural viz) | Cook-Torrance (GGX) | Physically accurate |
| **Mobile AR** (smartphone) | Blinn-Phong | Low computational cost |
| **Headset passthrough AR** (Quest 3) | Cook-Torrance + IBL | Must match real-world reflections |
| **Holographic display** | Full Fresnel + dispersion | Spectral accuracy required |
| **Training simulator** (medical) | Cook-Torrance + subsurface scattering | Tissue realism |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Pipeline — Reflection Model Evaluation Flow

The following block diagram maps the *entire* sequence of operations a GPU fragment shader performs for every pixel when evaluating a reflection model.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fragment Shader Invocation] --> B[Fetch Surface Normal n]
    B --> C[Fetch View Vector V]
    C --> D[Fetch Light Vector L]
    D --> E[Fetch Material Properties kd, ks, n, F0, roughness]
    E --> F{Compute Half Vector H}
    F --> G[Evaluate Ambient Term: Ia = ka x Ia]
    G --> H[Evaluate Diffuse Term: Id = kd x IL x max0, n.L]
    H --> I[Evaluate Specular Term]
    I --> J{Model Type}
    J -->|Phong| K[Is = ks x IL x max0, R.V^n]
    J -->|Blinn-Phong| L[Is = ks x IL x max0, n.H^n]
    J -->|Cook-Torrance| M[Is = D.G.F / 4(n.L)(n.V)]
    K --> N[Sum Components: I = Ia + Id + Is]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Apply Tone Mapping and Gamma Correction]
    O --> P[Write to Framebuffer]
    P --> Q[Present to VR Headset Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**: Each box is a GPU operation. In a modern VR pipeline running at 90 Hz, this entire graph executes for **~2.07 million pixels per frame** (1440 × 1600 × 2 eyes), giving ~186 million evaluations per second.

---

## 4.2 Microfacet Model Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MAC[Macrosurface]
        N[Surface Normal n]
    end
    subgraph MIC[Microscopic Facets]
        M1[Microfacet 1 with normal m1]
        M2[Microfacet 2 with normal m2]
        M3[Microfacet 3 with normal m3 aligned to H]
        M4[Microfacet 4 with normal m4]
    end
    L[Incoming Light L] --> MIC
    MIC --> V[Reflected Ray V]
    N -.guides.-> MIC
    H[Half Vector H] -.selects.-> M3
    M3 ==> V
    style M3 fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#ff6600,stroke-width:3px
    style H fill:#00ccff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

**Diagram Interpretation**:
- The macroscopic surface (top) has a single normal $\vec{n}$.
- Beneath it, thousands of microfacets (bottom) each have their own normal $\vec{m}_i$.
- The half-vector $\vec{H}$ *selects* which microfacets contribute to the specular highlight (highlighted in orange).
- Microfacets not aligned with $\vec{H}$ scatter light into the diffuse term or absorb it.

## 4.4 Fresnel Angle Diagram — Reflectance vs. Incidence

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Light Ray Strikes Surface] --> Angle{Angle of Incidence theta}
    Angle -->|theta = 0 degrees| Normal[F0 = 4 percent reflectance]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Cook-Torrance Function Dependencies

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (BRDF). State the two physical laws that any valid BRDF must obey.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The **BRDF** $f_r(\vec{\omega}_i, \vec{\omega}_o)$ is defined as the ratio of the outgoing radiance $L_o$ in direction $\vec{\omega}_o$ to the differential incoming irradiance $E_i$ from direction $\vec{\omega}_i$:

$$f_r(\vec{\omega}_i, \vec{\omega}_o) = \frac{dL_o(\vec{\omega}_o)}{dE_i(\vec{\omega}_i)} \quad \text{(units: sr}^{-1}\text{)}$$

The two physical laws are:
1. **Helmholtz Reciprocity** (1.5 marks): $f_r(\vec{\omega}_i, \vec{\omega}_o) = f_r(\vec{\omega}_o, \vec{\omega}_i)$ — the BRDF is symmetric with respect to swapping the incident and outgoing directions.
2. **Energy Conservation** (1 mark): The total reflected energy cannot exceed the incident energy, i.e., the integral of $f_r$ over the upper hemisphere is $\leq 1$.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Scheme**: 1 mark for definition, 0.5 marks for units, 1 mark for Helmholtz, 0.5 marks for energy conservation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the Phong and Blinn-Phong specular reflection models with one suitable diagram.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Feature | Phong (1973) | Blinn-Phong (1977) |
|---|---|---|
| **Reflection vector** | $\vec{R} = 2(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})\vec{n} - \vec{L}$ | Not required |
| **Half vector** | Not used | $\vec{H} = \frac{\vec{L}+\vec{V}}{\vert\vec{L}+\vec{V}\vert}$ |
| **Specular term** | $k_s(\vec{R}\cdot\vec{V})^n$ | $k_s(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{H})^n$ |
| **Cost per pixel** | 2 dot products | 1 dot product |
| **Physical basis** | Empirical | Approximates microfacet normal |

**Diagram**:

```mermaid
graph LR
    L[Light L] --> R[Compute R = 2 n.L n - L]
    R --> RDot[Compute R.V^n]
    RDot --> P[Phong: I_s = ks x I_L x R.V^n]
    L2[Light L] --> V[View V]
    L2 --> H[Compute H = L+V normalized]
    V --> H
    H --> HDot[Compute n.H^n]
    HDot --> BP[Blinn-Phong: I_s = ks x I_L x n.H^n]
    style P fill:#ffe6cc
    style BP fill:#cce6ff
```

> [!TIP]

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**a)** Explain the **Lambertian (diffuse) reflection model** with its mathematical formulation. Derive the formula $I_d = k_d I_L (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})$ from the definition of solid angle and irradiance. **[7 Marks]**

**b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Cook-Torrance microfacet reflection model**. Explain the role of the Normal Distribution Function (NDF) $D$, Geometric Attenuation $G$, and Fresnel term $F$ in the BRDF. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Lambertian Diffuse Model (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Lambertian model assumes a surface scatters incident light *uniformly in all directions* of the upper hemisphere. The observed intensity depends only on the angle between the light direction $\vec{L}$ and the surface normal $\vec{n}$.

**Mathematical Formulation (1 mark):**
$$I_d = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot \max(0, \vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})$$

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a small surface patch of area $dA$ at distance $r$ from a point light source of intensity $I$.

**Step 1** — The solid angle subtended by the patch is:
$$\d\omega = \frac{\dA \cos\theta}{r^2}$$
where $\theta$ is the angle between the patch normal and the light direction.

**Step 2** — The luminous flux intercepted is:
$$\d\Phi = I \cdot \d\omega = \frac{I \cdot \dA \cos\theta}{r^2}$$

**Step 3** — The irradiance (flux per unit area) is:
$$E = \frac{\d\Phi}{\dA} = \frac{I \cos\theta}{r^2}$$

**Step 4** — For a directional light ($r \to \infty$), the $\frac{1}{r^2}$ term collapses into a constant $I_L$:
$$E = I_L \cos\theta = I_L (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})$$

**Step 5** — The reflected intensity from a matte surface is a fraction $k_d$ of the incident irradiance:
$$I_d = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties to State**: (i) View-independent, (ii) $\max(0, \cdot)$ clamping, (iii) Energy conservation: $\int_{\Omega} (\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L}) d\omega = \pi$, so the diffuse albedo $k_d$ must be divided by $\pi$ in energy-conserving formulations.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Cook-Torrance Model (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[L, V, n, roughness, F0] --> D[D: NDF]
    In --> G[G: Shadowing-Masking]
    In --> F[F: Fresnel]
    D --> Mul[Multiply D x G x F]
    G --> Mul
    F --> Mul
    Mul --> Div[Divide by 4 n.L n.V]
    Div --> Out[Cook-Torrance BRDF]
```

**Mathematical Formulation (1 mark):**
$$f_r^{\text{spec}} = \frac{D(\vec{h}) \cdot G(\vec{n}, \vec{L}, \vec{V}) \cdot F(\vec{V}, \vec{h})}{4 (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{V})}$$

**Role of D — Normal Distribution Function (1.5 marks):**
- $D(\vec{h})$ represents the probability density of microfacets whose normals are aligned with the half-vector $\vec{h}$.
- Higher $D$ value → more microfacets reflect light toward the viewer → brighter highlight.
- Common form: **GGX / Trowbridge-Reitz**: $D(\vec{h}) = \frac{\alpha^2}{\pi (\cos^2\theta_h(\alpha^2-1) + 1)^2}$ where $\alpha = \text{roughness}^2$.

**Role of G — Geometric Attenuation (1.5 marks):**
- $G$ accounts for microfacets that are **shadowed** (light cannot reach them) or **masked** (reflected light cannot escape to the viewer).
- Common form: **Smith-Schlick**: $G(\vec{n}, \vec{L}, \vec{V}) = G_1(\vec{n}, \vec{L}) \cdot G_1(\vec{n}, \vec{V})$.
- As roughness increases, $G$ decreases, fading the highlight.

**Role of F — Fresnel Reflectance (1 mark):**
- $F$ describes the fraction of light reflected at the surface interface (vs. refracted into the material).
- **Schlick approximation**: $F(\theta) = F_0 + (1 - F_0)(1 - \cos\theta)^5$.
- For metals, $F_0$ is colored (e.g., gold = (1.0, 0.71, 0.29)).
- For dielectrics, $F_0 \approx 0.04$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**a)** With a neat diagram, derive the formula for the **Phong specular reflection vector** $\vec{R} = 2(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})\vec{n} - \vec{L}$. Write the complete Phong reflection model equation. **[7 Marks]**

**b)** Explain the **Fresnel effect** in reflection models. Derive the **Schlick approximation** of the Fresnel reflectance and discuss its role in the Cook-Torrance BRDF. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — Phong Specular Model (7 Marks)

**Diagrammatic Setup (1 mark):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    N[Surface Normal n] --> Base[Surface]
    L[Incident Light L] --> P[Point P on surface]
    P --> R[Reflected Ray R]
    V[View Vector V]
    style N fill:#ffcccc
    style L fill:#ccffcc
    style R fill:#ccccff
    style V fill:#ffccff
```

**Derivation of Reflection Vector (4 marks):**

**Step 1** — Decompose the incident light vector into components parallel and perpendicular to the normal:
$$\vec{L} = \vec{L}_\parallel + \vec{L}_\perp$$
where:
- $\vec{L}_\parallel = (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n}$
- $\vec{L}_\perp = \vec{L} - (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n}$

**Step 2** — Apply the law of reflection: the parallel component reverses, the perpendicular component is preserved:
$$\vec{R} = -\vec{L}_\parallel + \vec{L}_\perp$$

**Step 3** — Substitute:
$$\vec{R} = -(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n} + \vec{L} - (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n}$$
$$\vec{R} = \vec{L} - 2(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n}$$

**Step 4** — In computer graphics, $\vec{L}$ points *toward* the light, so the outward reflection vector is:
$$\boxed{\vec{R} = 2(\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) \vec{n} - \vec{L}}$$

**Complete Phong Model Equation (2 marks):**
$$I_{\text{Phong}} = \underbrace{k_a I_a}_{\text{ambient}} + \underbrace{k_d I_L (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L})}_{\text{diffuse}} + \underbrace{k_s I_L (\vec{R} \cdot \vec{V})^n}_{\text{specular}}$$

**Definitions**:
- $k_a, k_d, k_s$ = ambient, diffuse, specular reflection coefficients
- $n$ = shininess exponent (typically 1 to 256)
- $\vec{R} \cdot \vec{V} = \cos\alpha$ where $\alpha$ is the angle between reflection and view vectors

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — Fresnel Effect & Schlick Approximation (7 Marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (2 marks):**
The **Fresnel effect** describes the angle-dependent reflectance of light at the interface between two media. The reflectance depends on:
1. The angle of incidence $\theta$
2. The index of refraction $n$ of the material
3. The polarization of the light (s-polarized $\perp$ vs. p-polarized $\parallel$)

A counterintuitive result: *all* materials become more reflective at grazing angles, regardless of their base reflectance.

**Full Fresnel Equations (2 marks):**
For unpolarized light incident from medium 1 (refractive index $n_1$) into medium 2 (refractive index $n_2$):
$$F(\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\left[F_\parallel^2 + F_\perp^2 \right]$$
where:
$$F_\perp = \frac{n_1 \cos\theta_i - n_2 \cos\theta_t}{n_1 \cos\theta_i + n_2 \cos\theta_t}, \quad F_\parallel = \frac{n_2 \cos\theta_i - n_1 \cos\theta_t}{n_2 \cos\theta_i + n_1 \cos\theta_t}$$
with Snell's law: $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$.

**Schlick Approximation (2 marks):**
Christophe Schlick (1994) approximated the full Fresnel equations with a computationally efficient 5th-degree polynomial:
$$F(\theta) = F_0 + (1 - F_0)(1 - \cos\theta)^5$$
where $F_0$ is the reflectance at normal incidence ($\theta = 0$).
- For dielectrics (non-metals): $F_0 \approx 0.04$
- For conductors (metals): $F_0$ is RGB-colored (e.g., gold: $F_0 = (1.0, 0.71, 0.29)$)

**Role in Cook-Torrance BRDF (1 mark):**
In the Cook-Torrance model, $F$ is one of the three multiplicative components:
$$f_r^{\text{spec}} = \frac{D(\vec{h}) \cdot G(\vec{n}, \vec{L}, \vec{V}) \cdot F(\vec{V}, \vec{h})}{4 (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{L}) (\vec{n} \cdot \vec{V})}$$
The Fresnel term ensures the correct energy reflectance at grazing angles, making the model physically accurate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clamp dot products** — Students often write $(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})$ without $\max(0, \cdot)$, leading to negative intensity when the light is behind the surface. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing the sign convention** for $\vec{L}$ — If $\vec{L}$ points *from* light *to* surface, the reflection formula is $\vec{R} = \vec{L} - 2(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})\vec{n}$; if $\vec{L}$ points the other way, the sign flips. State your convention! **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Wrong units on BRDF** — The BRDF has units of **sr⁻¹** (per steradian). Writing it as a dimensionless number loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up D, G, F** — Students often swap the roles of Geometric Attenuation and Normal Distribution. Remember: **D** = distribution (counting), **G** = geometry (visibility), **F** = Fresnel (energy reflectance). **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — Always state: (i) unit vectors, (ii) directional light, (iii) opaque surface. **Loss: 0.5 marks per missing assumption.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Every Exam**

### Core Definitions
- ✅ **Reflection Model**: Mathematical approximation of how light reflects off a surface; outputs pixel color/intensity.
- ✅ **BRDF**: Ratio of outgoing radiance to incoming irradiance; units = **sr⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Two Physical Laws**: Helmholtz Reciprocity + Energy Conservation.

### Three Component Decomposition
- ✅ **Ambient**: $I_a = k_a I_a$ (constant fill light).
- ✅ **Diffuse**: $I_d = k_d I_L (\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})$ (Lambertian, view-independent).
- ✅ **Specular**: $I_s = k_s I_L (\vec{R}\cdot\vec{V})^n$ (Phong) or $k_s I_L (\vec{n}\cdot\vec{H})^n$ (Blinn-Phong).

### Critical Formulas to Memorize
- ✅ **Reflection Vector**: $\vec{R} = 2(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})\vec{n} - \vec{L}$
- ✅ **Half Vector**: $\vec{H} = \frac{\vec{L}+\vec{V}}{\vert\vec{L}+\vec{V}\vert}$
- ✅ **Lambert's Cosine Law**: $E = I \cos\theta$
- ✅ **Cook-Torrance BRDF**: $f_r = \frac{D \cdot G \cdot F}{4 (\vec{n}\cdot\vec{L})(\vec{n}\cdot\vec{V})}$
- ✅ **Schlick Fresnel**: $F(\theta) = F_0 + (1-F_0)(1-\cos\theta)^5$
- ✅ **GGX NDF**: $D(\vec{h}) = \frac{\alpha^2}{\pi(\cos^2\theta_h(\alpha^2-1)+1)^2}$

### Model Evolution Timeline
- ✅ **1760**: Lambert (diffuse only)
- ✅ **1973**: Phong (added specular)
- ✅ **1977**: Blinn (half-vector optimization)
- ✅ **1982**: Cook-Torrance (microfacet theory)
- ✅ **1994**: Schlick (Fresnel approximation)

### Key Numerical Values
- ✅ Dielectric $F_0 \approx 0.04$
- ✅ Metal $F_0$ = colored (e.g., gold = (1.0, 0.71, 0.29))
- ✅ Typical shininess $n$: 1 (matte plastic) to 256 (mirror)
- ✅ VR frame budget: **11.1 ms** (90 Hz)
- ✅ Schlick exponent: **5** (5th-degree polynomial)

### Common Exam Traps
- ⚠️ Always clamp dot products with $\max(0, \cdot)$.
- ⚠️ State unit-vector assumption explicitly.
- ⚠️ Specify sign convention for $\vec{L}$.
- ⚠️ Differentiate view-dependent vs. view-independent models.
- ⚠️ Remember: BRDF units are **steradian⁻¹**, not dimensionless.

### Engineering Applications in KTU Syllabus
- 🎯 **Module 2 link**: Reflection models feed directly into **ray tracing**, **shading algorithms (Gouraud/Phong)**, and **PBR pipelines** covered later in the course.
- 🎯 **VR/AR link**: Required for **real-time rendering**, **spatial computing shaders**, and **digital twin visualization** in the lab component.
- 🎯 **Industry link**: The Cook-Torrance + Schlick + GGX trio is the *de facto* standard in **Unity HDRP**, **Unreal Engine 5**, and **NVIDIA Omniverse**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy**: Always start with the *formula*, then *define every symbol*, then *show the derivation step-by-step*, and *end with a numerical example*. This 4-step structure is what KTU examiners reward with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shading Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shading Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Shading Algorithms** are computational techniques used in computer graphics and rendering pipelines (including **Virtual Reality (VR)** scene renderers) that determine the **color and intensity of light** displayed at each pixel of a 3D surface by simulating the interaction of light sources with object geometry and material properties.

In the context of **PECPT634 (Augmented and Virtual Reality)**, shading is the bridge between **geometric representation** (vertices, polygons, normals) and the **photorealistic / perceptual output** seen through a Head-Mounted Display (HMD). Without shading, a polygonal VR scene would appear as a flat wireframe mesh.

The three classical shading algorithms mandated by the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus are:

1. **Flat Shading** (also called *Constant Shading*) — a single intensity per polygon.
2. **Gouraud Shading** (also called *Intensity Interpolation Shading*) — intensities computed at vertices and interpolated across the polygon.
3. **Phong Shading** (also called *Normal Vector Interpolation Shading*) — normals interpolated across the polygon, and intensity evaluated per pixel.

These algorithms rely on a **Local Illumination Model**, most commonly the **Phong Illumination Model** (Ambient + Diffuse + Specular components).

## 1.2 Intuition: Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 🎨 **Conceptual Analogy — "The Stained Glass Artist"**
> Imagine you are a stained-glass artist looking at a glass sculpture under a single lamp.
> - **Flat Shading** is like a child coloring a wall — the entire wall is one uniform color, with no awareness that the lamp's position changes how the wall *should* glow.
> - **Gouraud Shading** is like pre-painting the *corners* of each glass panel with the correct highlight tone, then *blending* the colors smoothly between the corners. The middle of the panel gets an interpolated mix.
> - **Phong Shading** is like computing the angle of every single microscopic glass facet on the panel to determine the precise brightness — far more realistic but computationally heavier.

In VR (where the user can move their head and viewpoint continuously at **90 Hz** or higher), the choice of shading algorithm directly affects **frame rate, latency, and visual fidelity**.

## 1.3 Key Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference values used in KTU problems:
> - **Shininess exponent (n):** typically **1 ≤ n ≤ 200** (higher = tighter, glossier highlight).
> - **Reflection coefficients:** $k_a + k_d + k_s \leq 1$ (energy conservation principle).
> - **Update Rate for VR:** typically **90 Hz or 120 Hz** (refresh rate of HMD displays).
> - **Pixel Fill Rate:** measured in **MP/s (Million Pixels per second)** — directly tied to per-pixel cost of Phong shading.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Phong Illumination Model (Foundation of All Shading)

Before shading algorithms can operate, we need a **lighting equation** that, given a surface point, computes how much light reaches the eye. The dominant local model in KTU syllabus is the **Phong Illumination Model**.

The total intensity $I$ at a point on a surface is the **sum of three contributions**:

$$I = I_{\text{ambient}} + I_{\text{diffuse}} + I_{\text{specular}}$$

### Component Breakdown

**Step 1 — Ambient Component ($I_a$):**
Represents the *background uniform illumination* (light bouncing off walls, sky).
$$I_a = k_a \cdot I_L$$
where $k_a \in [0,1]$ is the ambient reflection coefficient and $I_L$ is the source light intensity.

**Step 2 — Diffuse Component ($I_d$):**
Models the **matte / Lambertian reflection** — light scattered equally in all directions from a rough surface. Depends on the angle $\theta$ between the surface normal $N$ and the light direction $L$.
$$I_d = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot (N \cdot L)$$
Here $k_d$ is the diffuse reflection coefficient and $(N \cdot L) = \cos(\theta)$.

**Step 3 — Specular Component ($I_s$):**
Models the **shiny highlight** seen when the view direction $V$ aligns with the perfect reflection direction $R$ from the light.
$$I_s = k_s \cdot I_L \cdot (R \cdot V)^n$$
Here $k_s$ is the specular reflection coefficient, and $n$ is the *shininess exponent* controlling highlight sharpness.

**Step 4 — Reflection Vector Calculation:**
The reflection of light direction $L$ about the surface normal $N$ is computed as:
$$R = 2(N \cdot L) N - L$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 📋 **Master Reference Table** — Memorize before every KTU Exam.

| # | Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Intensity | $I = I_a + I_d + I_s$ | Sum of all light contributions (W/m²·sr) |
| 2 | Ambient | $I_a = k_a \cdot I_L$ | Background illumination |
| 3 | Diffuse (Lambertian) | $I_d = k_d \cdot I_L (N \cdot L)$ | Matte surface brightness |
| 4 | Specular (Phong) | $I_s = k_s \cdot I_L (R \cdot V)^n$ | Glossy highlight |
| 5 | Reflection Vector | $R = 2(N \cdot L)N - L$ | Mirror reflection of $L$ about $N$ |
| 6 | Halfway Vector Variant | $H = (L + V)/\vert L+V \vert$ | Used to replace $R \cdot V$ computation |
| 7 | Flat Shading Cost | $O(F)$ per face | $F$ = number of polygon faces |
| 8 | Gouraud Cost | $O(V) + O(P)$ per poly | $V$ = vertices, $P$ = pixels |
| 9 | Phong Cost | $O(P)$ per pixel | Per-pixel normal interpolation |
| 10 | VR Frame Budget | $\leq 11.1$ ms/frame | For **90 Hz** HMD refresh |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall — Vector Convention:** In the KTU textbook convention, $L$ is a vector pointing **from the surface to the light source**. Some Western textbooks use $L$ pointing *from light to surface*. Always check sign before dot products!

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VR Game Engines (Unity, Unreal Engine):** Use **per-pixel Phong shading** for high-fidelity scenes and **Gouraud** for mobile VR where GPU power is constrained.
- **AR Devices (Microsoft HoloLens, Magic Leap):** Apply flat or simplified Gouraud shading on transparent displays to preserve battery and reduce latency.
- **Medical VR Training Simulators:** Use **Phong shading with high $n$** to simulate the wet, glossy surface of human tissue accurately.
- **Automotive VR Prototyping:** Combines ambient + diffuse Phong shading to pre-visualize how light interacts with new car body designs before physical prototyping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reflection Vector $R$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU ESE** (worth 3–7 marks). Memorize each line.

**Given:** Incident light direction $L$ (unit vector) and surface normal $N$ (unit vector).

**Goal:** Find the reflected direction $R$.

**Geometric Intuition:** The reflection vector is obtained by *reflecting $L$ across $N$*. The component of $L$ perpendicular to $N$ is reversed, while the parallel component stays.

**Step-by-step:**

Let $L$ be decomposed into two components:

$$L_{\parallel} = (N \cdot L) \cdot N \quad \text{(component parallel to } N\text{)}$$

$$L_{\perp} = L - L_{\parallel} = L - (N \cdot L) \cdot N \quad \text{(component perpendicular to } N\text{)}$$

When reflecting, $L_{\parallel}$ remains unchanged and $L_{\perp}$ is negated:

$$R = L_{\parallel} - L_{\perp}$$

Substituting:

$$R = (N \cdot L) N - \bigl(L - (N \cdot L) N\bigr)$$

Expanding:

$$R = (N \cdot L) N - L + (N \cdot L) N$$

Combining like terms:

$$R = 2(N \cdot L) N - L \qquad \blacksquare$$

This is the **canonical Phong reflection vector** used in every KTU problem.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why $(R \cdot V)^n$ Models Specular Highlight

Let $\alpha$ be the angle between $R$ and $V$.

$$\cos(\alpha) = R \cdot V$$

For a perfectly smooth mirror surface, the highlight is visible **only when** $V$ exactly coincides with $R$ (i.e., $\alpha = 0$, giving $\cos(\alpha) = 1$). As $V$ moves away from $R$, brightness should drop off *rapidly*.

Raising the cosine to a power $n$ achieves this falloff:

$$\cos^n(\alpha) = (R \cdot V)^n$$

As $n \to \infty$, the highlight collapses to a single point — the mathematical ideal of a perfect mirror.

As $n \to 1$, the highlight becomes broad — modeling a dull, semi-glossy material.

This single elegant parameter $n$ is what makes the Phong model so universally used in **real-time VR rendering**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Three Shading Algorithms Compared

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 📊 This matrix is **HIGH-YIELD** — appears in nearly every KTU Module 2 exam.

| Property | Flat Shading | Gouraud Shading | Phong Shading |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Computed at** | Per polygon face | Per vertex, then interpolated | Per pixel |
| **What is interpolated?** | Nothing (constant) | Intensity (color) values | Surface normal vectors |
| **Phong model applied** | Once per face | Once per vertex | Once per pixel |
| **Highlights (Specular)** | ❌ Missed (or look faceted) | ⚠️ Possible but blurry | ✅ Sharp, accurate |
| **Mach band effect** | None | Visible artifact | Eliminated |
| **Computation cost** | **Lowest** | **Medium** | **Highest** |
| **Visual realism** | Poor (low-poly look) | Good | Excellent |
| **Best for VR use** | Mobile AR / distant LOD | Mid-tier VR | High-end PC VR |
| **KTU Exam frequency** | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Algorithm: Gouraud Shading

> [!NOTE]
> Standard KTU textbook algorithm — practice writing it from memory.

**Step 1:** For each polygon vertex $V_i$ in the scene mesh, compute the **average normal** by averaging the face normals of all polygons that share $V_i$:

$$N_{V_i} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} N_{F_j}$$

where $m$ is the number of polygons sharing $V_i$ and $N_{F_j}$ is the $j$-th face normal.

**Step 2:** Apply the Phong illumination model at each vertex:

$$I_{V_i} = k_a I_L + k_d I_L (N_{V_i} \cdot L) + k_s I_L (R_{V_i} \cdot V)^n$$

**Step 3:** For each edge of the polygon, **linearly interpolate** the intensities between the two endpoint vertices to obtain edge intensities at each scan-line $y$.

**Step 4:** Across each scan line, **linearly interpolate** between the left and right edge intensities to obtain per-pixel intensities.

> [!WARNING]
> Linear interpolation of *colors* can produce **Mach bands** (false bright/dark stripes) when specular highlights fall *inside* a polygon — a classic KTU exam question!

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Algorithm: Phong Shading

**Step 1:** Compute vertex normals $N_{V_i}$ as in Gouraud Step 1.

**Step 2:** For each edge, **linearly interpolate** the normals (not intensities!) between endpoints.

**Step 3:** Across each scan line, **linearly interpolate** the normals to obtain a per-pixel normal $N_{pixel}$.

**Step 4:** Apply the full Phong illumination model at **each pixel** using $N_{pixel}$:

$$I_{pixel} = k_a I_L + k_d I_L (N_{pixel} \cdot L) + k_s I_L (R_{pixel} \cdot V)^n$$

**Why this fixes Mach bands:** Since normals are interpolated and the Phong model is *non-linear*, the resulting intensity varies smoothly and matches the underlying physics.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> Practice problem pattern: typical KTU 3-mark question.

**Given:**
- $N = (0, 0, 1)$
- $L = (0.5, 0, 0.866)$ (unit vector toward light source)
- $V = (0, 0, 1)$ (viewer looking straight at surface)
- $k_a = 0.2$, $k_d = 0.5$, $k_s = 0.3$, $n = 5$
- $I_L = 1.0$

**Step 1: Compute Ambient.**

$$I_a = k_a \cdot I_L = 0.2 \cdot 1.0 = 0.2$$

**Step 2: Compute Diffuse.**

$$N \cdot L = (0)(0.5) + (0)(0) + (1)(0.866) = 0.866$$

$$I_d = k_d \cdot I_L \cdot (N \cdot L) = 0.5 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.866 = 0.433$$

**Step 3: Compute Reflection Vector.**

$$N \cdot L = 0.866$$

$$R = 2(0.866)(0,0,1) - (0.5, 0, 0.866) = (1.732, 0, 1.732) - (0.5, 0, 0.866)$$

$$R = (-0.5, 0, 0.866)$$

**Step 4: Compute Specular.**

$$R \cdot V = (-0.5)(0) + (0)(0) + (0.866)(1) = 0.866$$

$$(R \cdot V)^n = (0.866)^5 \approx 0.486$$

$$I_s = k_s \cdot I_L \cdot (R \cdot V)^n = 0.3 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.486 = 0.146$$

**Step 5: Total Intensity.**

$$I = 0.2 + 0.433 + 0.146 = 0.779$$

**Final Answer:** $I \approx 0.779$ (or 77.9% of source intensity).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Phong Illumination Model — Component Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Light Source IL] --> B[Phong Illumination Model]
    P[Surface Point] --> N1[Compute Normal N]
    N1 --> B
    B --> C1[Ambient Component Ia]
    B --> C2[Diffuse Component Id]
    B --> C3[Specular Component Is]
    C1 --> D[Summation I = Ia + Id + Is]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Pixel Color Output]
    L1[View Direction V] --> B
    L2[Reflection Vector R] --> B
    K[Material Coeffs ka kd ks n] --> B
```

## 4.2 Shading Algorithm Comparison Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FLAT[Flat Shading]
        F1[Polygon Vertices] --> F2[Compute Face Normal N]
        F2 --> F3[Apply Phong Once]
        F3 --> F4[Constant Color Across Polygon]
    end

    subgraph GOURAUD[Gouraud Shading]
        G1[Polygon Vertices] --> G2[Compute Vertex Normals]
        G2 --> G3[Apply Phong at Vertices]
        G3 --> G4[Interpolate Intensities]
        G4 --> G5[Per-Pixel Color by Interpolation]
    end

    subgraph PHONG[Phong Shading]
        P1[Polygon Vertices] --> P2[Compute Vertex Normals]
        P2 --> P3[Interpolate Normals Across Edges]
        P3 --> P4[Interpolate Normals Across Scanlines]
        P4 --> P5[Apply Phong Per Pixel]
    end
```

## 4.3 VR Rendering Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3D Scene Geometry] --> B[Vertex Transformation]
    B --> C[Lighting Calculation]
    C --> D{Choose Shading Method}
    D -->|Low Power| E[Flat Shading]
    D -->|Mid Power| F[Gouraud Shading]
    D -->|High Power| G[Phong Shading]
    E --> H[Rasterization]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Frame Buffer]
    I --> J[HMD Display at 90 Hz]
    J --> K[User Perception]
```

## 4.4 Mach Band Artifact Visualization (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Specular Highlight Inside Polygon] --> B[Gouraud Intensity Interpolation]
    B --> C[Linear Color Blend Across Edge]
    C --> D[Visible False Bright or Dark Stripe]
    D --> E[User Perceives Non-Existent Edge Mach Band Effect]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---
