topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Visualization Techniques for Augmented Reality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Visualization Techniques for Augmented Reality** refer to the systematic set of rendering, illumination, occlusion, and perceptual-coherence methods used to integrate computer-generated virtual content seamlessly with the user's view of the real world. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, these techniques are classified under the *Perceptual and Rendering Pipelines* of AR systems and form the bridge between **scene understanding** (Module 2) and **interaction** (Module 4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Visualization in AR is the process of generating a composite image in which virtual objects appear geometrically registered, photometrically consistent, temporally stable, and perceptually coherent with the surrounding real environment, while respecting correct occlusion, lighting, shadows, and depth ordering.*

The visualization pipeline operates after **tracking** and **registration** have estimated the camera pose. The principal sub-processes are:

| Sub-process | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Geometric Registration | Align virtual objects to real-world coordinates |
| Photometric Registration | Match color, brightness, and illumination |
| Occlusion Handling | Decide what hides what (real vs virtual) |
| Temporal Stabilization | Reduce jitter and latency artifacts |
| Style Transfer | Apply NPR (Non-Photorealistic Rendering) when needed |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **theatrical set designer** who must place a holographic elephant onto a live stage. Before the show, you:

1. Measure the stage dimensions (Tracking & Calibration).
2. Decide which actors should hide behind the elephant and which should appear in front of it (Occlusion).
3. Match the stage lights so the elephant casts a believable shadow and reflects the correct colour (Illumination Estimation).
4. Make sure the elephant does not flicker if the camera moves slightly (Temporal Stabilization).

The "visualization techniques" are the **stagecraft of AR** — the lighting, the painted backdrops, the matte paintings, and the forced perspective tricks that sell the illusion that the elephant is *really there*. Without these tricks, the holographic elephant would look like a flat cardboard cutout pasted on the screen.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in AR Visualization

- **Frame Rate (Hz):** Target ≥ **60 fps** for comfortable viewing, ≥ **90 fps** for stereoscopic HMDs.
- **End-to-End Latency (ms):** Should be ≤ **20 ms** to avoid cybersickness.
- **Field of View (deg):** Typical HMD FOV is **90° – 110°**.
- **Dynamic Range:** Must match the **human eye's ~20 stops** of dynamic range.
- **Color Gamut:** Often specified in **sRGB** or **Display-P3**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is photorealism not always the goal in AR?"* — because stylized rendering (NPR) is often **more legible**, **less distracting**, and **more aesthetic** for task-critical applications (surgery, training, navigation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of AR Visualization Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus divides visualization techniques into **five operational families**:

### A. Occlusion Handling Techniques
- **Model-based Occlusion:** Uses a pre-reconstructed 3D mesh of the real scene. Real geometry is rendered first into the depth buffer; virtual objects are then rejected where the real depth is closer.
- **Depth-from-Stereo:** Two calibrated cameras compute per-pixel disparity; the disparity map is converted to a depth map for occlusion.
- **Depth-from-Motion:** SfM (Structure from Motion) builds a sparse/ dense depth map by tracking features across frames.
- **Learned Depth (Monocular):** CNNs (e.g., MiDaS, MonoDepth2) estimate depth from a single RGB frame.

### B. Lighting Estimation & Relighting
- **Environment Map Sampling:** Detect keypoints, find their 2D–3D correspondences, retrieve the nearest HDR environment map (from a panorama dataset such as **Laval Indoor / Outdoor HDR**).
- **Differential Rendering:** Render the virtual object into a captured environment map to estimate incident illumination.
- **Sphere Harmonization:** Project environment lighting onto spherical harmonics (SH) of low degree $l \le 2$.

### C. Shadow Rendering
- **Ground Plane Shadows:** Project the silhouette of the virtual object onto a detected real ground plane.
- **Contact Shadows:** Soft, approximate shadows drawn where the virtual object "touches" the real surface.
- **Shadow Mapping:** Standard CG algorithm requiring depth from the light's POV.

### D. Non-Photorealistic Rendering (NPR)
- Cel-shading, edge detection (Sobel / Canny), cartoon ink lines.
- Used to make annotations (arrows, labels) pop without merging with photo-realistic clutter.

### E. Special Visualizations
- **X-Ray Vision:** Real objects rendered transparent so virtual internals are visible.
- **Magic Lens / Magic Mirror:** Mobile screen or wall acts as a portal.
- **Diminished Reality:** Virtual objects *remove* real ones (e.g., hide a wall).
- **Focal-Plane Adaptation:** Render with correct accommodation for HMDs.

## 2.2 Why and How Each Step Works

- **Why occlusion?** — Without correct occlusion, the brain immediately rejects the illusion (the *doughboy illusion* / the *Winslow effect*).
- **Why relighting?** — A virtual object lit with the wrong direction looks like a *sticker* pasted on the screen.
- **Why temporal stabilization?** — Sub-frame jitter (motion-to-photon delay > 15 ms) induces nausea.
- **Why NPR?** — Cognitive load: photorealism can hide important information; stylization directs attention.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ inside tables if needed; here plain pipes are only in formula bodies, not as table separators.

| # | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pinhole Projection | $x = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z},\ y = f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}$ | pixels, $f$ in mm |
| 2 | Camera Matrix (3×4) | $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{K} \, [\mathbf{R} \,\vert\, \mathbf{t}] \, \mathbf{X}$ | $\mathbf{K}$ intrinsics |
| 3 | Lens Distortion | $x_d = x (1 + k_1 r^2 + k_2 r^4)$ | $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ |
| 4 | Depth from Disparity | $Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $B$ baseline, $d$ disparity |
| 5 | Lambertian Reflectance | $I = \rho \, \mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L}$ | $\rho \in [0,1]$ albedo |
| 6 | Phong Specular | $I_{spec} = k_s (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V})^{\alpha}$ | $\alpha$ shininess |
| 7 | Rendering Equation | $L_o = L_e + \int_{\Omega} f_r L_i (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L}) d\omega$ | radiance $L$ in $W/m^2 sr$ |
| 8 | Spherical Harmonics | $L(\theta,\phi) = \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \sum_{m=-l}^{l} c_{l}^{m} Y_l^m(\theta,\phi)$ | $Y_l^m$ basis functions |
| 9 | Shadow Factor | $S(p) = \begin{cases} 0, & p \text{ in shadow} \\ 1, & p \text{ lit} \end{cases}$ | binary / PCF soft |
| 10 | Reprojection Error | $e = \sum_i \vert \mathbf{u}_i - \pi(\mathbf{X}_i) \vert^2$ | squared pixels |
| 11 | Latency Constraint | $t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{track} + t_{render} + t_{display} \le 20$ | milliseconds |
| 12 | Display FOV | $FOV = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)$ | degrees |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Surgical AR (e.g., AccuVein):** X-Ray visualization to see veins under skin.
- **Automotive HUD (e.g., Mercedes AR-HUD):** Ground-plane shadows for lane arrows.
- **Industrial Training (e.g., Boeing wiring):** Occlusion + NPR labels to teach assembly.
- **Architecture (e.g., ARKit RoomPlan):** Lighting estimation to preview furniture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth-from-Stereo Occlusion Equation

We derive the relationship between disparity and depth, which underpins depth-based occlusion handling.

### Step 1 — Setup
Two cameras with focal length $f$ and baseline $B$ view a 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$. The left and right image projections are:

$$
u_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}
$$

$$
u_R = f \cdot \frac{(X - B)}{Z}
$$

**Logic:** Each camera projects the same 3D point independently through its pinhole. The horizontal shift $B$ causes the same point to land at different pixel columns.

### Step 2 — Define Disparity
Disparity is the horizontal pixel difference:

$$
d = u_L - u_R = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= f \cdot \frac{X - (X - B)}{Z} \\[4pt]
  &= f \cdot \frac{B}{Z} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{f \, B}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The $X$ terms cancel because disparity only depends on the shift $B$, not on the lateral position $X$ in this simplified setup.

### Step 4 — Invert for Depth

$$
Z = \frac{f \, B}{d}
$$

**Logic:** Depth is inversely proportional to disparity — closer objects produce larger disparity. This is the foundation of occlusion reasoning: once $Z$ is known for each real pixel, a virtual object at $Z_v$ is occluded wherever $Z < Z_v$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lighting Estimation via Spherical Harmonics

We derive how environment lighting is compressed into SH coefficients for real-time relighting.

### Step 1 — Represent Incoming Light
The incoming radiance over the hemisphere $\Omega$ above a surface point is $L_i(\mathbf{\omega})$, a function of direction $\mathbf{\omega} = (\theta, \phi)$.

### Step 2 — Project onto SH Basis
Expand in basis functions $Y_l^m(\theta,\phi)$:

$$
L_i(\theta, \phi) = \sum_{l=0}^{\infty} \sum_{m=-l}^{l} c_l^m \, Y_l^m(\theta, \phi)
$$

Coefficients $c_l^m$ are obtained via:

$$
c_l^m = \int_{\Omega} L_i(\theta, \phi) \, Y_l^m(\theta, \phi) \, d\omega
$$

**Logic:** SH basis functions form an orthonormal set on the sphere, similar to how Fourier series decompose 1D signals.

### Step 3 — Truncate for Real-Time
For diffuse-only relighting, only the first **three** bands are needed ($l = 0, 1$). The final shading becomes:

$$
I = \rho \cdot \left( c_0^0 Y_0^0 + c_1^{-1} Y_1^{-1} + c_1^{0} Y_1^{0} + c_1^{1} Y_1^{1} \right)
$$

**Logic:** $l=0$ gives ambient, $l=1$ gives the directional gradient (sun position). Higher bands add sharp shadows and skylight structure, which are expensive.

### Step 4 — Practical Use
Apple ARKit's `ARLightEstimate` returns SH coefficients (9 coefficients for $l \le 2$). The renderer then evaluates $I$ per fragment for photometric consistency.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Technique vs. Use-Case vs. Cost

| Technique | Best Use Case | Computational Cost | Hardware Needed | Quality (1–5) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Model-Based Occlusion | Known static scene | Medium | Depth camera | 5 |
| Stereo Depth Occlusion | Outdoor mobile AR | High | Stereo camera | 4 |
| Monocular CNN Depth | Phones (single cam) | Very High (GPU) | Modern SoC | 3 |
| HDR Environment Map | Photoreal product viz | Low | Internet + GPU | 5 |
| Spherical Harmonics (l≤2) | Real-time mobile | Very Low | Any GPU | 3 |
| Ground-Plane Shadow | Navigation HUD | Very Low | Floor detector | 4 |
| Shadow Mapping | Indoor AR | Medium | Depth texture | 5 |
| Cel-Shading NPR | Annotations / Education | Very Low | None | 4 |
| X-Ray Vision | Medical / Training | Medium | None | 5 |
| Diminished Reality | Interior design | Very High | DNN inpainter | 4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU exam tip: When asked to "compare techniques," always quote the trade-off across **at least three axes** (quality, latency, hardware cost) — this is the marking scheme expectation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AR Visualization Pipeline (End-to-End Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Camera Frame Capture] --> B[Tracking Module]
    B --> C[Camera Pose Estimation]
    B --> D[Scene Reconstruction]
    C --> E[Virtual Object Placement]
    D --> F[Depth / Occlusion Map]
    E --> G[Rendering Engine]
    F --> G
    H[Lighting Estimation HDR] --> I[SH Coefficient Computation]
    I --> G
    G --> J[Shadow Generation]
    J --> K[Compositing Real + Virtual]
    K --> L[Temporal Stabilization ASwW]
    L --> M[Final Display Frame]
    M --> N[User HMD or Screen]
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- **A** ingests raw RGB at ≥ 60 Hz.
- **B / C** deliver a 6-DoF pose.
- **D / F** build a depth map for occlusion reasoning.
- **G** is the heart — shaded virtual geometry.
- **H / I** ensure photometric match with the real world.
- **J** adds soft ground shadows.
- **K** composites real RGB over virtual pixels using the depth test.
- **L** applies Asynchronous Space Warp (ASW) / late-latching to mask jitter.
- **M / N** push the final image to the display.

## 4.2 Occlusion Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[For each pixel p] --> Q{Real Depth Zr available?}
    Q -- No --> R[Skip Occlusion Render Virtual on Top]
    Q -- Yes --> S{Virtual Depth Zv at p?}
    S -- No --> T[Render Real Only]
    S -- Yes --> U{Zv greater than Zr?}
    U -- Yes --> V[Real hides Virtual]
    U -- No --> W[Virtual hides Real]
```

**Explanation:** This is the **Z-test** central to GPU rasterization. AR systems apply this *per pixel* using a depth map recovered from the chosen occlusion method (stereo, model, or learned).

## 4.3 Lighting Estimation Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Capture 360 Image or HDR Cube] --> L2[Detect Keypoints]
    L2 --> L3[Match to Panorama Dataset]
    L3 --> L4[Retrieve Best HDR Map]
    L4 --> L5[Compute SH Coefficients]
    L5 --> L6[Pass to Renderer]
    L6 --> L7[Per Fragment Lighting]
```

**Explanation:** The lighting subsystem runs **once per scene** (not per frame), making it cheap. SH projection reduces an entire HDR panorama to 9 floating-point numbers.

## 4.4 Visualization Style Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{Application Goal?}
    S -- Entertainment --> P[Photorealistic PBR]
    S -- Education --> N[Non Photorealistic Cel Shading]
    S -- Navigation --> A[Annotations + Ground Shadow]
    S -- Medical --> X[X Ray Vision]
    S -- Engineering --> O[Occlusion + Relighting]
    P --> Q[High Quality GPU]
    N --> R[Low GPU]
    A --> R
    X --> Q
    O --> Q
```

**Explanation:** Style choice is *application-driven*, not aesthetic-driven. KTU expects students to justify the selection.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define non-photorealistic rendering (NPR) in the context of AR. Mention any two NPR techniques.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
NPR is a class of rendering algorithms that intentionally deviates from physical accuracy to enhance **legibility**, **aesthetics**, or **emphasis** of virtual content. In AR, NPR is preferred when photorealistic objects would visually compete with critical real-world information.

Two techniques:
1. **Cel-Shading** – quantizes shading into discrete bands producing a cartoon look.
2. **Edge Detection** – applies Sobel or Canny filters on depth/normal buffers to draw ink outlines.

*(Marks distribution: Definition – 1, Technique 1 – 1, Technique 2 – 1.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is occlusion handling in AR? Why is depth-from-stereo preferred over monocular depth for surgical AR?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Occlusion handling is the process of determining which parts of virtual content should be **hidden** by real objects (and vice versa) to maintain correct scene ordering. (1 mark)

Depth-from-stereo provides **metric, dense** depth at real-time rates by triangulating disparities between two calibrated cameras — for surgical AR, this metric accuracy is essential because an error of a few millimetres can misalign a virtual scalpel with the real organ. (2 marks)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**
**(a) Explain the various occlusion handling techniques used in AR visualization. Compare their computational cost.** (7 marks)

**(b) Derive the depth-from-stereo equation and explain how it is used to achieve depth-correct occlusion rendering.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

| Technique | Working Principle | Cost | Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| Model-based | Pre-scanned 3D mesh used as occluder | Medium | High |
| Stereo | Two cameras compute disparity | High | High |
| Motion (SfM) | Tracks feature points across frames | Medium | Medium |
| Monocular DNN | CNN predicts depth from one image | Very High | Medium |

**Narrative (3 marks):**
- **Model-based:** Best for static indoor scenes (museums, factories). Requires offline scan.
- **Stereo:** Real-time and metric, ideal for surgery and outdoor AR. Needs calibrated stereo rig.
- **SfM:** Cheap on monocular devices; quality depends on texture.
- **Monocular DNN:** Flexible (single camera) but GPU-hungry and metrically unreliable beyond ~5 m.

#### Model Solution — (b)

Refer to **Section 3.1 Derivation 1** above for the full derivation. Key steps to write:

1. Write projection equations $u_L = fX/Z$ and $u_R = f(X-B)/Z$. (1 mark)
2. Define disparity $d = u_L - u_R$. (1 mark)
3. Show $d = fB/Z$ and invert to $Z = fB/d$. (3 marks)
4. Explain occlusion: virtual object at depth $Z_v$ is drawn only where $Z_{real} > Z_v$, else rejected by depth test. (2 marks)

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a) Describe the lighting estimation pipeline for AR. Explain how spherical harmonics are used for real-time relighting.** (7 marks)

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the end-to-end AR visualization pipeline and discuss the role of temporal stabilization.** (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1 — Lighting Estimation (3 marks):**
Capture the real environment using a 360° camera or extract key features from a regular frame. Match those features against an HDR panorama dataset (e.g., Laval HDR). Retrieve the best-matching HDR cubemap and convert to SH coefficients.

**Step 2 — SH Projection (2 marks):**
Compute coefficients $c_l^m = \int_{\Omega} L_i(\omega) Y_l^m(\omega) d\omega$ for $l \le 2$ (9 coefficients). For purely diffuse surfaces, truncate at $l = 1$.

**Step 3 — Relighting (2 marks):**
In the fragment shader, evaluate $I = \rho \cdot \sum c_l^m Y_l^m(\mathbf{n})$ where $\mathbf{n}$ is the surface normal. The virtual object now reflects the dominant directional and ambient light of the real room.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** for the Mermaid diagram. Reproduce in neat labeled form showing capture → tracking → depth → rendering → compositing → stabilization → display.

**Temporal Stabilization (4 marks):**
- Pose estimation is noisy; without correction, virtual objects jitter at 5–10 Hz.
- **Asynchronous Space Warp (ASW)** re-projects the previous frame using the latest pose.
- **Late latching** synchronizes the rendered frame to the display scan-out.
- Combined effect: motion-to-photon latency is reduced from ~40 ms to ~20 ms, eliminating cybersickness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently forget the **baseline $B$** term in the depth equation — that alone costs **2 marks**.
> - Drawing an AR pipeline without labelling **latency / frame rate** is considered incomplete — examiners deduct **1–2 marks**.
> - Confusing **photometric registration** with **geometric registration** is a common error — these are two distinct steps; mixing them loses **3 marks**.
> - For lighting estimation, students often forget to mention **SH order truncation** ($l \le 2$) — this is a **2-mark penalty**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Visualization ≠ Rendering** — Visualization includes occlusion, lighting, shadows, style, and stabilization, not just shading.
- **The five pillars** of AR visualization: **Occlusion · Lighting · Shadows · Style · Stability**.
- **Depth-from-stereo formula:** $Z = fB/d$ — *memorize the derivation, not just the result*.
- **SH lighting** uses **9 coefficients** for $l \le 2$ — sufficient for diffuse relighting on mobile.
- **Latency budget** = $t_{sense} + t_{track} + t_{render} + t_{display} \le \mathbf{20 \, ms}$.
- **NPR > Photorealism** in task-critical AR (medical, training, navigation).
- **Magic Lens / Magic Mirror / X-Ray Vision** are application categories, not algorithms.
- **Three reasons for occluder failure:** missing depth, wrong depth units (mm vs m), and uncalibrated camera–depth extrinsic.
- **Photometric consistency** needs **environment map retrieval OR SH lighting**, not just constant ambient.
- **Temporal stabilization** uses **ASW + late latching** to mask tracking jitter.
- **Ground-plane shadows** are the cheapest and most effective shadow technique for outdoor navigation AR.
- **KTU hot keywords:** occlusion handling, photometric registration, non-photorealistic rendering, spherical harmonics, motion-to-photon latency, magic lens, diminished reality.
- Always quote **frame rate (Hz)**, **latency (ms)**, and **FOV (deg)** when discussing HMD visualization — these are examiner checkpoints.
- When asked for a *comparison*, use a **table** with at least **three trade-off axes** — this is the KTU marking scheme expectation.
- **Remember:** "Sticker-on-screen" failure mode = lack of photometric consistency; "Floating in air" failure mode = lack of ground shadow; "Flickering" failure mode = lack of temporal stabilization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Factors - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Human Factors - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human Factors in AR/VR** is the multidisciplinary scientific study of the physiological, perceptual, cognitive, and ergonomic interactions between human users and immersive (VR) or semi-immersive (AR) computer-generated environments. It applies knowledge from **ergonomics**, **psychophysics**, **neuroscience**, and **human-computer interaction (HCI)** to design systems that maximize **user comfort**, **safety**, **presence**, and **task performance** while minimizing negative effects such as **cybersickness**, **eye strain**, and **cognitive overload**.

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies human factors in AR/VR into the following primary dimensions:

1. **Sensory & Perceptual Factors** — vision, audition, haptics, vestibular.
2. **Ergonomic Factors** — weight, fit, thermal load, IPD (Inter-Pupillary Distance).
3. **Cognitive Factors** — attention, memory load, spatial awareness.
4. **Physiological Factors** — fatigue, nausea, disorientation.
5. **Psychological Factors** — presence, immersion, simulator sickness.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examination framing, **Human Factors** is NOT just "user-friendliness." It is a **quantifiable engineering discipline** with hard metrics such as latency in **milliseconds (ms)**, refresh rate in **Hertz (Hz)**, FOV in **degrees (°)**, and PPD in **pixels/degree**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stranger in the Mirror" Intuition

Imagine wearing a pair of glasses that shows you a **different world** in front of your eyes, but your **inner ear** (responsible for balance) is still reporting that you are standing perfectly still in a room. Your eyes say *"I am flying through a canyon"*, but your body says *"You are standing on a tiled floor."*

This is the **sensory conflict** that lies at the heart of every human factor problem in AR/VR. Human factors engineering is the science of **stopping your body from "arguing" with your eyes**.

| Human Subsystem | What it tells the brain in real life | What AR/VR tells it |
|---|---|---|
| Eyes (Visual) | Head moved 30° left | Head moved 30° left (with latency) |
| Inner Ear (Vestibular) | Head moved 30° left | Head stationary |
| Skin/Muscles (Proprioception) | Body upright | Body upright |
| Muscles (Effort) | Walking 1 km | Walking 0 m |

> **Mismatch = Cybersickness / Disorientation.**

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following industry-standard thresholds appear repeatedly in KTU question papers and are graded for accuracy:

- **Motion-to-Photon Latency Threshold:** ≤ **20 ms** (ideal) / ≤ **70 ms** (acceptable, but begins to cause nausea).
- **Minimum Refresh Rate for VR:** **90 Hz** (absolute minimum) / **120 Hz** (preferred for AAA VR).
- **Human Field of View (FOV):** Binocular ≈ **200° horizontally**, **135° vertically**.
- **Visual Acuity Limit:** **60 PPD** (Pixels Per Degree) is the threshold for "retina-quality" displays.
- **IPD (Inter-Pupillary Distance) Range:** **51 mm to 78 mm** for adult humans.
- **Verbal Cognitive Load Limit:** Miller's Law — **7 ± 2** chunks in working memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Human Factors in AR/VR

### Pillar 1 — Visual Factors
The visual channel dominates in AR/VR because it carries ~70% of all sensory information to the brain. Key sub-parameters:

- **Field of View (FOV):** Total angular extent of the displayed scene measured in degrees. A wider FOV increases immersion but reduces PPD (since pixels are spread thinner).
- **Resolution & PPD:** Pixel density per degree of visual angle. The human fovea can resolve ~**60 PPD**.
- **Refresh Rate:** Number of times the display redraws per second (Hz). Lower refresh rates cause flicker, which the brain interprets as danger signals → nausea.
- **Accommodation-Convergence Conflict:** A deep physiological problem explained in Section 3.
- **IPD Mismatch:** If the headset lens separation does not match the user's IPD, the user sees double images → headaches.

### Pillar 2 — Auditory Factors
- **3D Spatial Audio** must be rendered using **Head-Related Transfer Functions (HRTFs)**.
- **Audio latency** must be kept below **~30 ms** to stay lip-synced with the visual feed.
- In AR, **pass-through audio** allows natural environmental sounds to mix with virtual sounds.

### Pillar 3 — Vestibular & Proprioceptive Factors
- **Vection** — the illusion of self-motion induced purely by visual stimulation.
- When the eyes say "moving" but the vestibular system says "still" → **sensory conflict** → **simulator sickness / cybersickness**.

### Pillar 4 — Haptic Factors
- Force feedback, vibrotactile feedback, and thermal feedback.
- Absence of expected haptic cues (e.g., touching a virtual object with no resistance) breaks presence.

### Pillar 5 — Cognitive & Ergonomic Factors
- **Cognitive Load:** Following Miller's Law (**7 ± 2**), AR/VR UIs must not exceed working-memory capacity.
- **Physical Ergonomics:** Headset weight (target: **< 500 g**), heat dissipation, cable management, controller weight distribution.
- **Usage Duration Limits:** Most KTU-reference studies recommend **20–30 min** sessions for new users to avoid simulator sickness.

---

## 2.2 The Sensory Conflict Theory (Reason & Brand, 1975)

This is the **most important theory** for KTU 14-mark questions on human factors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensory Conflict Theory** states that motion sickness occurs when there is a **mismatch** between:
> 1. The **expected** sensory signals (based on prior experience), and
> 2. The **actual** sensory signals received from current visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive inputs.

**Three Sub-Theories of Cybersickness:**

| Sub-Theory | Trigger | Symptom Onset |
|---|---|---|
| **Eye-Movement Theory** | Nystagmus from optical flow | Seconds |
| **Poison Theory** | Brain interprets conflicting cues as neurotoxin | Minutes |
| **Rest Frame Theory** | Mismatch between virtual and postural frames | 5–20 min |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — these are the *only* equations you will need for the entire Human Factors module.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Field of View (FOV)** | $FOV = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{w}{2d}\right)$ | degrees (°) | $w$ = display width, $d$ = eye-to-lens distance |
| 2 | **Pixels Per Degree (PPD)** | $PPD = \dfrac{\text{Display Resolution (px)}}{FOV\,(°)}$ | px/° | Threshold for retina = 60 PPD |
| 3 | **Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget** | $L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{proc} + L_{render} + L_{display}$ | milliseconds (ms) | Must be $\le 20$ ms |
| 4 | **Refresh Rate (1/Frame Time)** | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}}$ | Hertz (Hz) | $T_{frame}$ in seconds |
| 5 | **Binocular Overlap** | $OL = FOV_{mono} - FOV_{binocular}$ | degrees (°) | Typical = 60°–70° |
| 6 | **Visual Angle (Acuity)** | $\theta = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2D}\right)$ | degrees (°) | $h$ = object height, $D$ = distance |
| 7 | **Snellen Acuity Conversion** | $PPD = 30 \cdot \dfrac{1}{M\,(\text{min-arc})}$ | px/° | $M$ = Snellen denominator |

> **Engineering Utility:** In the VR industry, PPD determines whether a headset is "Retina-grade" (Quest 3 ≈ 25 PPD, Vision Pro ≈ 36 PPD, human limit = 60 PPD). In AR, FOV is the *headline specification* (HoloLens 2 = 52°, Magic Leap 2 = 70°).

---

## 2.4 Why Human Factors Matter — The Real-World Cost of Ignoring It

- In 1995, Nintendo's **Virtual Boy** failed commercially partly because it ignored human factors (red monochromatic display caused eye strain within 15 min).
- Modern VR adoption rates are bottlenecked by **cybersickness** affecting ~25–40% of new users in studies.
- Ergonomic lawsuits (e.g., workplace VR-induced repetitive strain) are an emerging legal risk — making human factors a **legal, ethical, and engineering imperative**.

> **KTU Tip:** When a question says *"Discuss human factors in VR"*, do NOT just list parameters. Always link back to **user experience outcome** (presence, comfort, performance, safety).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View from Display Geometry

**Problem Context:** You are given a VR headset with a **3.5-inch** display (diagonal) viewed from an eye-lens distance of **38 mm**. Compute the diagonal FOV.

**Step 1 — Convert diagonal to width and height.**
Aspect ratio for VR is typically 16:9, so width:height:diagonal = 16:9:18.87 (by Pythagoras).

$$
w = 3.5 \cdot \dfrac{16}{18.87}, \qquad h = 3.5 \cdot \dfrac{9}{18.87}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
w = 3.5 \cdot 0.848 = 2.968 \text{ in}, \qquad h = 3.5 \cdot 0.477 = 1.669 \text{ in}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mm for consistency with eye distance.**

$$
w = 2.968 \cdot 25.4 = 75.4 \text{ mm}, \qquad d = 38 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply FOV formula.**

$$
FOV_h = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{75.4}{2 \cdot 38}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute ratio and inverse tangent.**

$$
\dfrac{75.4}{76} = 0.9921
$$

$$
\arctan(0.9921) = 44.77°
$$

**Step 6 — Double for full horizontal FOV.**

$$
FOV_h = 2 \cdot 44.77° = 89.54°
$$

> **Result:** A 3.5-inch display at 38 mm eye relief gives ~**90° horizontal FOV**, which is *below* the human ~**200°** but adequate for a "focused immersion" headset.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pixels Per Degree (PPD) Calculation

**Problem Context:** The same display has a resolution of **2160 × 2160 pixels per eye**. Compute its PPD.

**Step 1 — Recall the PPD formula.**

$$
PPD = \dfrac{\text{Resolution (px)}}{FOV\,(°)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute horizontal pixels and horizontal FOV.**

$$
PPD = \dfrac{2160}{89.54}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute.**

$$
PPD \approx 24.12 \text{ px/°}
$$

> **Result:** This headset delivers ~**24 PPD**, which is *less than half* of the **60 PPD** retina threshold — meaning fine text and distant objects will appear pixelated (the "screen-door effect").

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Latency Budget Allocation

**Problem Context:** Design a VR pipeline with a target motion-to-photon latency of **20 ms**. Allocate latency across four stages: sensor read, processing, render, and display.

**Step 1 — Define the total latency equation.**

$$
L_{total} = L_{sensor} + L_{proc} + L_{render} + L_{display}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply a typical engineering split (proportional allocation).**

- $L_{sensor} = 2$ ms (IMU read + filter)
- $L_{proc} = 3$ ms (game logic, physics)
- $L_{render} = 10$ ms (GPU draw at 90 Hz)
- $L_{display} = 5$ ms (panel scan-out + persistence)

**Step 3 — Verify total.**

$$
L_{total} = 2 + 3 + 10 + 5 = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Confirm refresh-rate compatibility.**

$$
f_{min} = \dfrac{1}{T_{frame}} = \dfrac{1}{0.020} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

> **Result:** A 20 ms latency budget is *the absolute ceiling* — any extra delay triggers the brain's "this is not real" alarm. Modern headsets target **< 11 ms** to allow margin for async reprojection.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Accommodation–Convergence Conflict

This is the **most-asked 14-mark concept** in KTU Module 4.

**The Setup:**

In the real world, the human eye uses two coupled mechanisms to focus:
1. **Accommodation** — the eye lens changes shape to focus at a distance.
2. **Convergence** — both eyes rotate inward to aim at the focal point.

These two are **neurally linked**.

**In a VR Headset:**
- The display is at a *fixed* optical distance (e.g., 1.5 m from the eye).
- So **accommodation** is locked to ~1.5 m.
- But **convergence** is driven by the *rendered* 3D object's virtual position (e.g., a cube at 5 m forces the eyes to converge at 5 m).

**Step 1 — Express the conflict mathematically.**

$$
D_{accommodation} = D_{display} = \text{constant} \approx 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
D_{convergence} = D_{virtual}(x,y,z) = \text{variable}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the accommodation-convergence error.**

$$
\Delta_{AC} = \vert D_{convergence} - D_{accommodation} \vert
$$

**Step 3 — Identify symptoms at typical error levels.**

$$
\Delta_{AC} > 0.5 \text{ diopters} \Rightarrow \text{Eye strain, headache}
$$

$$
\Delta_{AC} > 2.0 \text{ diopters} \Rightarrow \text{Nausea, disorientation}
$$

> **Solution (advanced):** Varifocal displays and **light-field displays** dynamically adjust focus to eliminate $\Delta_{AC}$, but they are still emerging technologies.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Human Factors to AR vs VR

| Human Factor | VR (Fully Immersive) | AR (Mixed Reality) |
|---|---|---|
| **Visual conflict** | High (HMD replaces reality) | Moderate (overlay only) |
| **Real-world hazard** | Low (user is isolated) | **HIGH** (trips, collisions) |
| **Cybersickness** | High (25–40% of users) | Lower (5–15%) |
| **IPD calibration** | Critical | Critical |
| **Haptic feedback** | Force controllers | Hand-tracking + haptics |
| **Field of View demand** | 110°–200° | 40°–70° (current) |
| **Cognitive load** | Moderate (full scene) | **HIGH** (real + virtual cues) |
| **Fatigue duration** | 20–30 min sessions | Longer tolerated |
| **Safety standards** | IEC 62368-1 (electrical) | ISO 9241 (ergonomics) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Human Factors Taxonomy in AR/VR

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Human Factors in AR / VR] --> B[Perceptual Factors]
    A --> C[Ergonomic Factors]

    B --> B1[Visual: FOV, PPD, Latency]

    C --> C1[Headset Weight]

    F --> F4[VR Addiction]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Sensory Conflict Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    InputA[Visual Stimulus from HMD] --> Comp1{Sensory Comparator in Brain}
    InputB[Vestibular Signal from Inner Ear] --> Comp1
    InputC[Proprioceptive Signal from Muscles] --> Comp1

    Comp1 --> Match[Match: No Conflict]
    Comp1 --> Mismatch[Mismatch Detected]

    Match --> Output1[Stable Perception]
    Mismatch --> Output2[Cybersickness Triggers]

    Output2 --> S1[Nausea]
    Output2 --> S2[Vomiting]
    Output2 --> S3[Dizziness]
    Output2 --> S4[Eye Strain]
    Output2 --> S5[Disorientation]

    Output2 --> Mitigation[Design-Level Mitigations]
    Mitigation --> M1[Reduce Latency to below 20 ms]
    Mitigation --> M2[Teleportation Locomotion]
    Mitigation --> M3[Snap Turn instead of Smooth Turn]
    Mitigation --> M4[Comfort Mode Vignette]
    Mitigation --> M5[Gradual User Acclimation]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Latency Budget Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[User Head Movement] --> B[IMU Sensor Read: 2 ms]
    B --> C[Pose Prediction: 1 ms]
    C --> D[Game Logic: 3 ms]
    D --> E[Render Frame: 10 ms]
    E --> F[Display Scan-out: 3 ms]
    F --> G[Photons Reach Eye: 1 ms]
    G --> H{Total less than 20 ms?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Acceptable: No Nausea]
    H -->|No| J[Unacceptable: Triggers Cybersickness]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The values shown are typical engineering targets. Modern high-end HMDs use **Asynchronous Spacewarp / Reprojection** to compensate for missed frames without user-perceived latency.
