# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
Define *Sensor Hardware* in the context of AR/VR systems. List **any four** key performance parameters used to evaluate AR/VR sensors.

**Model Answer:**

Sensor Hardware refers to the physical electronic and electro-mechanical components in an AR/VR system that transduce real-world physical quantities (motion, light, magnetic field, sound, force) into electrical signals for real-time processing and rendering of immersive environments.

**Four key performance parameters:**
1. **Latency** — delay between physical event and digital response; target < 20 ms.
2. **Sampling Rate (Hz)** — IMUs typically 1000 Hz; cameras 30–120 Hz.
3. **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** — 3-DOF (orientation) vs 6-DOF (position + orientation).
4. **Accuracy and Drift** — gyroscope bias drift over time; corrected via sensor fusion.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark each for the four parameters.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
Differentiate between **inside-out** and **outside-in** tracking with one example device for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Inside-Out Tracking | Outside-In Tracking |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor Location | On the HMD | External fixed base stations |
| Examples | Meta Quest 3, HoloLens 2 | HTC Vive (pre-2019), PSVR |
| Setup | No external hardware; portable | Requires stationary external rig |
| Tracking Volume | Limited by HMD FOV | Defined by base-station coverage |
| Marker Type | Natural feature points (SLAM) | Active IR LEDs or laser sweeps |

*(2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for one valid example each.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of a **MEMS accelerometer** and a **MEMS gyroscope** with neat diagrams. Derive the expression for position $x(t)$ from the accelerometer output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**MEMS Accelerometer Working Principle:** *(2 marks)*
A MEMS accelerometer consists of a tiny silicon proof mass suspended on compliant spring beams. Comb-finger capacitors are etched on either side of the mass. Under external acceleration, the mass deflects by $\Delta x$, changing the capacitance $C \propto 1/d$. A demodulation circuit converts $\Delta C$ into a voltage proportional to acceleration. The governing equation is:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = -m\,a_{\text{ext}}$$

**MEMS Gyroscope Working Principle:** *(2 marks)*
A MEMS gyroscope uses a mass driven into resonant vibration at frequency $f_0$ along the drive axis. When the chip rotates with angular velocity $\omega$, the mass experiences the Coriolis force $F_c = 2m\,\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}$ perpendicular to the drive direction. The Coriolis displacement is measured capacitively and is proportional to $\omega$.

**Derivation of position from accelerometer:** *(3 marks — see Section 3.1 for full step-by-step)*
Starting from $a(t)$, integrate once to get velocity, then integrate again to get position:

$$v(t) = v(0) + \int_{0}^{t} a(\tau)\,d\tau$$

$$x(t) = x(0) + v(0)t + \int_{0}^{t}\!\int_{0}^{\tau} a(\sigma)\,d\sigma\,d\tau$$

Explicitly state that the *initial conditions* $x(0)$ and $v(0)$ are required, and the integration is *open-loop* in raw form — hence drift — solved by sensor fusion.

---

**Q. (b)** With a block diagram, describe the **sensor fusion pipeline** used in a modern 6-DOF HMD. Explain why the **Kalman filter** is preferred over a simple complementary filter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram Description:** *(2 marks)*
A 6-DOF HMD typically fuses:
- **IMU (1000 Hz)** — accelerometer + gyroscope (predict step, high rate)
- **Magnetometer (100 Hz)** — absolute heading reference
- **Inside-out cameras (90 Hz)** — visual SLAM providing absolute position correction
- **EKF / ESKF** — fuses all streams into a single pose estimate $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$

**Kalman Filter vs Complementary Filter:** *(3 marks)*
| Feature | Complementary Filter | Kalman Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Weighting | Fixed, hand-tuned ($\alpha$) | Adaptive via Kalman gain $K_k$ |
| Noise model | None explicit | Explicit $Q$ and $R$ covariances |
| Optimality | Sub-optimal | Minimum-variance linear estimator |
| Multi-sensor | Awkward | Scales to N sensors naturally |
| Latency | Constant | Predicts forward, smooths backward |

**Why Kalman is preferred:** *(2 marks)*
- Provides **statistically optimal** weighting between prediction (IMU) and measurement (camera/magnetometer).
- **Adaptive** — automatically trusts whichever sensor has lower noise.
- **Recursive** — $O(n)$ computation per step, ideal for real-time embedded HMDs.
- Naturally extends to **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** for non-linear rotation dynamics (quaternions).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Compare **Time-of-Flight (ToF)**, **Structured Light**, and **Stereo Vision** depth-sensing techniques. Give one AR/VR product example for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Time-of-Flight (ToF) | Structured Light | Stereo Vision |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working | Measure return time of IR pulse | Project known IR pattern, compute disparity | Two cameras mimic binocular disparity |
| Depth formula | $d = ct/2$ | Disparity via pattern matching | $Z = fB/d$ |
| Accuracy | ±1 cm @ 4 m | ±mm level at close range | Depends on texture & baseline |
| Range | 0.1 m – 10 m | 0.2 m – 5 m | 0.5 m – 50 m |
| Compute load | Low (hardware ToF) | Medium | High (dense stereo) |
| Outdoor | Good | Poor (sun washes IR) | Good |
| Example product | Microsoft Azure Kinect, iPhone Face ID | Microsoft Kinect v1, Intel RealSense SR300 | Meta Quest 3 passthrough, ZED 2i |

*(3 marks for the table, 2 marks for working principles, 1 mark each for one valid example per technique.)*

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **gyroscope drift problem** in detail. With mathematical justification, show how a **magnetometer-aided Kalman filter** corrects the drift. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Gyroscope Drift — The Problem:** *(2 marks)*
A MEMS gyroscope measures $\omega_{\text{measured}} = \omega_{\text{true}} + b_\omega + n(t)$ where $b_\omega$ is a slowly varying bias and $n(t)$ is white noise. The orientation is obtained by integration:

$$\theta(t) = \theta(0) + \int_{0}^{t} \omega_{\text{measured}}(\tau)\,d\tau$$

The bias term integrates linearly: $\theta_{\text{err}}(t) = b_\omega \cdot t$. For $b_\omega = 0.01°/\text{s}$, drift after 10 min is $6°$ — catastrophic for HMDs.

**Magnetometer as Heading Reference:** *(2 marks)*
The magnetometer measures the Earth magnetic field vector $\vec{B} = (B_x, B_y, B_z)$. The heading (yaw $\psi$) is computed as:

$$\psi_{\text{mag}} = \arctan2(-B_y,\,B_x)$$

This is *absolute* and *non-drifting*, but is corrupted near metal objects and indoor environments (ferromagnetic distortion).

**Kalman Filter Correction:** *(3 marks)*
- **State vector:** $\mathbf{x} = [\phi, \theta, \psi, b_\phi, b_\theta, b_\psi]^T$ (3 angles + 3 gyro biases).
- **Predict:** Use gyroscope integration with bias included.
- **Update:** Use magnetometer yaw $\psi_{\text{mag}}$ as a measurement.
- The Kalman gain $K_k$ automatically **down-weights the magnetometer** when its noise $R$ is large (near metal) and **up-weights it** when the gyro drift $P$ grows.

Result: the filter output $\hat{\psi}$ is a *drift-free, low-noise* estimate of the true heading. This is the standard **9-DOF MARG sensor fusion** algorithm used in nearly every commercial HMD.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting initial conditions** in integration problems — *always write $x(0), v(0)$ explicitly*.
> 2. **Confusing Coriolis force direction** — $F_c = 2m\,\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}$, *not* $\vec{v} \times \vec{\omega}$.
> 3. **Writing "Kalman filter gives correct answer"** without showing the **gain equation** $K_k = P H^T(HPH^T+R)^{-1}$ — partial credit only.
> 4. **Stating "drift is bad"** without *quantifying* it via $\sigma_{\text{drift}} = b_\omega \cdot t$.
> 5. **Skipping units** in the formula sheet — *always* mention m/s², rad/s, Hz, ms.
> 6. **Mixing up ToF formula** — depth is $d = ct/2$ because the pulse travels *round-trip*.
> 7. **Drawing sensor block diagrams without arrows** — KTU examiner expects *labeled* signal flow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this as a **30-minute pre-exam revision sheet**. Cover all bullets verbally to confirm mastery.

- ✅ **Definition:** Sensor hardware = physical devices transducing real-world physics into electrical signals for AR/VR processing.
- ✅ **Five metrics:** Latency (< 20 ms), Sampling rate (Hz), Accuracy, Precision, DOF (3 vs 6).
- ✅ **Accelerometer equation:** $a = F/m$; uses proof-mass + capacitive comb readout.
- ✅ **Gyroscope equation:** $F_c = 2m\,\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}$ (Coriolis force).
- ✅ **Magnetometer:** Hall-effect / magnetoresistive; provides absolute heading $\psi = \arctan2(-B_y, B_x)$.
- ✅ **Drift formula:** $\sigma_{\text{drift}} = b_\omega \cdot t$; catastrophic over minutes.
- ✅ **Position from accel:** $x(t) = x(0) + v(0)t + \int\!\!\int a(\tau)\,d\tau\,dt$.
- ✅ **Angle from gyro:** $\theta(t) = \theta(0) + \int \omega(\tau)\,d\tau$.
- ✅ **ToF depth:** $d = ct/2$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **Stereo depth:** $Z = fB/d$.
- ✅ **Kalman gain:** $K_k = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$.
- ✅ **State update:** $\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k(z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$.
- ✅ **Inside-out tracking:** cameras on HMD (Meta Quest, HoloLens).
- ✅ **Outside-in tracking:** external base stations (Vive, PSVR).
- ✅ **6-DOF pose vector:** $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi) \in \mathbb{R}^3 \times SO(3)$.
- ✅ **Euler ZYX rotation matrix:** $R = R_z(\psi)\,R_y(\theta)\,R_x(\phi)$.
- ✅ **Sensor trade-offs:** gyro drifts, accelerometer noisy double-integrates, camera low rate, magnetometer distorts near metal → **fusion is mandatory**.
- ✅ **Motion-to-photon latency budget:** $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{sense}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{render}} < 20$ ms.
- ✅ **Depth-sensing triad:** ToF (range), Structured Light (precision), Stereo (texture-rich scenes).
- ✅ **Bioelectric sensors:** EMG (muscle), EEG (brain), GSR (skin conductance) — used in affective/neural AR/VR.
- ✅ **Haptic sensors:** FSR, flex sensor, strain gauge — used in gloves, suits, treadmills.

**End of Module 4 — Sensor Hardware Notes** | *Good luck with your KTU examinations!*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Head-Coupled Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Head-Coupled Displays

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Head-Coupled Display (HCD)** is a display subsystem in which the image source and its associated optics are mechanically, optically, or electronically coupled to the orientation and position of the user's head. The display's viewing frustum is dynamically co-aligned with the user's line of sight, producing a stable, egocentric (first-person), perspective-correct view of a synthetic or augmented scene. The fundamental invariant is: **for every head pose, there exists exactly one corresponding rendered image**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine strapping a small television to your forehead using a headband, with magnifying lenses right in front of your eyes, and the television is fed a video feed from a computer. Now, tilt your head left — the video feed shifts so it looks like you just turned your head inside a virtual world. That is exactly what a **Head-Coupled Display** does:

- **HMD (Head-Mounted Display)** → like wearing ski goggles with screens inside.
- **BOOM Display** → like holding two binoculars to your eyes, mounted on a counter-weighted arm.
- **Glasses-style HCD (AR/MR)** → like ordinary spectacles whose lenses are also transparent micro-displays.

Wherever your head turns, the rendered scene "rotates" with it, simulating the feeling that you are *inside* the computer-generated world rather than watching it on an external monitor.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Average human **IPD (Interpupillary Distance)** | **63 mm** | Range: 50 mm – 75 mm |
| Display refresh rate | **60 – 120 Hz** | Minimum **90 Hz** recommended to avoid motion sickness |
| Field of View (FOV) | **40° – 110°** (HMD), up to **140°** (wide FOV) | Human binocular FOV ≈ 200° horizontal |
| Eye relief | **10 – 25 mm** | Distance from eye to nearest optical surface |
| Exit pupil | **8 – 15 mm** | Tolerance zone for eye placement |
| Pixel density | **15 – 60 PPD (Pixels Per Degree)** | 60 PPD = "retinal resolution" threshold |
| Head tracking latency | **< 20 ms** | Above 70 ms induces VR sickness |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** HCDs form the **output end** of the classic VR pipeline — they are the perceptual gateway through which synthesized content reaches the user's retina. The other half (input) is discussed under *Head & Hand Tracking*.

---

### Taxonomy of Head-Coupled Displays

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies HCDs along three orthogonal axes:

1. **By Viewing Mode**
   - *Fully immersive (opaque)* — used for VR; blocks the real world.
   - *See-through (optical or video pass-through)* — used for AR/MR.

2. **By Coupling Mechanism**
   - *Direct-mounted* (helmet/glasses form factor) — HMD family.
   - *Mechanically tracked boom* — BOOM displays.
   - *Body-coupled with inertial/optical tracking* — modern stand-alone HMDs.

3. **By Channel Count**
   - *Monocular* — one eye only (rare, e.g., private notification displays).
   - *Binocular (stereo)* — one display per eye ⇒ stereoscopic depth perception.

> [!TIP]
> A **BOOM (Binocular Omni-Orientation Monitor)** is technically still a *Head-Coupled* display even though it is not worn on the head — because its viewing box is **physically servo-tracked to the user's head pose** through an articulated counter-weighted arm. Always remember: the coupling, not the mount, defines the HCD.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Head-Coupled Display

The HCD operates as the final stage in the standard VR rendering pipeline:

1. **Pose Sampling** — Head tracker (6-DoF) reports position $\mathbf{p}(t) = (x, y, z)$ and orientation $\mathbf{R}(t)$ at frame $t$.
2. **View Matrix Construction** — The view matrix $\mathbf{V}$ is built from $\mathbf{R}(t)^{-1}$ and $-\mathbf{p}(t)$, transforming world coordinates into camera (eye) coordinates.
3. **Stereo Projection** — Two asymmetric view frustums are generated, offset by $\pm \tfrac{\text{IPD}}{2}$ along the camera's right axis, producing left- and right-eye projection matrices $\mathbf{P}_L$ and $\mathbf{P}_R$.
4. **Rasterization** — The GPU renders two images, one per eye, into framebuffers at the target resolution $(w \times h)$.
5. **Distortion Compensation** — Pre-distortion is applied in the GPU (Barrel/Pincushion inverse) to compensate for the optical distortion introduced by the eyepiece lens.
6. **Display & Scan-out** — Frames are routed to the left and right micro-displays (LCD, OLED, or Micro-LED) at refresh rate $f_{\text{refresh}}$.
7. **Optical Magnification** — The eyepiece re-images the display surface onto the retina, creating a virtual image at the optical far point (Optical Infinity, typically 2 m – 6 m virtual distance).
8. **Sensor Fusion Closure** — End-to-end latency $t_{\text{Motion-to-Photon}}$ must be kept below **20 ms** for comfortable VR.

### 2.2 Why Coupling Must Be Tight (Engineering Justification)

The human vestibular-ocular reflex (VOR) detects **mismatch** between visual flow and inner-ear motion. If the rendered image lags behind a real head turn by more than $\sim$ **70 ms**, the brain interprets it as self-motion through a static world, triggering cybersickness. Hence the "head-coupling" must be both:

- **Geometrically accurate** — FOV, parallax, and scale must match real-world retinal projection.
- **Temporally tight** — end-to-end latency $\le$ 20 ms in modern systems (e.g., Valve Index, Meta Quest 3).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Monocular Field of View** | $\text{FOV}_h = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d_h}{2 f_e}\right)$ | $d_h$ = horizontal display dimension, $f_e$ = eyepiece focal length | degrees |
| 2 | **Total Binocular FOV (overlap)** | $\text{FOV}_{\text{total}} = 2\cdot\text{FOV}_h - \text{FOV}_{\text{overlap}}$ | $\text{FOV}_{\text{overlap}}$ is the binocular overlap region | degrees |
| 3 | **Angular Resolution** | $\theta_{\text{pix}} = \dfrac{\text{FOV}_h}{w}$ | $w$ = horizontal pixel count | deg/pixel |
| 4 | **Pixels Per Degree (PPD)** | $\text{PPD} = \dfrac{w}{\text{FOV}_h}$ | Inverse of angular resolution | pixels/deg |
| 5 | **Vergence Angle to Fixation** | $\theta_v = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\text{IPD}}{2 D}\right)$ | $D$ = distance to fixation point | radians / degrees |
| 6 | **Stereoscopic Parallax (Disparity)** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{f_e \cdot \text{IPD}}{Z}$ | $Z$ = depth of object in scene units | scene units |
| 7 | **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC) distance** | $D_{\text{VAC}} = \dfrac{f_e \cdot d_h}{\text{IPD}}$ | Distance at which vergence and focal planes coincide | mm |
| 8 | **Latency Budget (motion-to-photon)** | $t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{render}} + t_{\text{scan}} + t_{\text{disp}}$ | Sum of sensor, render, scan-out, display persistence | ms |
| 9 | **Newton's Lens Equation (Virtual Image)** | $\dfrac{1}{f_e} = \dfrac{1}{s_o} + \dfrac{1}{s_i}$ (signed) | $s_o$ = object (display) distance, $s_i$ = virtual image distance | mm |
| 10 | **Pupil Magnification** | $m_p = \dfrac{D_{\text{exit}}}{D_{\text{ent}}}$ | Ratio of exit to entrance pupil diameters | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Break Note:** All vertical bars in tables use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (LaTeX) — never a literal pipe `|`, which would corrupt the markdown table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Head-coupled displays underpin several engineering domains:

- **Aerospace simulation** — Pilot training (F-16, F-35 HMDS) using wide-FOV binocular HCDs.
- **Surgical training** — AR see-through HCDs overlay anatomy (Microsoft HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2).
- **Industrial design & CAD review** — Immersive visualization of large assemblies.
- **Telemedicine / Remote Operation** — Telerobotics with stereoscopic HCDs.
- **Entertainment & Gaming** — Consumer VR (Meta Quest, PSVR2, Apple Vision Pro).
- **Scientific visualization** — Volume rendering of medical or geospatial datasets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are **explicit and exhaustive**. No step is skipped; each algebraic transition is annotated with its physical/logical justification.

### 3.1 Derivation: Field of View from Display and Eyepiece Geometry

We model the eyepiece as a thin convex lens of focal length $f_e$. A micro-display of physical width $d_h$ sits at the **front focal plane** of the eyepiece (object distance $s_o \approx f_e$). The lens then produces a virtual image at optical infinity, which the relaxed human eye focuses on comfortably.

**Step 1 — Setup the geometry.**
Consider a ray originating from the center of the display (mid-height) and passing through the optical center of the lens undeviated. A ray from the display edge, however, emerges from the lens at an angle $\alpha$ to the optical axis.

The lens equation gives:

$$\frac{1}{f_e} = \frac{1}{s_o} + \frac{1}{s_i}$$

**Step 2 — Place the display at the focal plane.**
Set $s_o = f_e$ (display at the focal point). Then:

$$\frac{1}{f_e} = \frac{1}{f_e} + \frac{1}{s_i} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{1}{s_i} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s_i \to \infty$$

This means all rays from a point on the display emerge **parallel** from the eyepiece — i.e., the eye perceives a virtual image at infinity.

**Step 3 — Compute the half-angle.**
A ray from the display edge (at height $d_h/2$ above the axis) emerges parallel to the line joining the lens center to that edge point. By similar triangles (or simply by the small-angle tangent definition):

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{d_h/2}{f_e}$$

The **monocular horizontal field of view** is twice this half-angle:

$$\text{FOV}_h = 2\alpha = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_h}{2 f_e}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check with a real device.**
Consider the Meta Quest 3 panel: $d_h = 22.86\,\text{mm}$ (approx), $f_e \approx 18\,\text{mm}$.

$$\text{FOV}_h = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{22.86}{2 \cdot 18}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.635) = 2 \cdot 32.4^\circ \approx 64.8^\circ$$

This matches the marketed $\approx$ 110° **diagonal** FOV (horizontal is smaller because $d_h < d_{\text{diag}}$). The formula is verified.

### 3.2 Derivation: Pixels Per Degree (PPD)

Given a horizontal display resolution $w$ pixels and a horizontal field of view $\text{FOV}_h$ in degrees, the angular subtense of a single pixel is:

$$\theta_{\text{pix}} = \frac{\text{FOV}_h}{w}$$

The reciprocal is the conventional metric **Pixels Per Degree**:

$$\text{PPD} = \frac{w}{\text{FOV}_h}$$

**Numerical example.** A Meta Quest 3 panel has $w = 2064$ pixels across a $\text{FOV}_h \approx 98°$ horizontal FOV (in mixed passthrough mode). Then:

$$\text{PPD} = \frac{2064}{98} \approx 21\,\text{PPD}$$

This is below the "retinal limit" of 60 PPD — explaining why fine text in current HMDs still looks pixelated.

### 3.3 Derivation: Vergence Angle and Stereoscopic Depth

For a binocular viewer with inter-pupillary distance $\text{IPD}$, fixating on a point at distance $D$ in front of the face, each eye rotates inward by a **vergence angle** $\theta_v / 2$ from the primary position. By the geometry of an isoceles triangle with base $\text{IPD}$ and equal sides $D$:

$$\tan\!\left(\frac{\theta_v}{2}\right) = \frac{\text{IPD}/2}{D}$$

Solving for $\theta_v$:

$$\theta_v = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{\text{IPD}}{2 D}\right)$$

**Numerical example.** IPD = 63 mm, $D$ = 2 m = 2000 mm:

$$\theta_v = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{63}{2 \cdot 2000}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.01575) = 2 \cdot 0.902^\circ \approx 1.80^\circ$$

The eyes are verged inward by 0.9° each, consistent with normal viewing of a desktop monitor.

### 3.4 Derivation: Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)

In the natural world, vergence (eye-rotation angle) and accommodation (lens focus) are neurally coupled. In an HCD, the display is fixed at the focal plane of the eyepiece — so **accommodation** is locked to the virtual image distance $s_i$ (typically 2 m – 6 m). But **vergence** is set by the *stereo disparity* on the displays, which can place objects at any virtual depth. This is the **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)** — a leading cause of HCD-induced eye strain.

Define the **zero-disparity distance** $D_0$ as the depth at which the two eyes' visual axes converge naturally with the display's focal plane. For a thin-lens eyepiece producing a virtual image at $s_i$, with a display of width $d_h$ subtending $\text{FOV}_h$:

$$D_0 = \frac{\text{IPD} \cdot s_i}{d_h}$$

Derivation: the eye's pupil separation is IPD; the lens projects a virtual image at $s_i$; the perceived depth of the central point of the virtual image equals the **far-point** of the accommodation. The geometry of similar triangles from the lens center to the two pupils yields:

$$\frac{D_0}{s_i} = \frac{\text{IPD}}{d_h} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; D_0 = \frac{\text{IPD}\cdot s_i}{d_h}$$

For Quest 3: $\text{IPD} = 63\,\text{mm}$, $s_i \approx 2000\,\text{mm}$ (2 m optical infinity proxy), $d_h = 22.86\,\text{mm}$:

$$D_0 = \frac{63 \cdot 2000}{22.86} \approx 5510\,\text{mm} \approx 5.5\,\text{m}$$

So **objects rendered at zero disparity appear at ~5.5 m** — comfortable for the eyes. Disparate objects placed virtually closer than this trigger VAC.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HCD Sub-Families

| Family | Coupling Type | Pros | Cons | Typical FOV | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HMD (helmet)** | Rigid mount + IMU/optical tracking | Hands-free, wide FOV, mobile | Heavy, sweaty, VAC, fragile | 90°–120° | Meta Quest 3, Vision Pro |
| **BOOM** | Counter-weighted articulated arm with mechanical encoders | Zero perceived latency, perfect tracking, can swap users | Tethered, stationary, single user | 140°–168° | FakeSpace BOOM, Fakespace Wide5 |
| **Binocular Viewer (CAVE-style wearable)** | Magnetic / optical tracker on glasses + off-board rendering | High-res, lightweight optics | External rendering, tracker drift | 60°–90° | eMagin Z800 |
| **Smart Glasses (AR)** | See-through waveguide + SLAM tracking | Hands-free, real-world overlay | Low FOV (~50°), brightness issues | 30°–52° | HoloLens 2, Magic Leap 2 |
| **Fibre-Optic HMD (CAE)** | Rigid helmet + magnetic tracking | Used in military sims | Heavy, expensive | ~80° | CAE Fibre-Optic HMD |
| **EyePhone / VIVED (historical)** | LCD + magnetic tracker | Pioneered consumer VR | Low res (VGA), heavy | ~60° | VPL EyePhone (1989) |

### 3.6 End-to-End Latency Budget Derivation

The motion-to-photon latency $t_{\text{MP}}$ is:

$$t_{\text{MP}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{predict}} + t_{\text{render}} + t_{\text{scan}} + t_{\text{persistence}}$$

| Stage | Typical Value (Modern HMD) |
|---|---|
| $t_{\text{sense}}$ (IMU sample) | 1 – 2 ms |
| $t_{\text{predict}}$ (pose prediction) | 2 – 5 ms |
| $t_{\text{render}}$ (GPU) | 5 – 8 ms @ 90 Hz |
| $t_{\text{scan}}$ (display scan-out) | 5 – 6 ms |
| $t_{\text{persistence}}$ (pixel hold until refresh) | 4 – 8 ms |
| **Total** | **17 – 29 ms** |

To hit the **20 ms** target, GPU + display pipeline must be tightly co-designed (e.g., asynchronous timewarp, late latching).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams are rendered using **Mermaid** syntax with strict alphanumeric node IDs and clean text labels (no markdown formatting inside quoted labels).

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Head-Coupled Display System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputA[Input Layer]
        tracker6[6DoF Head Tracker IMU plus Optical]
        userHead[User Head Motion]
    end

    subgraph procB[Processing Layer]
        poseEst[Pose Estimator Sensor Fusion]
        viewMat[View Matrix Builder]
        stereoProj[Stereo Projection Two Frustums]
        rasterGPU[GPU Rasterizer with Distortion]
    end

    subgraph outputC[Display Layer]
        lcdL[Left Micro Display OLED or LCD]
        lcdR[Right Micro Display OLED or LCD]
        lensL[Left Eyepiece Lens]
        lensR[Right Eyepiece Lens]
        eyeL[Left Eye Retina]
        eyeR[Right Eye Retina]
    end

    userHead --> tracker6
    tracker6 --> poseEst
    poseEst --> viewMat
    viewMat --> stereoProj
    stereoProj --> rasterGPU
    rasterGPU --> lcdL
    rasterGPU --> lcdR
    lcdL --> lensL
    lcdR --> lensR
    lensL --> eyeL
    lensR --> eyeR
    eyeL -->|Feedback| userHead
    eyeR -->|Feedback| userHead
```

### 4.2 Optical Path of an Eyepiece (Lens Geometry)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    microdisp[Micro Display at Focal Plane]
    edgeRay[Edge Ray from Display]
    centerRay[Central Ray along Optical Axis]
    lens[Convex Eyepiece Lens]
    paraOut[Parallel Rays Exit Lens]
    eyePupil[Eye Pupil at Exit Pupil Zone]
    retina[Retina Virtual Image at Infinity]

    microdisp --> edgeRay
    microdisp --> centerRay
    edgeRay --> lens
    centerRay --> lens
    lens --> paraOut
    paraOut --> eyePupil
    eyePupil --> retina
```

### 4.3 Classification Tree of Head-Coupled Displays

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hcdRoot[Head Coupled Display]
    hcdRoot --> chanA[By Channel Count]
    hcdRoot --> mountB[By Mount Mechanism]
    hcdRoot --> visC[By Visibility Mode]

    chanA --> mono[Monocular]
    chanA --> binoc[Binocular Stereo]

    mountB --> helmet[Helmet Mounted HMD]
    mountB --> boom[Articulated BOOM]
    mountB --> glass[Glasses Form Smart Glasses]

    visC --> opq[Fully Immersive Opaque]
    visC --> seeT[See Through Optical]
    visC --> seeV[See Through Video Pass Through]
```

### 4.4 Tracking Subsystem Coupling (Why Coupling Matters)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userHead2[User Head Moves]
    sensorIMU[Inertial IMU Gyro plus Accel]
    sensorCam[Outside In Cameras or Inside Out SLAM]
    fuse[Sensor Fusion Kalman Filter]
    poseOut[6DoF Pose Vector]
    renderEngine[Render Engine]
    dispLCD[Display Showing New Frame]

    userHead2 -->|Real Motion| sensorIMU
    userHead2 -->|Visual Features| sensorCam
    sensorIMU --> fuse
    sensorCam --> fuse
    fuse --> poseOut
    poseOut --> renderEngine
    renderEngine --> dispLCD
    dispLCD -->|Perceived Motion| userHead2
```

### 4.5 End-to-End Latency Budget (Stack Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[IMU Sense 2ms]
    a2[Pose Fusion 3ms]
    a3[GPU Render 7ms]
    a4[Scan Out 6ms]
    a5[Persistence 5ms]
    aSum[Total 23ms Target Under 20ms Ideal]

    a1 --> a2 --> a3 --> a4 --> a5 --> aSum
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
Define a **Head-Coupled Display (HCD)**. List any two examples of HCDs that are *not* helmet-mounted.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** A Head-Coupled Display is a display device whose image source and optics are physically or electronically coupled to the user's head pose such that for every head orientation and position, a corresponding perspective-correct image is generated and presented to the user, producing a first-person egocentric view of a synthetic or augmented scene.
>
> **Two non-helmet examples (1 mark):**
> 1. **BOOM Display (Binocular Omni-Orientation Monitor)** — mounted on a counter-weighted articulated arm with mechanical encoders reporting head pose.
> 2. **Cave Automatic Virtual Environment (CAVE) with shutter-glasses** — wearable stereo glasses that are head-tracked via external cameras.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (2M) + Examples (1M) = **3 Marks**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
State the **Vergence–Accommodation Conflict (VAC)**. Why does it arise specifically in HCDs?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> In natural viewing, the eyes' **vergence** (rotation inward to fixate at a depth) and **accommodation** (lens focus) are neurally coupled and both change together with object distance (1 M).
>
> In an HCD, the **accommodation** is locked to the optical infinity / virtual image distance of the eyepiece (1 M), while the **vergence** is dictated by the stereo disparity of the rendered image, which can place objects at any perceived depth (1 M). This decoupling produces the **VAC**, causing eye strain and visual fatigue during prolonged use.

**Marks Distribution:** Natural coupling (1M) + HCD decoupling (1M) + Why it arises (1M) = **3 Marks**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 4 Compulsory Section]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **optical architecture of a typical binocular HMD**, identifying the role of the micro-display, eyepiece lens, and exit pupil. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **horizontal field of view** of an HMD in terms of display width $d_h$ and eyepiece focal length $f_e$. Compute the FOV for a system with $d_h = 24\,\text{mm}$ and $f_e = 20\,\text{mm}$. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Optical Architecture (7 Marks)**

| Component | Role | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Micro-display (LCD/OLED/Micro-LED)** | Renders the image to be viewed; acts as the "object" for the eyepiece | 2 |
| **Eyepiece lens (convex, achromatic doublet)** | Re-images the display as a virtual image at optical infinity; provides magnification and FOV control | 2 |
| **Exit pupil** | Region behind the eyepiece where the eye must be placed to capture full FOV; typically 8–15 mm diameter | 2 |
| **Housing & IPD adjustment** | Maintains correct inter-pupillary distance and seals out ambient light (for VR) | 1 |

A labelled Mermaid diagram suffices (refer to Section 4.2 above).

**Part (b) — FOV Derivation (7 Marks)**

Place the display at the focal plane of the eyepiece ($s_o = f_e$).
The lens equation gives:

$$\frac{1}{f_e} = \frac{1}{s_o} + \frac{1}{s_i} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s_i \to \infty$$

A ray from the display edge (at height $d_h/2$) emerges parallel to the line joining the edge to the lens centre:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{d_h/2}{f_e}$$

The full field of view is twice this half-angle:

$$\boxed{\text{FOV}_h = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d_h}{2 f_e}\right)}$$

**Numerical Computation (3 Marks):**

$$\text{FOV}_h = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{24}{2 \cdot 20}\right) = 2 \arctan(0.6) = 2 \cdot 30.96^\circ \approx 61.92^\circ$$

So the monocular horizontal FOV is approximately **62°**.

---

## Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 4 Internal Choice B]**
>
> **(a)** Compare and contrast **BOOM displays**, **helmet-mounted HMDs**, and **see-through AR smart glasses** along the axes of (i) latency, (ii) field of view, (iii) mobility, and (iv) ergonomic comfort. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **motion-to-photon latency**. A modern HMD has the following stage times: IMU sensing = 2 ms, pose fusion = 3 ms, GPU render = 7 ms, scan-out = 6 ms, persistence = 5 ms. Compute the total latency and state whether it meets the KTU-recommended budget for comfortable VR. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparative Table (7 Marks)**

| Axis | BOOM Display | Helmet HMD | AR Smart Glasses |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | Near-zero (mechanical, no sensing delay) (1M) | 15–25 ms with sensor fusion (1M) | 20–40 ms due to SLAM pipeline (1M) |
| **FOV** | 140°–168° (1M) | 90°–120° (1M) | 30°–52° (1M) |
| **Mobility** | Tethered to a stand; single-station (1M partial) | Fully mobile, battery-powered (1M partial) | Mobile but requires scene-mapping compute (1M partial) |
| **Ergonomics** | Heavy arm fatigue, no head load (1M partial) | Head/neck strain over time (1M partial) | Lightweight (≈ 100 g) (1M partial) |

**Part (b) — Motion-to-Photon Latency (7 Marks)**

**Definition (3 Marks):**
Motion-to-photon latency is the time elapsed between a real physical movement of the user's head and the moment the corresponding updated pixel reaches the user's retina through the display. It is the sum of every pipeline stage from sensor to photons:

$$t_{\text{MP}} = t_{\text{sense}} + t_{\text{fuse}} + t_{\text{render}} + t_{\text{scan}} + t_{\text{persist}}$$

**Computation (3 Marks):**

$$t_{\text{MP}} = 2 + 3 + 7 + 6 + 5 = 23\,\text{ms}$$

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
The KTU-recommended (and industry-standard) budget for comfortable VR is **≤ 20 ms** to avoid the onset of cybersickness. This system's latency of **23 ms** slightly exceeds the recommended budget, so it would **not** meet the ideal comfort criterion, although it remains within the *acceptable* range (≤ 30 ms) for short-duration sessions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students frequently lose marks on HCD questions for these specific reasons:
>
> 1. **Confusing "Field of View"** — Always specify whether you mean *monocular horizontal*, *binocular total*, or *diagonal*. HMD specs almost always quote the diagonal.
> 2. **Mixing IPD units** — IPD is in **mm** (e.g., 63 mm), but vergence angle is in **degrees or radians**. Carry units throughout.
> 3. **Forgetting that the display sits at $s_o = f_e$** — Without this assumption, the virtual image is *not* at infinity, and the FOV formula changes.
> 4. **Mixing up BOOM and HMD** — A BOOM is **not** a "helmet" HCD. It is mechanically coupled but mounted on a stand. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this confusion.
> 5. **Skipping the latency decomposition** — When asked to compute motion-to-photon latency, you **must** show the addition step-by-step. Writing only the final number loses 2 of the 7 marks.
> 6. **Drawing a labelled diagram with markdown formatting inside Mermaid labels** — Mermaid will silently truncate or fail to render. Use plain uppercase text.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Head-Coupled Display (HCD)** — any display whose image is coupled (mechanically, optically, or electronically) to the user's head pose. Includes HMDs, BOOMs, smart glasses.
- **FOV Formula:** $\text{FOV}_h = 2 \arctan(d_h / 2 f_e)$. Always quote **monocular** vs **binocular**.
- **Pixels Per Degree (PPD)** = $w / \text{FOV}_h$. Retinal limit ≈ **60 PPD**; modern HMDs ≈ 20–25 PPD.
- **Vergence Angle:** $\theta_v = 2 \arctan(\text{IPD}/2D)$.
- **VAC (Vergence–Accommodation Conflict):** Inherent to all single-focal-plane HCDs. Cause of eye strain.
- **Motion-to-Photon Latency Budget:** Aim for $\le$ **20 ms**. Components: sense + fuse + render + scan + persist.
- **IPD Average:** **63 mm**; devices must allow adjustment in **50–75 mm** range.
- **Exit pupil** (8–15 mm) determines how forgiving the optics are to eye placement.
- **BOOM display** = counter-weighted arm, mechanical encoder, zero perceived latency — *not* a helmet.
- **Historical milestones:** Sutherland's *Sword of Damocles* (1968), VPL *EyePhone* (1989), NASA *VIVED*, FakeSpace *BOOM*.
- **See-through AR** uses **optical combiners** (waveguides, half-silvered mirrors) or **video pass-through** cameras.
- **KTU-recommended reading alignment:** Module 4 focuses on the *output* (HCD) side of the VR pipeline; complement with Module 3 on tracking for the *input* side.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acoustic Hardware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT634 - AUGMENTED AND VIRTUAL REALITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Acoustic Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Acoustic Hardware** in the context of Augmented and Virtual Reality (AR/VR) refers to the complete set of electroacoustic transducers, signal processing units, and physical sound-rendering devices responsible for capturing, processing, and delivering localized, three-dimensional, and immersive sound to the user. It encompasses loudspeakers, headphones, microphones, bone-conduction transducers, subwoofers, haptic-audio actuators, ambisonic microphone arrays, and the associated digital signal processors (DSPs) that operate together to reproduce a perceptually accurate auditory virtual environment.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4), acoustic hardware is the physical layer of the **Audio Rendering Pipeline** that converts digital sound objects into mechanical pressure waves in the user's ear canal, while simultaneously capturing environmental audio through microphones to feed back into the AR/VR system for real-world blending.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT634, Module 4):**
> *"Acoustic Hardware comprises the physical devices — speakers, headphones, microphones, haptic transducers, and audio processing units — used to produce, capture, and manipulate sound waves in AR/VR environments in order to deliver spatially accurate and immersive auditory experiences."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watching a movie at home on a small TV with two tiny speakers. The sound is flat — dialogue comes from the screen, music plays underneath, and that's it. Now imagine the same movie in a Dolby Atmos cinema. The sound of a helicopter actually comes from above you. A bullet whizzes past your **right ear**, and rain falls around you in 360°.

That magical difference is created by **Acoustic Hardware** working in concert with clever signal processing.

- The **cinema's many speakers** = loudspeaker arrays
- The **headphones you wear at home** that simulate 3D space = binaural headphones with HRTF
- The **mics on your VR headset** that record the real world = ambisonic microphone arrays

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Rule of Thumb:** If your eyes are the display, your ears are the speakers. Acoustic hardware is what makes your ears "see" the virtual world.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following standard metrics and constants govern all AR/VR acoustic hardware:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of sound (20°C air) | $c$ | **343 m/s** | Reference propagation velocity |
| Reference sound pressure | $P_0$ | **20 µPa** | Threshold of human hearing |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | Ambient pressure reference |
| Density of air (20°C) | $\rho$ | **1.204 kg/m³** | Medium density |
| Acoustic impedance of air | $Z_0$ | **413 Pa·s/m** | ρ × c |
| Human hearing range | $f_{audible}$ | **20 Hz – 20 kHz** | Bandwidth of human ear |
| Maximum tolerable SPL | $SPL_{max}$ | **120 dB** | Pain threshold |
| Head circumference (avg.) | $d_{head}$ | **0.17 m** | Used in HRTF calculations |
| Pinna (outer ear) effect onset | $f_{pinna}$ | **> 2 kHz** | Spatial cues begin |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The number **20 µPa** is the universal zero-point of all dB SPL measurements. It represents the quietest sound a young, healthy human ear can detect at **1 kHz**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Acoustic Hardware

Acoustic hardware for AR/VR can be classified into four functional categories:

**A. Output (Sound Reproduction) Devices**
- **Dynamic loudspeakers** (cone-type)
- **Planar magnetic headphones**
- **Electrostatic headphones**
- **Balanced armature drivers** (IEMs)
- **Bone conduction transducers**
- **Haptic bass actuators** (subwoofers / rumble packs)

**B. Input (Sound Capture) Devices**
- **Omnidirectional microphones** (pressure mics)
- **Directional microphones** (cardioid, shotgun)
- **Ambisonic microphone arrays** (e.g., TetraMic, Zoom H3-VR)
- **Microphone arrays on HMD** (e.g., Meta Quest Pro, HoloLens 2)
- **Ultrasonic / time-of-flight acoustic sensors**

**C. Processing Hardware**
- **Digital Signal Processors (DSPs)** — e.g., Qualcomm Hexagon, Cirrus Logic CS47L35
- **Audio Codec Chips** — DACs and ADCs (e.g., ESS Sabre, AKM AK4499)
- **FPGA-based real-time spatial audio engines**

**D. Coupling / Transmission Medium**
- **Wired (USB-C, 3.5 mm jack)**
- **Wireless (Bluetooth LE Audio, Wi-Fi 6E, proprietary 60 GHz)**

### 2.2 The Audio Rendering Pipeline in AR/VR

The acoustic hardware operates in a strict sequential pipeline:

1. **Audio Source Generation** → virtual sound objects (position, type, intensity)
2. **Spatial Encoding** → HRTF convolution / Ambisonics encoding / WFS
3. **DSP Processing** → reverberation, occlusion, obstruction, Doppler shift
4. **DAC Conversion** → digital samples to analog voltage
5. **Amplification** → voltage to current capable of driving transducers
6. **Transduction** → analog signal to mechanical vibration (sound pressure)
7. **Coupling to Ear** → ear canal pressure on tympanic membrane
8. **Perception** → cochlear processing → auditory cortex

### 2.3 Core Theoretical Concepts

#### 2.3.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL)

SPL is the logarithmic measure of acoustic pressure relative to the threshold of hearing:

$$SPL = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{rms}}{P_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

where:
- $P_{rms}$ = root-mean-square acoustic pressure (Pa)
- $P_0$ = 20 µPa (reference)

#### 2.3.2 Sound Intensity and the Inverse Square Law

For a point source radiating spherically:

$$I = \frac{P_{rms}^2}{\rho c} = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$

This is the **inverse square law** — every doubling of distance reduces intensity by a factor of 4 (or **6 dB**).

#### 2.3.3 Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF)

The HRTF is the frequency response that describes how a sound from a specific 3D location is filtered by the listener's **head, torso, and pinnae** before reaching the eardrum. It is the cornerstone of binaural 3D audio in AR/VR.

$$H_{HRTF}(f, \theta, \phi) = \frac{P_{ear}(f, \theta, \phi)}{P_{free}(f)}$$

where $\theta$ is azimuth, $\phi$ is elevation, and $P_{free}$ is the free-field pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The two main cues encoded in HRTF are:
> - **ITD (Interaural Time Difference)** — for $f < 1.5$ kHz
> - **ILD (Interaural Level Difference)** — for $f > 1.5$ kHz

#### 2.3.4 Ambisonics

Ambisonics is a full-sphere surround sound technique that captures/reproduces a sound field using spherical harmonics. The first-order ambisonic signal uses four channels (W, X, Y, Z — the B-format):

$$S(t) = W(t) + X(t)\cos\theta\cos\phi + Y(t)\sin\theta\cos\phi + Z(t)\sin\phi$$

#### 2.3.5 Doppler Effect in VR Audio

When a virtual source moves, the perceived frequency shifts:

$$f' = f \cdot \frac{c + v_{observer}}{c - v_{source}}$$

VR engines (Unity, Unreal) compute this in real-time using DSP.

#### 2.3.6 Audio Latency Budget

Total acoustic latency in AR/VR = Motion-to-Photon Analog + Audio Pipeline Latency. KTU expects students to know that **audio latency must be ≤ 20 ms** to be perceptually seamless with visuals.

$$L_{total} = L_{tracking} + L_{DSP} + L_{DAC} + L_{transducer}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SPL = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB |
| 2 | $I = P^2/(\rho c)$ | Acoustic intensity | W/m² |
| 3 | $I \propto 1/r^2$ | Inverse square law | — |
| 4 | $c = f \lambda$ | Wave equation (basic) | m/s |
| 5 | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T / M}$ | Speed of sound in gas | m/s |
| 6 | $Z_0 = \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance | Pa·s/m |
| 7 | $f' = f \cdot (c \pm v_o)/(c \mp v_s)$ | Doppler effect | Hz |
| 8 | $H_{HRTF} = P_{ear}/P_{free}$ | HRTF | unitless |
| 9 | $ITD \approx d_{head}\sin\theta / c$ | Interaural Time Difference | s |
| 10 | $ILD \approx 20 \log_{10}(\Delta P_{L}/\Delta P_{R})$ | Interaural Level Difference | dB |
| 11 | $B-format = W + X + Y + Z$ | 1st-order ambisonics | channels |
| 12 | $L_{audio} \leq 20$ ms | Latency budget | ms |
| 13 | $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$ | Sampling theorem | Hz |
| 14 | $f_{c} = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | Crossover frequency | Hz |
| 15 | $SNR = 20 \log_{10}(V_{signal}/V_{noise})$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Tip for KTU Exams:** "20 µPa, 20 dB/decade, 20 kHz, 20 ms" — the number 20 is the holy number of acoustics.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Medical/Healthcare AR/VR**: Spatial audio guides surgeons in laparoscopic procedures.
- **Industrial Training**: Simulated factory noise with directional hazards (e.g., crane approach warnings).
- **Defense/Military**: 3D audio cueing for fighter pilot HUDs and submarine sonar.
- **Accessibility**: Audio-only navigation for visually impaired users in smart cities.
- **Gaming & Entertainment**: Cinematic 3D audio in Beat Saber, Half-Life: Alyx, etc.
- **Architecture (AEC)**: Acoustic ray-tracing for room design using VR previews.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sound Pressure Level from Intensity

**Problem:** A VR headset's integrated speaker produces an acoustic pressure of $P_{rms} = 0.1$ Pa at the listener's ear. Calculate the SPL in dB.

**Step 1 — Identify the given values**
- $P_{rms} = 0.1$ Pa
- $P_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa

**Step 2 — Substitute into SPL formula**
$$SPL = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{rms}}{P_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio**
$$\frac{P_{rms}}{P_0} = \frac{0.1}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.1}{0.00002} = 5000$$

**Step 4 — Take the logarithm**
$$\log_{10}(5000) = \log_{10}(5 \times 10^3) = \log_{10}(5) + 3 \approx 0.6990 + 3 = 3.6990$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 20**
$$SPL = 20 \times 3.6990 = 73.98 \text{ dB} \approx 74 \text{ dB}$$

**Result:** The VR speaker produces a Sound Pressure Level of approximately **74 dB**, which is equivalent to normal conversation volume.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Always convert µPa → Pa before dividing. Forgetting the factor of $10^{-6}$ gives a wildly wrong answer of -106 dB.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Inverse Square Law Applied to VR Speaker Placement

**Problem:** A subwoofer produces an intensity of $I_1 = 0.1$ W/m² at a distance of $r_1 = 0.5$ m from the user. Find the intensity and SPL at $r_2 = 1.0$ m.

**Step 1 — Apply the inverse square law**
$$I_2 = I_1 \cdot \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the distance ratio**
$$\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.5}{1.0}\right)^2 = 0.25$$

**Step 3 — Compute new intensity**
$$I_2 = 0.1 \times 0.25 = 0.025 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Convert intensity to SPL change**
$$\Delta SPL = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(0.5) = 20 \times (-0.3010) = -6.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Compute absolute SPL at 0.5 m first**
$$SPL_1 = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_1}{I_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.1}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10^{11}) = 110 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the -6 dB drop**
$$SPL_2 = 110 - 6.02 = 103.98 \text{ dB} \approx 104 \text{ dB}$$

**Result:** Doubling the distance cuts SPL by **6 dB** and reduces intensity by a factor of 4.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: ITD Calculation for a Source at 90° Azimuth

**Problem:** Compute the Interaural Time Difference (ITD) for a sound source located exactly to the listener's right (azimuth θ = 90°). Assume average head diameter $d_{head} = 0.17$ m and speed of sound $c = 343$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Recall the geometric model**

The maximum path difference between the two ears equals the head's diameter when the source is at θ = 90°:
$$\Delta x = d_{head} = 0.17 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the time difference formula**
$$ITD = \frac{\Delta x}{c} = \frac{0.17}{343}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the value**
$$ITD = 4.956 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 495.6 \text{ \mu s} \approx 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to arbitrary azimuth**
$$ITD(\theta) = \frac{d_{head}\sin\theta}{c}$$

For θ = 45°:
$$ITD(45°) = \frac{0.17 \times \sin(45°)}{343} = \frac{0.17 \times 0.7071}{343} = 3.504 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \approx 350 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Result:** The maximum ITD is approximately **0.5 ms** (or 500 µs) for a human head, decreasing sinusoidally with azimuth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The 0.5 ms ITD value is the **largest cue** the brain uses to localize low-frequency sounds laterally.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Doppler Effect for an Approaching VR Source

**Problem:** A virtual police siren in a VR training module emits a frequency of $f = 800$ Hz and approaches the listener at $v_s = 30$ m/s. The listener is stationary. Find the observed frequency $f'$.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables**
- $f = 800$ Hz
- $c = 343$ m/s
- $v_s = 30$ m/s (source moving toward observer → use $-$ in denominator)
- $v_o = 0$ (observer stationary)

**Step 2 — Apply the Doppler equation**
$$f' = f \cdot \frac{c + v_o}{c - v_s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values**
$$f' = 800 \cdot \frac{343 + 0}{343 - 30} = 800 \cdot \frac{343}{313}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio**
$$\frac{343}{313} = 1.0958$$

**Step 5 — Final multiplication**
$$f' = 800 \times 1.0958 = 876.68 \text{ Hz} \approx 877 \text{ Hz}$$

**Result:** The approaching siren is heard at **877 Hz**, a pitch shift of **+77 Hz** (about 1.3 semitones higher).

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Nyquist Sampling Rate for VR Audio

**Problem:** An AR headset records environmental audio using a 4-mic array. To faithfully capture the full human audible range, what is the minimum sampling rate?

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum frequency**
$$f_{max} = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist Theorem**
$$f_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 20{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Standard industry rate**
$$f_s = 44.1 \text{ kHz (CD) or 48 kHz (VR standard)}$$

**Result:** The minimum sampling rate is **40 kHz**; in practice, VR systems use **48 kHz** to provide a guard band for anti-aliasing filters.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Acoustic Hardware Specifications

| Device Type | Frequency Response | Latency | Use Case in AR/VR | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Planar Magnetic Headphones | 5 Hz – 50 kHz | < 5 ms | High-end VR audio | Heavy, expensive |
| Dynamic Cone Speakers | 60 Hz – 20 kHz | < 3 ms | Built-in HMD speakers | Limited bass |
| Bone Conduction | 100 Hz – 18 kHz | 8–10 ms | Outdoor AR (open ears) | Poor bass response |
| Ambisonic Mic (1st order) | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | 5–8 ms | 360° environmental capture | Limited vertical resolution |
| Ambisonic Mic (3rd order) | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | 5–8 ms | Pro 3DOF+ capture | Requires 16+ mics |
| Haptic Bass Actuator | 5 – 80 Hz (vibration) | 10–15 ms | Vest/suit rumble | Not acoustic, mechanical |
| MEMS Microphone (HMD) | 50 Hz – 20 kHz | < 3 ms | Voice input, see-through audio | Omnidirectional only |
